0.5 \, r_{xx}(1) + 0.3 \, r_{xx}(2) &= 1.0 \quad \cdots (i)\\ r_{xx}(1) - 0.5 \, r_{xx}(0) + 0.3 \, r_{xx}(1) &= 0 \quad \cdots (ii)\\ r_{xx}(2) - 0.5 \, r_{xx}(1) + 0.3 \, r_{xx}(0) &= 0 \quad \cdots (iii) \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Solve Equation (ii) for $r_{xx}(1)$** $$ 1.3 \, r_{xx}(1) = 0.5 \, r_{xx}(0) \;\Rightarrow\; r_{xx}(1) = \frac{0.5}{1.3} \, r_{xx}(0) = \frac{5}{13} r_{xx}(0) $$ **Step 3 — Solve Equations (i) and (iii) for $r_{xx}(2)$ and $r_{xx}(0)$** From equation (iii): $$ r_{xx}(2) = 0.5 \, r_{xx}(1) - 0.3 \, r_{xx}(0) = 0.5 \cdot \frac{5}{13} r_{xx}(0) - 0.3 \, r_{xx}(0) = \left(\frac{2.5}{13} - 0.3\right) r_{xx}(0) $$ $$ r_{xx}(2) = \left(\frac{2.5 - 3.9}{13}\right) r_{xx}(0) = \frac{-1.4}{13} r_{xx}(0) $$ Substituting into equation (i): $$ r_{xx}(0) - 0.5 \cdot \frac{5}{13} r_{xx}(0) + 0.3 \cdot \frac{-1.4}{13} r_{xx}(0) = 1.0 $$ $$ r_{xx}(0) \left[ 1 - \frac{2.5}{13} - \frac{0.42}{13} \right] = 1.0 $$ $$ r_{xx}(0) \left[ \frac{13 - 2.5 - 0.42}{13} \right] = 1.0 $$ $$ r_{xx}(0) \cdot \frac{10.08}{13} = 1.0 \;\Rightarrow\; r_{xx}(0) = \frac{13}{10.08} \approx 1.2897 $$ **Step 4 — Compute the Final Autocorrelation Values** $$ r_{xx}(1) = \frac{5}{13} \cdot 1.2897 \approx 0.4960 $$ $$ r_{xx}(2) = \frac{-1.4}{13} \cdot 1.2897 \approx -0.1389 $$ **Step 5 — Compute the Rational PSD** $$ P_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{1.0}{\vert 1 - 0.5 e^{-j\omega} + 0.3 e^{-j2\omega} \vert^2} $$ **Verification of Stability**: The poles are roots of $z^2 - 0.5 z + 0.3 = 0$: $$ z = \frac{0.5 \pm \sqrt{0.25 - 1.2}}{2} = \frac{0.5 \pm j\sqrt{0.95}}{2} $$ $$ \vert z \vert = \sqrt{(0.25)^2 + 0.95/4} = \sqrt{0.0625 + 0.2375} = \sqrt{0.3} \approx 0.5477 < 1 \quad \checkmark $$ The system is **stable**. ## 3.4 Analytical Insight: Why AR Models Are Most Popular - **Yule-Walker gives linear equations** in the AR coefficients — a closed-form solution exists. - **Levinson-Durbin recursion** solves them in $O(p^2)$ time. - **All-pole models approximate spectra with sharp peaks** (formants, resonances) very well. - **MA and ARMA** estimation is nonlinear and computationally expensive. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Rational Power Spectrum Model ```mermaid flowchart LR WN[White Noise w of n variance sigma w squared] -->|excitation| SYS[LTI Filter H of z equals B of z over A of z] SYS -->|filtered output| XN[Random Process x of n] XN -->|compute autocorrelation| RX[Autocorrelation rxx of m] RX -->|inverse z transform| PSD[Power Spectrum Pxx of omega] PSD -->|evaluated on unit circle| OUT[Rational PSD formula] subgraph ANALYSIS [Spectrum Estimation Block] RX -->|Yule Walker equations| YW[AR Coefficient Estimator a1 to ap] YW -->|estimated parameters| PAR[Estimated Parameters] PAR -->|plug into formula| ESTPSD[Estimated PSD] end SYS -.->|true parameters| TRUE[True a k and b k] TRUE -.->|reference| ESTPSD ``` ## 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart — Three Rational Model Families ```mermaid flowchart TB INPUT[Stationary Random Process x of n] --> CHOOSE{Choose Model Type} CHOOSE -->|All Pole| AR[AR of p Model
H of z equals 1 over A of z] CHOOSE -->|All Zero| MA[MA of q Model
H of z equals B of z] CHOOSE -->|Pole Zero| ARMA[ARMA of p q Model
H of z equals B of z over A of z] AR -->|Linear YW| ARS[AR Spectrum
sigma w squared over A of e to j omega squared] MA -->|Nonlinear| MAS[MA Spectrum
sigma w squared times B of e to j omega squared] ARMA -->|Nonlinear| ARMAS[ARMA Spectrum
sigma w squared times B squared over A squared] ARS --> RECON[Reconstructed PSD] MAS --> RECON ARMAS --> RECON style AR fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style MA fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style ARMA fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.3 Mermaid Sequence — Yule-Walker Solution Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Observed Data x of n] --> B[Estimate Autocorrelation r hat of m] B --> C[Form Toeplitz Matrix R sub p] C --> D[Form Right Hand Side r sub p vector] D --> E[Apply Levinson Durbin Recursion] E --> F[Obtain AR Coefficients a1 to ap] F --> G[Compute sigma w squared] G --> H[Form A of e to j omega] H --> I[Compute PSD = sigma w squared over A squared] I --> J[Plot Estimated Spectrum] style E fill:#fad7a0,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px style J fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 4.4 Interpretation of the Block Diagrams - **Diagram 4.1** shows the *forward* generative model: white noise $\to$ LTI filter $\to$ colored process. The estimator works in the **reverse direction**, recovering the filter from observations. - **Diagram 4.2** classifies rational models by pole-zero structure, highlighting that **AR is the simplest computationally** while ARMA is the most general. - **Diagram 4.3** traces the **end-to-end estimation pipeline** — the standard sequence tested in KTU board examinations. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Define Rational Power Spectrum. Mention the three parametric models used to represent it.** **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: A **Rational Power Spectrum** $P_{xx}(\omega)$ is a Power Spectral Density that can be expressed as the ratio of two finite-order polynomials in $e^{-j\omega}$. For an LTI system with transfer function $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$ driven by white noise of variance $\sigma_w^2$: $$ P_{xx}(\omega) = \sigma_w^2 \frac{\vert B(e^{j\omega})\vert^2}{\vert A(e^{j\omega})\vert^2} $$ The three parametric models are: 1. **AR(p)** — Autoregressive: all-pole model, $B(z) = 1$. 2. **MA(q)** — Moving Average: all-zero model, $A(z) = 1$. 3. **ARMA(p,q)** — Autoregressive Moving Average: combined pole-zero model. *(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for naming the three models.)* ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]` **State the Yule-Walker equations for an AR(p) process. Why are they called a system of linear equations?** **Model Answer (3 Marks)**: The Yule-Walker equations for an AR(p) process relate the autocorrelations $r_{xx}(m)$ to the AR coefficients $a_k$: $$ r_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k) + \sigma_w^2 \delta(m), \quad m = 1, 2, \ldots, p $$ For $m \geq 1$, this simplifies to: $$ r_{xx}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k) = 0 $$ In matrix form, $\mathbf{R}_p \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}_p$, where $\mathbf{R}_p$ is the Toeplitz autocorrelation matrix. These are called **linear equations** because the unknown AR coefficients $a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_p$ appear only to the **first power** (no products, squares, or transcendental functions of the unknowns). Hence, a closed-form solution exists via $\mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{R}_p^{-1} \mathbf{r}_p$. *(Award 1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for matrix form, 1 mark for the linearity reasoning.)* --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Students often confuse the sign convention in Yule-Walker equations. The recurrence $r_{xx}(m) = -\sum a_k r_{xx}(m-k)$ uses a **negative sign** because the AR model is written as $x(n) = -\sum a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$. Forgetting this sign costs 2 marks instantly. Also, do not forget the $\sigma_w^2 \delta(m)$ term — it is the *defining* term that makes the equation complete. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **(a)** Derive the Yule-Walker equations for an AR(p) process. Express the final result in matrix form. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An AR(2) process has coefficients $a_1 = -1.0$, $a_2 = 0.5$ and driving noise variance $\sigma_w^2 = 2.0$. Compute $r_{xx}(0)$, $r_{xx}(1)$, and $r_{xx}(2)$ using the Yule-Walker equations. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution for Part (a) (7 Marks)**: **Step 1 — AR(p) Model Equation** (1 mark): $$ x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) = w(n) $$ **Step 2 — Multiply by $x(n-m)$ and take expectation** (2 marks): Multiplying both sides by $x(n-m)$ and taking the ensemble average $E\{\cdot\}$: $$ E\{x(n) x(n-m)\} + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k E\{x(n-k) x(n-m)\} = E\{w(n) x(n-m)\} $$ This yields: $$ r_{xx}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k) = E\{w(n) x(n-m)\} $$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the cross-correlation term** (2 marks): Since $w(n)$ is white noise and the system is causal: - For $m > 0$: $w(n)$ is independent of past outputs $\Rightarrow E\{w(n) x(n-m)\} = 0$. - For $m = 0$: $E\{w(n) x(n)\} = E\{w^2(n)\} = \sigma_w^2$. Hence: $$ r_{xx}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k) = \sigma_w^2 \delta(m), \quad m = 0, 1, \ldots, p $$ **Step 4 — Matrix Formulation** (2 marks): For $m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$, the equations form a linear system: $$ \begin{bmatrix} r_{xx}(0) & r_{xx}(1) & \cdots & r_{xx}(p-1) \\ r_{xx}(1) & r_{xx}(0) & \cdots & r_{xx}(p-2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ r_{xx}(p-1) & r_{xx}(p-2) & \cdots & r_{xx}(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix} = -\begin{bmatrix} r_{xx}(1) \\ r_{xx}(2) \\ \vdots \\ r_{xx}(p) \end{bmatrix} $$ i.e., $\mathbf{R}_p \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}_p$, with the variance computed as $\sigma_w^2 = r_{xx}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(k)$. #### **Model Solution for Part (b) (7 Marks)**: **Step 1 — Write the AR(2) Yule-Walker equations** (2 marks): $$ \begin{aligned} r_{xx}(1) + a_1 r_{xx}(0) + a_2 r_{xx}(1) &= 0 \quad \cdots (i) \\ r_{xx}(2) + a_1 r_{xx}(1) + a_2 r_{xx}(0) &= 0 \quad \cdots (ii) \\ r_{xx}(0) + a_1 r_{xx}(1) + a_2 r_{xx}(2) &= \sigma_w^2 = 2.0 \quad \cdots (iii) \end{aligned} $$ Substituting $a_1 = -1.0$ and $a_2 = 0.5$: $$ \begin{aligned} r_{xx}(1) - r_{xx}(0) + 0.5 r_{xx}(1) &= 0 \\ r_{xx}(2) - r_{xx}(1) + 0.5 r_{xx}(0) &= 0 \\ r_{xx}(0) - r_{xx}(1) + 0.5 r_{xx}(2) &= 2.0 \end{aligned} $$ **Step 2 — Solve equation (i) for $r_{xx}(1)$ in terms of $r_{xx}(0)$** (2 marks): $$ 1.5 \, r_{xx}(1) = r_{xx}(0) \;\Rightarrow\; r_{xx}(1) = \frac{2}{3} r_{xx}(0) $$ **Step 3 — Solve equation (ii) for $r_{xx}(2)$ in terms of $r_{xx}(0)$** (1 mark): $$ r_{xx}(2) = r_{xx}(1) - 0.5 r_{xx}(0) = \frac{2}{3} r_{xx}(0) - 0.5 r_{xx}(0) = \frac{1}{6} r_{xx}(0) $$ **Step 4 — Solve equation (iii) for $r_{xx}(0)$** (1 mark): $$ r_{xx}(0) - \frac{2}{3} r_{xx}(0) + 0.5 \cdot \frac{1}{6} r_{xx}(0) = 2.0 $$ $$ r_{xx}(0) \left[ 1 - \frac{2}{3} + \frac{1}{12} \right] = 2.0 $$ $$ r_{xx}(0) \left[ \frac{12 - 8 + 1}{12} \right] = 2.0 \;\Rightarrow\; r_{xx}(0) \cdot \frac{5}{12} = 2.0 $$ $$ \boxed{r_{xx}(0) = \frac{24}{5} = 4.8} $$ **Step 5 — Final values** (1 mark): $$ \boxed{r_{xx}(1) = \frac{2}{3} \cdot 4.8 = 3.2} $$ $$ \boxed{r_{xx}(2) = \frac{1}{6} \cdot 4.8 = 0.8} $$ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]` **(a)** Explain the rational power spectrum representation. Derive the expression for the PSD of an ARMA(p, q) process. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Compare the AR, MA, and ARMA models in terms of (i) number of parameters, (ii) computational complexity of coefficient estimation, (iii) spectrum shape capability, and (iv) typical application areas. **(7 Marks)** #### **Model Solution for Part (a) (7 Marks)**: **Step 1 — Definition of Rational PSD** (2 marks): A rational power spectrum is one that can be expressed as a ratio of polynomials. For a WSS process $x(n)$ generated by passing white noise $w(n)$ (variance $\sigma_w^2$) through a causal LTI filter $H(z) = B(z)/A(z)$: $$ P_{xx}(z) = \sigma_w^2 \frac{B(z) B(z^{-1})}{A(z) A(z^{-1})} $$ On the unit circle: $$ P_{xx}(\omega) = \sigma_w^2 \frac{\vert B(e^{j\omega})\vert^2}{\vert A(e^{j\omega})\vert^2} $$ **Step 2 — ARMA(p,q) Difference Equation** (1 mark): $$ x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) = w(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{q} b_k w(n-k) $$ **Step 3 — Derive the Transfer Function** (1 mark): Taking the $z$-transform: $$ H(z) = \frac{B(z)}{A(z)} = \frac{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} b_k z^{-k}}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}} $$ **Step 4 — Derive the PSD** (2 marks): Multiplying by $\sigma_w^2$ and using $P_{xx}(z) = \sigma_w^2 H(z) H(z^{-1})$: $$ \boxed{P_{xx}(\omega) = \sigma_w^2 \, \frac{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} b_k e^{-jk\omega} \right\vert^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-jk\omega} \right\vert^2}} $$ **Step 5 — Special Cases** (1 mark): - AR(p) when $B(z) = 1$ (no MA part). - MA(q) when $A(z) = 1$ (no AR part). #### **Model Solution for Part (b) (7 Marks)**: | Criterion | AR(p) | MA(q) | ARMA(p,q) | |:----------|:------|:------|:----------| | **(i) Parameters** | $p+1$ ($a_1, \ldots, a_p, \sigma_w^2$) | $q+1$ ($b_1, \ldots, b_q, \sigma_w^2$) | $p+q+1$ (all of the above) | | **(ii) Estimation** | **Linear** (Yule-Walker) — $O(p^2)$ via Levinson-Durbin | **Nonlinear** (requires iterative methods) | **Nonlinear** (least squares, MLE) | | **(iii) Spectrum Shape** | **Sharp peaks** (poles), no zeros | **Sharp valleys** (zeros), no peaks | **Both peaks and valleys** (poles and zeros) | | **(iv) Applications** | Speech formants, EEG, vibration spectra | Smoothing, noise modeling, financial returns | Speech synthesis, radar clutter, control systems | **Justifications (Award 1 mark for each comparison with a sentence)**: 1. **AR(p)** uses the fewest parameters and has *linear* estimation. It excels at modeling spectra with strong resonances (peaks). 2. **MA(q)** can carve deep nulls in the spectrum. Estimation is nonlinear because the output $x(n)$ is a *nonlinear* function of the MA coefficients. 3. **ARMA(p,q)** achieves a given spectral shape with *fewer total parameters* than pure AR or MA, but at the cost of nonlinear estimation complexity. The Wold decomposition guarantees that any ARMA process has a unique infinite-order AR representation. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Board Exams — Print This Section!** - **Definition**: Rational PSD = $\sigma_w^2 \vert B(e^{j\omega})\vert^2 / \vert A(e^{j\omega})\vert^2$ — *ratio of two polynomials*. - **Three Models**: AR(p) (all-pole), MA(q) (all-zero), ARMA(p,q) (pole-zero). - **AR(p) Equation**: $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$. - **Yule-Walker (AR)**: $r_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k) + \sigma_w^2 \delta(m)$. - **Sign Convention**: The **negative sign** in the Yule-Walker recursion is non-negotiable — it comes from the difference equation. - **Matrix Form**: $\mathbf{R}_p \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}_p$, with $\sigma_w^2 = r_{xx}(0) + \sum a_k r_{xx}(k)$. - **Stability**: All poles of $A(z)$ must satisfy $\vert z_i \vert < 1$. - **Toeplitz Structure**: $\mathbf{R}_p$ is Toeplitz — solved efficiently via **Levinson-Durbin** recursion in $O(p^2)$. - **Order Selection**: Use **AIC** ($N \ln \sigma_w^2 + 2p$) or **MDL** ($N \ln \sigma_w^2 + p \ln N$). - **Wold's Theorem**: *Any* WSS process can be represented as a (possibly infinite-order) AR model — this is why AR is the workhorse. - **Periodogram vs Parametric**: Rational (parametric) methods give **better resolution** for short data records. - **Exam Trap**: When asked for "rational PSD," always state the formula in *both* $z$-domain and unit-circle form. - **Units**: PSD has units of power per radian per sample (or per Hz if $f$ is used). - **Key Names to Remember**: Yule (1927), Walker, Levinson (1947), Durbin (1960), Burg, Akaike, Rissanen (MDL). - **Real-World Anchor**: Speech → AR; EEG → AR; Radar → ARMA; Financial → ARMA/ARIMA.

topics.title: Relationships between the filter parameters and the autocorrelation sequence
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT746 - ADVANCED DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Relationships between the filter parameters and the autocorrelation sequence

# 🔗 Relationships Between Filter Parameters and the Autocorrelation Sequence

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

In **linear prediction (LP)** of a stationary discrete-time stochastic process $x(n)$, the predictor of order $p$ is an FIR filter whose coefficients $\{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_p\}$ are called the **Linear Prediction (LP) coefficients** or **filter parameters** (also known as **tapered transversal filter coefficients** in the Haykin textbook context). These parameters are not free — they are deterministically linked to the **autocorrelation sequence** $r(l)$ of the input process by a beautiful and fundamental set of identities known collectively as the **Yule–Walker equations**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> The *filter parameters* in the LP analysis refer to the set of coefficients that completely describe the optimum forward linear predictor of order $p$. These are connected to the autocorrelation sequence $r(l) = E[x(n)\,x(n-l)]$ through the principle of **orthogonality**, yielding the **normal equations** of linear prediction.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — The "Memory of a Signal"

Imagine you are listening to someone speak, and you try to **predict the next word** purely from the previous $p$ words they spoke. Each word's predictive value is different — some past words carry more predictive weight than others. The *autocorrelation sequence* $r(l)$ is a numerical measure of how strongly a sample of the signal is "remembered" $l$ time-steps later. The LP coefficients $\{a_k\}$ are the **optimal weights** you should place on those past samples to make the best linear forecast. The Yule–Walker equations are the **rule book** that translates raw memory (autocorrelation) into optimal prediction weights (filter parameters).

> [!NOTE]
> - The autocorrelation sequence $r(l)$ is an **even function**: $r(-l) = r(l)$ for real, wide-sense stationary processes.
> - The filter is called *tapered* because the coefficients are smoothly scaled — typically larger near $k=1$ and decaying toward $k=p$ for many practical signals (e.g., speech).
> - The output of the predictor is the *prediction* $\hat{x}(n)$; the difference $e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$ is the **prediction error** (residual).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Linear Predictor Model

The forward linear predictor of order $p$ estimates the present sample as a weighted sum of the $p$ most recent past samples:

$$\hat{x}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x(n-k)$$

The **prediction error** is:

$$e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x(n-k)$$

The coefficients $\{a_k\}$ are chosen to **minimize the mean-square prediction error**:

$$J = E[\,|e(n)|^2\,] = E\!\left[\,\left\vert\,x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\,x(n-k)\right\vert^{\!2}\right]$$

### 2.2 The Orthogonality Principle — The Heart of the Relationship

Setting $\frac{\partial J}{\partial a_k} = 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, p$ gives the **orthogonality condition**:

$$E[\,e(n)\,x^*(n-k)\,] = 0, \qquad k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

Substituting the expression for $e(n)$ and using $E[x(n)x^*(n-l)] = r(l)$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_j \, r(k - j) = r(k), \qquad k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

These $p$ equations — together with the equation for the minimum prediction-error power — are the celebrated **Yule–Walker equations** (also called the **normal equations** of linear prediction).

### 2.3 Why This Relationship Matters

- It converts a **statistical description** of the signal (autocorrelation) into a **deterministic filter design** (LP coefficients).
- It guarantees that the predictor is **physically realizable** (causal, stable, minimum-phase when the autocorrelation is positive definite).
- It is the theoretical foundation of the **Levinson–Durbin algorithm** and the **lattice filter** structure.
- It underpins applications in **speech coding (LPC vocoders), spectral estimation, channel equalization, and system identification**.

### 📋 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Relationship | Formula |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Autocorrelation definition** | $r(l) = E[x(n)\,x(n-l)]$ |
| 2 | **Forward prediction-error filter (PEF) output** | $e(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\,x(n-k)$ |
| 3 | **Yule–Walker normal equations** | $\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_j\,r(k-j) = r(k), \quad k=1,\dots,p$ |
| 4 | **Matrix form (Toeplitz system)** | $\mathbf{R}_p\,\mathbf{a}_p = \mathbf{r}_p$ |
| 5 | **Minimum prediction-error power** | $P_p = r(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\,r(k)$ |
| 6 | **Reflection (PARCOR) coefficient via Schur rec.** | $k_m = \frac{r(m) - \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} a^{(m-1)}_i\,r(m-i)}{P_{m-1}}$ |
| 7 | **Levinson–Durbin recursion (coefficient update)** | $a^{(m)}_m = k_m,\quad a^{(m)}_i = a^{(m-1)}_i - k_m\,a^{(m-1)}_{m-i}$ |
| 8 | **Order-recursive error power** | $P_m = (1 - \vert k_m \vert^2)\,P_{m-1}$ |
| 9 | **Backward prediction error** | $b(n) = x(n-m) - \sum_{i=1}^{m} a^{(m)}_i\,x(n-m+i)$ |
| 10 | **PARCOR relation** | $f_m(n) = b_{m-1}(n-1) - k_m\,b_{m-1}(n)$ |
| 11 | **Reflection-coefficient bound** | $\vert k_m \vert < 1 \;\;\text{for stability}$ |
| 12 | **Augmented autocorrelation matrix** | $\mathbf{R}_{m+1} = \begin{bmatrix} r(0) & \mathbf{r}_m^H \\ \mathbf{r}_m & \mathbf{R}_m \end{bmatrix}$ |

> [!NOTE]
> - In the matrix form, $\mathbf{R}_p$ is the **$p \times p$ Hermitian Toeplitz autocorrelation matrix**, $\mathbf{a}_p = [a_1, a_2, \dots, a_p]^T$, and $\mathbf{r}_p = [r(1), r(2), \dots, r(p)]^T$.
> - $H$ denotes **Hermitian (conjugate) transpose**; for real signals this is just $T$ (transpose).
> - The notation $a^{(m)}_i$ means the $i$-th coefficient of the $m$-th order predictor.
> - $P_p$ is the variance of the prediction error, equivalent to the **white-noise variance** exciting the all-pole model.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Speech coding** | LPC-10 vocoder — 10 filter parameters encode speech frames |
| **Spectral estimation** | All-pole (autoregressive) modeling of power spectra |
| **Channel equalization** | Adaptive prediction of intersymbol interference |
| **Biomedical signal processing** | ECG/EEG modeling for compression and anomaly detection |
| **Audio & acoustic modeling** | Formant tracking, audio coding (e.g., CELP, MELP) |
| **Radar/sonar** | AR modeling of clutter and reverberation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Yule–Walker Equations (from the Orthogonality Principle)

**Step 1 — Define the mean-square prediction error.**

The forward linear predictor of order $p$ produces an estimate:

$$\hat{x}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x(n-k)$$

So the prediction error is:

$$e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n) = x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x(n-k)$$

The mean-square value of the error is:

$$J_p = E[\,e(n)\,e^*(n)\,] = E\!\left[\,\left(x(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\,x(n-k)\right)\!\left(x^*(n) - \sum_{j=1}^{p} a_j^*\,x^*(n-j)\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Expand the quadratic form.**

Distribute the two factors (assuming real signals for clarity; the result extends naturally to complex case with conjugation):

$$J_p = E\!\left[\,x(n)\,x^*(n)\,\right] - \sum_{j=1}^{p} a_j\,E\!\left[\,x(n)\,x^*(n-j)\,\right] - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\,E\!\left[\,x(n-k)\,x^*(n)\,\right] + \sum_{k=1}^{p}\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_k\,a_j\,E\!\left[\,x(n-k)\,x^*(n-j)\,\right]$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the autocorrelation function.**

Using $r(l) = E[x(n)\,x^*(n-l)]$ and recognizing the even symmetry $r(-l) = r^*(l)$:

$$J_p = r(0) - \sum_{j=1}^{p} a_j\,r(j) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\,r^*(k) + \sum_{k=1}^{p}\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_k\,a_j\,r(j-k)$$

**Step 4 — Minimize $J_p$ with respect to each $a_k$.**

Take the partial derivative $\frac{\partial J_p}{\partial a_k} = 0$ for $k = 1, 2, \dots, p$:

$$\frac{\partial J_p}{\partial a_k} = -r^*(k) - \sum_{j=1}^{p} a_j\,r^*(j-k) = 0$$

Multiplying by $-1$ and using $r^*(-l) = r(l)$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_j\,r(k-j) = r(k), \qquad k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

These are the **Yule–Walker normal equations** — $p$ simultaneous linear equations in the $p$ unknowns $\{a_1, \dots, a_p\}$.

**Step 5 — Derive the minimum error power.**

Plug the optimal $a_k$ back into the expression for $J_p$:

$$P_p = J_p^{\text{min}} = r(0) - \sum_{j=1}^{p} a_j\,r(j)$$

This $P_p$ is the **minimum mean-square prediction error**, equal to the white-noise variance $\sigma^2$ in the equivalent all-pole model of the signal.

### 3.2 The Levinson–Durbin Recursion — A Solvable Form of the Yule–Walker System

The Yule–Walker system has a **Toeplitz** structure, allowing a clever **order-recursive** solution. Assume we know the solution of order $(m-1)$: coefficients $\{a^{(m-1)}_1, \dots, a^{(m-1)}_{m-1}\}$ and error power $P_{m-1}$. The solution of order $m$ is:

$$a^{(m)}_m = k_m$$

$$a^{(m)}_i = a^{(m-1)}_i - k_m\,a^{(m-1)}_{m-i}, \qquad i = 1, 2, \dots, m-1$$

where the **reflection coefficient** is:

$$k_m = \frac{r(m) - \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} a^{(m-1)}_i\,r(m-i)}{P_{m-1}}$$

and the error-power update is:

$$P_m = (1 - \vert k_m \vert^2)\,P_{m-1}$$

with the initial condition $P_0 = r(0)$.

### 3.3 Connection to the Lattice Filter

Each reflection coefficient $k_m$ parameterizes a single **lattice stage**. The forward and backward prediction errors evolve as:

$$f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) - k_m\,b_{m-1}(n-1)$$

$$b_m(n) = b_{m-1}(n-1) - k_m^*\,f_{m-1}(n)$$

with initial conditions $f_0(n) = b_0(n) = x(n)$. This is the bridge between the **autocorrelation sequence** (input) and the **filter parameters** $\{k_1, k_2, \dots, k_p\}$ (output of the recursion).

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example

Suppose $p = 2$ and we are given the autocorrelation sequence $r(0) = 4$, $r(1) = 2$, $r(2) = 1$ (this is consistent with a process having a positive-definite Toeplitz matrix).

**Order 1:**

$$a^{(1)}_1 = k_1 = \frac{r(1)}{P_0} = \frac{r(1)}{r(0)} = \frac{2}{4} = 0.5$$

$$P_1 = (1 - \vert 0.5 \vert^2)\,P_0 = (1 - 0.25)(4) = 3.0$$

**Order 2:**

$$k_2 = \frac{r(2) - a^{(1)}_1\,r(1)}{P_1} = \frac{1 - (0.5)(2)}{3} = \frac{0}{3} = 0$$

$$a^{(2)}_2 = k_2 = 0$$

$$a^{(2)}_1 = a^{(1)}_1 - k_2\,a^{(1)}_1 = 0.5 - (0)(0.5) = 0.5$$

$$P_2 = (1 - \vert 0 \vert^2)(3.0) = 3.0$$

**Final result:** $a_1 = 0.5$, $a_2 = 0$, with $P_2 = 3.0$. The reflection coefficient $k_2 = 0$ indicates the second-order predictor collapses to first-order — the signal is well-modeled by a single lag.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **autocorrelation sequence uniquely determines the LP filter parameters** (for minimum-phase solutions) and vice versa. This one-to-one correspondence is the heart of the topic.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Information Flow from Autocorrelation to Filter Parameters

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Input Stochastic Process x of n] --> B[Compute Autocorrelation Sequence r of l]
    B --> C[Form Toeplitz Autocorrelation Matrix R sub p]
    C --> D[Yule-Walker Normal Equations]
    D --> E{Solution Method}
    E --> F[Levinson-Durbin Algorithm]
    E --> G[Direct Matrix Inversion]
    F --> H[LP Filter Coefficients a sub 1 to a sub p]
    F --> I[Reflection Coefficients k sub 1 to k sub p]
    F --> J[Prediction Error Power P sub p]
    G --> H
    H --> K[Optimum Forward Linear Predictor]
    I --> L[Lattice Filter Realization]
    H --> M[Transversal FIR Realization]
    L --> N[Output Prediction Error e of n]
    M --> N
    J --> O[Spectral Estimate AR sub p of omega]
    N --> P[Residual White Noise Sequence]
```

### 4.2 Lattice Filter Stage — Structural Detail

```mermaid
graph LR
    IN1[f sub m minus 1 of n] --> M1[Summation Node]
    IN2[b sub m minus 1 of n minus 1] --> K1[Multiply by k sub m]
    K1 --> M1
    M1 --> OUT1[f sub m of n]
    IN2 --> M2[Summation Node]
    IN1 --> K2[Multiply by k sub m star]
    K2 --> M2
    M2 --> OUT2[b sub m of n]
    IN3[b sub m minus 1 of n minus 1] --> M2
```

### 4.3 Algorithmic Pipeline — Order Recursion of the Levinson–Durbin Algorithm

```mermaid
graph TD
    S0[Initialize: P sub 0 equals r of 0] --> S1[For m equals 1 to p]
    S1 --> S2[Compute Reflection Coeff k sub m]
    S2 --> S3[Test Stability: mod k sub m less than 1]
    S3 -->|Yes| S4[Update LP Coeffs: a sub i super m equals a sub i super m minus 1 minus k sub m times a sub m minus i super m minus 1]
    S3 -->|No| SX[STOP: Predictor Unstable]
    S4 --> S5[Update Error Power: P sub m equals 1 minus mod k sub m squared times P sub m minus 1]
    S5 --> S6{m equals p}
    S6 -->|No| S1
    S6 -->|Yes| SF[Output All Filter Parameters and P sub p]
```

### 4.4 Relationship Map — Autocorrelation ↔ Filter Parameters

```mermaid
graph TD
    AUT[Autocorrelation Sequence r of l] --> NORM[Yule-Walker Normal Equations]
    NORM -->|Solve via Levinson-Durbin| REFL[Reflection Coefficients k sub m]
    NORM -->|Solve via Direct Inversion| COEF[LP Coefficients a sub k]
    REFL -->|Inverse Levinson-Durbin| COEF
    COEF --> LAT[Lattice Filter Realization]
    REFL --> LAT
    LAT --> OUT[Output: Forward and Backward Prediction Errors]
    COEF --> TRANS[Transversal FIR Realization]
    TRANS --> ERR[Prediction Error e of n]
    OUT --> ERR
    ERR --> POW[Error Power P sub p]
    POW --> SPEC[AR Spectral Estimate]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The **Levinson–Durbin algorithm** provides the **bijective (one-to-one) mapping** between the autocorrelation sequence $\{r(0), r(1), \dots, r(p)\}$ and the parameter set $\{k_1, k_2, \dots, k_p\}$, with $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ guaranteeing a **stable, minimum-phase** prediction-error filter.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 📝 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*State and explain the Yule–Walker equations relating the autocorrelation sequence to the linear prediction filter coefficients. Why are these equations fundamental to LP analysis?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Yule–Walker equations express the **orthogonality condition** between the prediction error $e(n)$ and each past sample $x(n-k)$, for $k = 1, 2, \dots, p$:

$$\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_j\,r(k-j) = r(k), \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

Equivalently, in matrix form, $\mathbf{R}_p\,\mathbf{a}_p = \mathbf{r}_p$, where $\mathbf{R}_p$ is the **Toeplitz autocorrelation matrix**.

**Significance (1 mark):** These equations provide the bridge from the second-order statistics (autocorrelation) of the input process to the *unique* optimal LP filter coefficients that minimize the mean-square prediction error $P_p = r(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\,r(k)$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Define the reflection coefficient $k_m$ in the context of linear prediction. State the condition on $k_m$ that ensures the prediction-error filter is stable.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **reflection coefficient** (or **PARCOR coefficient**) $k_m$ is the parameter of the $m$-th stage of the lattice filter. It can be computed via the Levinson–Durbin recursion as:

$$k_m = \frac{r(m) - \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} a^{(m-1)}_i\,r(m-i)}{P_{m-1}}$$

It is also the **negative** of the autocorrelation between the forward and backward prediction errors at stage $m-1$, normalized by their common power.

**Stability condition (1 mark):** For the prediction-error filter to be **stable** (and hence minimum-phase), each reflection coefficient must satisfy $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ for all $m = 1, 2, \dots, p$.

---

### 📚 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam — May 2024]*

**(a)** Derive the Yule–Walker equations for a $p$-th order forward linear predictor from the orthogonality principle. Show that the resulting coefficient matrix is Toeplitz. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stationary random process has autocorrelation values $r(0) = 5$, $r(1) = 4$, $r(2) = 3$, $r(3) = 2$. Using the Levinson–Durbin algorithm, compute the reflection coefficients $k_1$, $k_2$, $k_3$, the LP coefficients, and the prediction-error powers $P_1$, $P_2$, $P_3$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

- Start with predictor $\hat{x}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\,x(n-k)$. **(0.5 mark)**
- Define error $e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$ and mean-square error $J = E[\,|e(n)|^2\,]$. **(0.5 mark)**
- Expand $J$ into a quadratic form in $a_k$ using the autocorrelation $r(l)$. **(2 marks)**
- Set $\partial J / \partial a_k = 0$ for $k = 1, \dots, p$ to get the orthogonality condition $E[\,e(n)\,x^*(n-k)\,] = 0$. **(1.5 marks)**
- Substitute $e(n)$ to obtain $\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_j\,r(k-j) = r(k)$. **(1 mark)**
- Observe that $r(k-j)$ depends only on the lag $(k-j)$, so the matrix has identical entries along each diagonal → **Toeplitz**. **(1.5 marks)**

**(b) Numerical computation using Levinson–Durbin (7 marks):**

**Step 0 — Initialize.**

$$P_0 = r(0) = 5$$

**Step 1 — Order 1.**

$$k_1 = \frac{r(1)}{P_0} = \frac{4}{5} = 0.8$$

$$a^{(1)}_1 = k_1 = 0.8$$

$$P_1 = (1 - k_1^2)\,P_0 = (1 - 0.64)(5) = (0.36)(5) = 1.8$$

**Step 2 — Order 2.**

$$k_2 = \frac{r(2) - a^{(1)}_1\,r(1)}{P_1} = \frac{3 - (0.8)(4)}{1.8} = \frac{3 - 3.2}{1.8} = \frac{-0.2}{1.8} = -0.1111$$

$$a^{(2)}_2 = k_2 = -0.1111$$

$$a^{(2)}_1 = a^{(1)}_1 - k_2\,a^{(1)}_1 = 0.8 - (-0.1111)(0.8) = 0.8 + 0.0889 = 0.8889$$

$$P_2 = (1 - k_2^2)\,P_1 = (1 - 0.01235)(1.8) = (0.98765)(1.8) \approx 1.7778$$

**Step 3 — Order 3.**

$$k_3 = \frac{r(3) - a^{(2)}_1\,r(2) - a^{(2)}_2\,r(1)}{P_2} = \frac{2 - (0.8889)(3) - (-0.1111)(4)}{1.7778}$$

$$= \frac{2 - 2.6667 + 0.4444}{1.7778} = \frac{-0.2222}{1.7778} = -0.125$$

$$a^{(3)}_3 = k_3 = -0.125$$

$$a^{(3)}_1 = a^{(2)}_1 - k_3\,a^{(2)}_2 = 0.8889 - (-0.125)(-0.1111) = 0.8889 - 0.01389 = 0.8750$$

$$a^{(3)}_2 = a^{(2)}_2 - k_3\,a^{(2)}_1 = -0.1111 - (-0.125)(0.8889) = -0.1111 + 0.1111 = 0$$

$$P_3 = (1 - k_3^2)\,P_2 = (1 - 0.015625)(1.7778) = (0.984375)(1.7778) \approx 1.75$$

**Final tabulated answer:**

| Order $m$ | $k_m$ | $a^{(m)}_1$ | $a^{(m)}_2$ | $a^{(m)}_3$ | $P_m$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.8 | 0.8 | — | — | 1.8 |
| 2 | -0.1111 | 0.8889 | -0.1111 | — | 1.7778 |
| 3 | -0.125 | 0.8750 | 0 | -0.125 | 1.75 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common mistakes that cost marks:**
> 1. **Sign errors in $k_m$ formula:** A frequent blunder is forgetting the **minus sign** in the recursion $a^{(m)}_i = a^{(m-1)}_i - k_m\,a^{(m-1)}_{m-i}$. This single sign error propagates through the entire table.
> 2. **Stability check skipped:** The examiner expects explicit verification that $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ for all stages. **If you skip this, deduct 0.5–1 mark.**
> 3. **Conflating $r(l)$ with $r(-l)$:** Always remember $r(-l) = r(l)$ for real wide-sense stationary processes. Many students write $r(1-k)$ instead of $r(k-1)$ and lose track.
> 4. **Order of updates:** Compute $k_m$ *first* using $P_{m-1}$, then update coefficients, *then* compute $P_m$. Reversing the order is a classic blunder.
> 5. **Lack of units / box the final answer:** Always enclose the final reflection coefficients, LP coefficients, and error powers in a box or table for clarity.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice* **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the lattice filter realization of the linear predictor. Show how the reflection coefficients $k_m$ and the forward/backward prediction errors $f_m(n)$ and $b_m(n)$ are related. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** For an AR(2) process $x(n) = 0.9\,x(n-1) - 0.2\,x(n-2) + w(n)$ where $w(n)$ is white noise with variance $\sigma^2_w = 1$, compute the autocorrelation values $r(0)$, $r(1)$, $r(2)$ and verify the Yule–Walker equations. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Lattice filter explanation (7 marks):**

- Draw the **single-stage lattice structure** with input $f_{m-1}(n)$ from above and $b_{m-1}(n-1)$ from below, multiplied by $k_m$ and combined via adders. **(2 marks)**
- Write the **lattice recursions**: $f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) - k_m\,b_{m-1}(n-1)$ and $b_m(n) = b_{m-1}(n-1) - k_m^*\,f_{m-1}(n)$. **(2 marks)**
- Initialize with $f_0(n) = b_0(n) = x(n)$ and cascade $p$ stages. **(1 mark)**
- State that the **PARCOR coefficient** $k_m$ satisfies $k_m = \frac{E[\,f_{m-1}(n)\,b^*_{m-1}(n-1)\,]}{E[\,\vert f_{m-1}(n)\vert^2\,]}$. **(1 mark)**
- Note that stability requires $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ and that the lattice structure is **modular**: each stage can be designed and tested independently. **(1 mark)**

**(b) Yule–Walker verification (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Compute autocorrelation from AR(2) model.**

For an AR(2) process $x(n) = a_1\,x(n-1) + a_2\,x(n-2) + w(n)$ with $a_1 = 0.9$, $a_2 = -0.2$, $\sigma^2_w = 1$, multiply both sides by $x(n-l)$ and take expectation.

**For $l = 0$:** $r(0) = a_1\,r(1) + a_2\,r(2) + \sigma^2_w$. **(1 mark)**

**For $l = 1$:** $r(1) = a_1\,r(0) + a_2\,r(1)$, so $r(1)(1 - a_2) = a_1\,r(0)$:

$$r(1) = \frac{a_1\,r(0)}{1 - a_2} = \frac{0.9\,r(0)}{1 - (-0.2)} = \frac{0.9\,r(0)}{1.2} = 0.75\,r(0)$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**For $l = 2$:** $r(2) = a_1\,r(1) + a_2\,r(0)$:

$$r(2) = (0.9)(0.75\,r(0)) + (-0.2)(r(0)) = 0.675\,r(0) - 0.2\,r(0) = 0.475\,r(0)$$

**(1.5 marks)**

**Substitute back into $l = 0$ equation:**

$$r(0) = a_1\,r(1) + a_2\,r(2) + 1$$

$$r(0) = (0.9)(0.75\,r(0)) + (-0.2)(0.475\,r(0)) + 1 = 0.675\,r(0) - 0.095\,r(0) + 1 = 0.58\,r(0) + 1$$

$$0.42\,r(0) = 1 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; r(0) = \frac{1}{0.42} = 2.3810$$

**(2 marks)**

**Therefore:**

$$r(0) = 2.3810,\quad r(1) = 0.75 \times 2.3810 = 1.7857,\quad r(2) = 0.475 \times 2.3810 = 1.1310$$

**Step 2 — Verify Yule–Walker.** **(1 mark)**

Check $\mathbf{R}_2\,\mathbf{a}_2 = \mathbf{r}_2$:

$$\begin{bmatrix} r(0) & r(1) \\ r(1) & r(0) \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} r(1) \\ r(2) \end{bmatrix}$$

$$\begin{bmatrix} 2.3810 & 1.7857 \\ 1.7857 & 2.3810 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.9 \\ -0.2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} (2.3810)(0.9) + (1.7857)(-0.2) \\ (1.7857)(0.9) + (2.3810)(-0.2) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.1429 - 0.3571 \\ 1.6071 - 0.4762 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.7858 \\ 1.1309 \end{bmatrix}$$

This matches $r(1) = 1.7857$ and $r(2) = 1.1310$ to numerical precision. ✓

> [!WARNING]
> **Additional Examiner Pitfalls for AR(2) problems:**
> 1. **Sign convention for AR coefficients:** Some textbooks use $x(n) = -a_1\,x(n-1) - a_2\,x(n-2) + w(n)$. Confirm the sign convention from your course material — KTU follows Haykin's notation: $x(n) = a_1\,x(n-1) + a_2\,x(n-2) + w(n)$.
> 2. **Inverting the wrong matrix:** The autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}_p$ must be **positive definite** for inversion. If you get a singular matrix, re-check your autocorrelation computation.
> 3. **Final answer presentation:** Box or tabulate the final autocorrelation values; do not leave them scattered in derivation.

---

### 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ The **Yule–Walker equations** are the **definitive link** between the autocorrelation sequence $r(l)$ and the LP filter parameters $\{a_k\}$.
- ✅ They arise from the **orthogonality principle**: $E[\,e(n)\,x^*(n-k)\,] = 0$ for $k = 1, \dots, p$.
- ✅ In matrix form: $\mathbf{R}_p\,\mathbf{a}_p = \mathbf{r}_p$, where $\mathbf{R}_p$ is a **Hermitian Toeplitz** matrix.
- ✅ The **minimum prediction-error power** is $P_p = r(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\,r(k)$.
- ✅ The **Levinson–Durbin algorithm** solves the Yule–Walker system in $\mathcal{O}(p^2)$ operations using the order-recursive formula.
- ✅ **Reflection coefficients** $k_m$ form an **equivalent parameter set**: $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ is required for a **stable, minimum-phase** prediction-error filter.
- ✅ The lattice structure is a **modular realization** of the LP filter where each stage corresponds to one $k_m$.
- ✅ For an **AR($p$) process**, the LP coefficients equal the AR coefficients — this is the foundation of the Burg and Yule–Walker AR spectral estimators.
- ✅ The **backward prediction error** $b_m(n)$ is the *time-reversed* counterpart of $f_m(n)$, and both share the same power $P_m$.
- ✅ The **PARCOR (partial correlation) interpretation** of $k_m$ connects LP analysis to classical statistics: it is the partial correlation between $x(n)$ and $x(n-m)$ given the intermediate samples.
- ✅ **Stability test:** Always check $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ after each Levinson–Durbin iteration. If violated, the predictor is unstable and the algorithm must terminate.
- ✅ **Uniqueness:** For a positive-definite autocorrelation matrix, the LP parameters are **uniquely determined** by the autocorrelation sequence.

> [!TIP]
> **Quick Memory Trick for Exam Day:** Think **"Y-R-L"** — **Y**ule–Walker, **R**eflection coefficients, **L**evinson–Durbin. These three are inseparable: Yule–Walker gives the equations, Reflection coefficients solve them, and Levinson–Durbin provides the efficient algorithm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Parametric method of power spectrum estimation - Yule Walker equations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT746 - ADVANCED DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Parametric method of power spectrum estimation - Yule Walker equations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Parametric Methods of Power Spectrum Estimation** are statistical signal processing techniques in which the underlying random process is modelled as the output of a linear, time-invariant, causal, discrete-time system driven by white noise. Instead of computing the Discrete Fourier Transform (DFT) directly from the data (as in non-parametric methods like the Periodogram), parametric methods assume the data is generated by a model with a small number of unknown parameters (the "parameters" of the model) and estimate these parameters from the observed samples. The power spectrum is then computed analytically from the estimated model parameters.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Definition (KTU Board Standard)**
> **Yule-Walker Equations** are a set of *p+1 linear simultaneous equations* that relate the *autocorrelation lags* $r_{xx}(0), r_{xx}(1), \ldots, r_{xx}(p)$ of a Wide-Sense Stationary (WSS) random process to the parameters $\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_p\}$ and the white noise driving variance $\sigma_w^2$ of a **p-th order Autoregressive (AR(p))** model. They form the backbone of the AR method of power spectrum estimation.

### 1.2 Three Canonical Parametric Models

Parametric models belong to one of three families, all derivable as rational transfer functions in the z-domain:

| Model | Z-Domain Transfer Function | Governing Equations |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **AR (Autoregressive)** | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sigma_w}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$ | **Yule-Walker** (linear) |
| **MA (Moving Average)** | $H(z) = \sigma_w \left[1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} b_k z^{-k}\right]$ | Nonlinear (Wold decomposition) |
| **ARMA (Combined)** | $H(z) = \dfrac{\sigma_w \left[1 + \sum_{k=1}^{q} b_k z^{-k}\right]}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$ | Nonlinear (mixed) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why AR is the KTU-favourite**: AR modelling yields the **Yule-Walker equations**, which are *linear* in the unknown AR coefficients. Hence, the model parameters can be solved by simple (and numerically robust) linear algebra, making AR the most taught and tested parametric method. MA and ARMA require non-linear optimisation (e.g., Steiglitz-McBride).

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Recipe" Interpretation

Imagine a master chef who has *never tasted a particular cake* but knows the **recipe (model structure)** used to bake it. If the chef is told the cake is a **sponge cake made with flour, sugar, eggs, and butter in a fixed ratio**, the chef can ask:
1. *"How sweet is it?"*  → Equivalent to estimating the *autocorrelation* of the data.
2. *"Given this taste profile, what ratio of ingredients most likely produced it?"*  → Equivalent to *solving for the AR coefficients*.
3. *"Knowing the recipe, what does the original baker's oven sound like (i.e., its power spectrum)?"*  → Equivalent to *analytically computing the PSD from the model parameters*.

A non-parametric method (Periodogram) is like the chef **tasting only the final cake** and trying to guess all flavours at once — accurate but limited by the available "bites" (data length). A parametric method gives the chef the **recipe in advance** — even from very few "bites", the entire flavour profile can be reconstructed with high resolution.

### 1.4 Why Do We Need Parametric Methods?

The Periodogram and its variants (Bartlett, Welch, Blackman-Tukey) suffer from three fundamental limitations:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limitations of Non-Parametric (Periodogram) Methods** — *motivates the need for Yule-Walker*
> 1. **Spectral Leakage**: Windowing causes energy to "leak" between frequency bins.
> 2. **Poor Resolution**: Resolution is $\Delta f \approx 1/N$, the Fourier limit. Two closely-spaced sinusoids cannot be separated if $N$ is small.
> 3. **High Variance**: The Periodogram is *not a consistent* estimator — its variance does **not** decrease with $N$ (it remains $O(\sigma^2)$).

Parametric AR methods using Yule-Walker equations **overcome all three** by imposing a smooth rational model on the data, producing spectra with much higher resolution and stability — even for short data records.

### 1.5 Physical / Engineering Significance

The Yule-Walker method is used in:
- **Speech processing** (LPC vocoders, formant tracking).
- **Radar and Sonar** (Doppler spectrum estimation).
- **Biomedical signal analysis** (EEG/ECG spectral features).
- **Seismology** and **geophysics** (earthquake spectral modelling).
- **Econometrics and time-series forecasting** (AR, ARMA models).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Constants / Conventions Used in KTU Notation**
> - Sampling period: $T_s = 1/F_s$ (seconds).
> - Normalised frequency: $\omega = 2\pi f T_s$ (radians/sample).
> - Variance of white driving noise: $\sigma_w^2$ (units: signal$^2$).
> - Power spectrum units: $P_{xx}(\omega)$ (signal$^2$ / (rad/sample)).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The AR(p) Model — Foundation of Yule-Walker

A WSS random process $x(n)$ is said to be **AR(p)** if it can be generated by passing white noise $w(n)$ (with zero mean and variance $\sigma_w^2$) through an all-pole filter of order *p*:

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$$

Equivalently, the transfer function of the generating filter is:

$$H(z) = \frac{X(z)}{W(z)} = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

The corresponding **autoregressive parameters** are $\{a_1, a_2, \ldots, a_p\}$ and the **excitation variance** is $\sigma_w^2$. These are the $(p+1)$ unknowns we must estimate.

### 2.2 Logical Flow of the Yule-Walker Method

The method proceeds in **four logical steps** — internalise this sequence; it is the most frequently tested conceptual path in KTU:

1. **Assume** the data $x(n)$ is WSS and generated by an AR(p) model.
2. **Compute** the biased autocorrelation estimates:
   $$\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-m} x(n) x(n+m), \quad m = 0, 1, \ldots, p$$
3. **Solve** the Yule-Walker equations for $\{a_1, \ldots, a_p, \sigma_w^2\}$.
4. **Compute** the power spectrum analytically:
   $$P_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left| 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right|^2}$$

### 2.3 Derivation of the Yule-Walker Equations (Logical Walk-Through)

The fundamental relation is obtained by multiplying both sides of the AR difference equation by $x(n-m)$ and taking the **expectation**:

**Step 1 — Multiply by $x(n-m)$:**

$$x(n) x(n-m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) x(n-m) + w(n) x(n-m)$$

**Step 2 — Take expectation $E[\cdot]$** (uses WSS property $E[x(n)x(n-m)] = r_{xx}(m)$ and orthogonality $E[w(n)x(n-m)] = 0$ for $m > 0$):

$$r_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k) + \sigma_w^2 \delta(m)$$

where $\delta(m)$ is the Kronecker delta and $\sigma_w^2 = E[w^2(n)]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $\sigma_w^2$ appears only for $m = 0$**: White noise $w(n)$ is uncorrelated with past outputs $x(n-m)$ for $m \geq 1$, so their product has zero mean. Only at $m = 0$ does $w(n)$ correlate with itself, contributing $\sigma_w^2$.

**Step 3 — Write explicitly for $m = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, p$** to form the Yule-Walker system:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m=0: \quad r_{xx}(0) &= -a_1 r_{xx}(1) - a_2 r_{xx}(2) - \cdots - a_p r_{xx}(p) + \sigma_w^2 \\
m=1: \quad r_{xx}(1) &= -a_1 r_{xx}(0) - a_2 r_{xx}(1) - \cdots - a_p r_{xx}(p-1) \\
m=2: \quad r_{xx}(2) &= -a_1 r_{xx}(1) - a_2 r_{xx}(0) - \cdots - a_p r_{xx}(p-2) \\
&\;\;\vdots \\
m=p: \quad r_{xx}(p) &= -a_1 r_{xx}(p-1) - a_2 r_{xx}(p-2) - \cdots - a_p r_{xx}(0)
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4 The Yule-Walker Matrix Form

The above $p+1$ equations can be compactly written as a **Toeplitz linear system**:

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
r_{xx}(0) & r_{xx}(1) & r_{xx}(2) & \cdots & r_{xx}(p) \\
r_{xx}(1) & r_{xx}(0) & r_{xx}(1) & \cdots & r_{xx}(p-1) \\
r_{xx}(2) & r_{xx}(1) & r_{xx}(0) & \cdots & r_{xx}(p-2) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
r_{xx}(p) & r_{xx}(p-1) & r_{xx}(p-2) & \cdots & r_{xx}(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
a_1 \\
a_2 \\
\vdots \\
a_p
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
\sigma_w^2 \\
0 \\
0 \\
\vdots \\
0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

The $(p+1) \times (p+1)$ autocorrelation matrix is **symmetric and Toeplitz** (constant along each diagonal). This special structure enables the highly efficient **Levinson-Durbin recursion**, which solves the system in $O(p^2)$ operations instead of the $O(p^3)$ required by Gaussian elimination.

### 2.5 Power Spectrum from AR Parameters

Once the AR coefficients and the driving variance are estimated, the power spectral density is obtained by evaluating the magnitude-squared of the inverse model polynomial on the unit circle:

$$P_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left| A(e^{j\omega}) \right|^2} = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left| 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right|^2}$$

For the discrete-frequency form, substitute $\omega = 2\pi f T_s$ or simply $\omega = 2\pi k / N$ for the $k$-th DFT bin.

### 2.6 Relation to Linear Prediction (Conceptual Bridge)

The Yule-Walker AR coefficients are **mathematically identical** to the coefficients of the **optimum forward linear predictor** of order $p$. The prediction error equals the white noise driving sequence, and the **minimum mean-square prediction error** equals $\sigma_w^2$. This connection is the deepest reason for the name "autoregressive" — the process is regressed on its own past.

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mandatory Formula Table for KTU 2024 Board Exams**

| # | Formula / Result | Description / Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) + w(n)$ | AR(p) model difference equation |
| 2 | $H(z) = \dfrac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$ | All-pole AR model transfer function |
| 3 | $r_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k) + \sigma_w^2 \delta(m)$ | **Yule-Walker relation** (general form) |
| 4 | $\mathbf{R}_p \mathbf{a} = -\mathbf{r}_p$ (for $m \geq 1$) | Normal equations (Levinson form) |
| 5 | $\sigma_w^2 = r_{xx}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(k)$ | Driving-noise variance (from $m=0$ equation) |
| 6 | $P_{xx}(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert^2}$ | **AR power spectrum** (all-pole PSD) |
| 7 | $E_{p}^{f} = \sigma_w^2$ | Forward prediction error power (MMSE) |
| 8 | $\hat{r}_{xx}(m) = \dfrac{1}{N} \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-m} x(n) x(n+m)$ | **Biased** autocorrelation estimate |
| 9 | $\mathrm{Toeplitz:}\; R_{ij} = r_{xx}(i-j)$ | Special structure of $\mathbf{R}$ matrix |
| 10 | Levinson recursion: $a_p^{(p)} = -\dfrac{r_{xx}(p)+\sum_{k=1}^{p-1} a_k^{(p-1)} r_{xx}(p-k)}{E_{p-1}^{f}}$ | Order-recursive solver |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility Recap**: A radar engineer estimating the Doppler spectrum of a slow-moving target, or a speech coder compressing a 8 kHz voice signal at 16 kbps, both rely on Yule-Walker AR estimation to obtain high-resolution, low-variance spectral estimates from a few milliseconds of data.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: From AR Model to Yule-Walker Equations

**Starting Point**: The AR(p) difference equation
$$x(n) + a_1 x(n-1) + a_2 x(n-2) + \cdots + a_p x(n-p) = w(n)$$

We will derive the Yule-Walker equations in full algebraic detail.

---

**Derivation Step 1 — Multiply both sides by $x^*(n-m)$** (the asterisk denotes complex conjugate for the general complex-WSS case; for real signals it is omitted):

$$x(n) x^*(n-m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k x(n-k) x^*(n-m) + w(n) x^*(n-m)$$

> **Conversion logic**: Multiplying by a delayed version of the same signal isolates the autocorrelation structure. The use of $x^*(n-m)$ ensures the result remains valid for complex-valued processes.

---

**Derivation Step 2 — Take the mathematical expectation $E[\cdot]$ of both sides**:

$$E[x(n) x^*(n-m)] = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k E[x(n-k) x^*(n-m)] + E[w(n) x^*(n-m)]$$

> **Conversion logic**: The expectation operator is linear, so it distributes over the finite sum.

---

**Derivation Step 3 — Apply the WSS property**:

For a WSS process, the autocorrelation depends only on the lag: $E[x(n) x^*(n-m)] = r_{xx}(m)$. Hence:

$$r_{xx}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k) + E[w(n) x^*(n-m)]$$

> **Conversion logic**: $r_{xx}(m-k)$ comes from $E[x(n-k) x^*(n-m)] = E[x(n-m) x^*(n-(m+k))] \cdot \text{(reindex)} = r_{xx}(m-k)$.

---

**Derivation Step 4 — Evaluate $E[w(n) x^*(n-m)]$**:

Since $x(n)$ is the *output* of a causal system driven by $w(n)$, $x(n)$ depends only on $w(k)$ for $k \leq n$. Thus for $m \geq 1$:

$$E[w(n) x^*(n-m)] = 0 \quad (\text{predictable part})$$

For $m = 0$:

$$E[w(n) x^*(n)] = E[w(n) w^*(n)] + \sum_{k=1}^{p}(\text{cross terms}) = \sigma_w^2$$

> **Conversion logic**: $x(n) = w(n) - \sum_k a_k x(n-k)$, so the cross terms involve $E[w(n) x^*(n-k)]$ for $k \geq 1$, all zero.

Combining both cases compactly:

$$E[w(n) x^*(n-m)] = \sigma_w^2 \delta(m)$$

where $\delta(m)$ is the Kronecker delta: $\delta(0) = 1$ and $\delta(m) = 0$ for $m \neq 0$.

---

**Derivation Step 5 — Assemble the final Yule-Walker relation**:

$$\boxed{\,r_{xx}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k r_{xx}(m-k) = \sigma_w^2 \delta(m)\,}$$

This is the single-equation Yule-Walker relation. Writing it out for $m = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, p$ produces the $(p+1)$ linear system.

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Power Spectrum from AR Model

**Goal**: Derive $P_{xx}(\omega) = \dfrac{\sigma_w^2}{\vert A(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2}$.

**Step 1 — Take the z-transform of the AR difference equation** (assuming zero initial conditions for the WSS process on $z = e^{j\omega}$):

$$X(z) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k} X(z) + W(z)$$

**Step 2 — Solve for the transfer function**:

$$X(z) \left[ 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k} \right] = W(z)$$

$$H(z) = \frac{X(z)}{W(z)} = \frac{1}{1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$

> **Conversion logic**: This is the all-pole model filter. The roots of the denominator polynomial are the *poles* of the AR model and correspond to spectral peaks in the PSD.

**Step 3 — Apply the input–output PSD relation**:
For a WSS process passed through an LTI system with transfer function $H(z)$:

$$P_{xx}(\omega) = \vert H(e^{j\omega}) \vert^2 P_{ww}(\omega)$$

**Step 4 — Substitute** $P_{ww}(\omega) = \sigma_w^2$ (white noise has flat PSD equal to its variance) and $H(e^{j\omega}) = \dfrac{1}{A(e^{j\omega})}$ where $A(e^{j\omega}) = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j\omega k}$:

$$P_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k e^{-j\omega k} \right\vert^2}$$

$$\boxed{\,P_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{\sigma_w^2}{\left\vert A(e^{j\omega}) \right\vert^2}\,}$$

> **Conversion logic**: The squared magnitude in the denominator converts the complex polynomial evaluation into a real, even, non-negative function of $\omega$ — the defining property of a true PSD.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example — AR(2) Yule-Walker Estimation (KTU Standard Pattern)

**Problem**: Given an observed sequence $x(0) = 1, x(1) = 2, x(2) = 3, x(3) = 2, x(4) = 1$ (N = 5), fit an AR(2) model using Yule-Walker. Compute the PSD.

---

**Step 1 — Compute the biased autocorrelation** $\hat{r}_{xx}(m)$ for $m = 0, 1, 2$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\hat{r}_{xx}(0) &= \frac{1}{5} \sum_{n=0}^{4} x^2(n) = \frac{1}{5}(1^2 + 2^2 + 3^2 + 2^2 + 1^2) = \frac{19}{5} = 3.8 \\
\hat{r}_{xx}(1) &= \frac{1}{5} \sum_{n=0}^{3} x(n) x(n+1) = \frac{1}{5}\bigl(1{\cdot}2 + 2{\cdot}3 + 3{\cdot}2 + 2{\cdot}1\bigr) = \frac{16}{5} = 3.2 \\
\hat{r}_{xx}(2) &= \frac{1}{5} \sum_{n=0}^{2} x(n) x(n+2) = \frac{1}{5}\bigl(1{\cdot}3 + 2{\cdot}2 + 3{\cdot}1\bigr) = \frac{10}{5} = 2.0
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 2 — Form the Yule-Walker linear system for $p = 2$**:

From $m = 1$:
$$\hat{r}_{xx}(1) = -a_1 \hat{r}_{xx}(0) - a_2 \hat{r}_{xx}(1)$$
$$3.2 = -a_1(3.8) - a_2(3.2)$$

From $m = 2$:
$$\hat{r}_{xx}(2) = -a_1 \hat{r}_{xx}(1) - a_2 \hat{r}_{xx}(0)$$
$$2.0 = -a_1(3.2) - a_2(3.8)$$

In matrix form:
$$\begin{bmatrix} 3.8 & 3.2 \\ 3.2 & 3.8 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} -3.2 \\ -2.0 \end{bmatrix}$$

> **Conversion logic**: For $m \geq 1$, the $\sigma_w^2$ term vanishes; only the $m=0$ equation carries the variance.

---

**Step 3 — Solve the 2×2 system** (determinant $= 3.8^2 - 3.2^2 = 14.44 - 10.24 = 4.20$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
a_1 &= \frac{(-3.2)(3.8) - (3.2)(-2.0)}{4.20} = \frac{-12.16 + 6.40}{4.20} = \frac{-5.76}{4.20} \approx -1.3714 \\
a_2 &= \frac{(3.8)(-2.0) - (-3.2)(3.2)}{4.20} = \frac{-7.60 + 10.24}{4.20} = \frac{2.64}{4.20} \approx 0.6286
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 4 — Compute $\sigma_w^2$ from the $m = 0$ equation**:

$$\sigma_w^2 = \hat{r}_{xx}(0) + a_1 \hat{r}_{xx}(1) + a_2 \hat{r}_{xx}(2)$$

$$
\sigma_w^2 = 3.8 + (-1.3714)(3.2) + (0.6286)(2.0) = 3.8 - 4.3886 + 1.2572 \approx 0.6686
$$

---

**Step 5 — Write the AR power spectrum**:

$$
P_{xx}(\omega) = \frac{0.6686}{\left\vert 1 - 1.3714\, e^{-j\omega} + 0.6286\, e^{-j2\omega} \right\vert^2}
$$

For a specific frequency, say $\omega = 0$ (DC):
$$P_{xx}(0) = \frac{0.6686}{(1 - 1.3714 + 0.6286)^2} = \frac{0.6686}{(0.2572)^2} \approx \frac{0.6686}{0.0661} \approx 10.11$$

> **Conversion logic**: Note that the spectral peak at DC matches the fact that the observed data has a strong low-frequency trend (rising then falling slowly).

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Stability Conditions & Properties

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU-Mandated Validity Conditions** for the Yule-Walker Solution
>
> 1. **Stability (Minimum-Phase)**: All poles of $H(z) = 1/A(z)$ must lie *strictly inside the unit circle*. This guarantees a stable, causal, minimum-phase AR process.
> 2. **Positive Definiteness of $\mathbf{R}$**: The autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ must be positive definite. This is guaranteed if the driving noise has $\sigma_w^2 > 0$.
> 3. **Levinson-Durbin Recursion** validity: The prediction-error power $E_p^{f}$ must remain positive at every recursion step (otherwise the order is over-specified).

| Property | Yule-Walker AR Method | Periodogram |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Resolution | High (model-driven) | Low ($\propto 1/N$) |
| Variance | Low (consistent as $N \to \infty$) | High (does not decrease) |
| Spectral leakage | None (rational model) | Yes (window-dependent) |
| Computations | $O(p^2)$ via Levinson-Durbin | $O(N \log N)$ via FFT |
| Requires model order $p$ | Yes | No |
| Produces smooth PSD | Yes | No (noisy) |
| **Pitfall** | Model order selection (AIC, MDL) | Bias-variance trade-off |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture — The Yule-Walker Method Workflow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the complete procedural flow of the Yule-Walker power spectrum estimation method, from raw data acquisition to the final smooth PSD curve.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Observed Data xn N samples] --> B[Choose AR model order p using AIC or MDL]
    B --> C[Compute biased autocorrelation rxxm for m = 0 to p]
    C --> D[Form Toeplitz autocorrelation matrix R of size p+1 by p+1]
    D --> E[Solve Yule Walker linear system Ra = r]
    E --> F[Obtain AR coefficients a1 to ap]
    F --> G[Compute driving variance sigma_w squared from m = 0 equation]
    G --> H[Form all pole polynomial A of z = 1 plus sum a_k z to the minus k]
    H --> I[Evaluate magnitude squared vert A of e to the j omega vert squared over omega]
    I --> J[Smooth all pole PSD Pxx of omega = sigma_w squared divided by vert A vert squared]
    J --> K[Output high resolution power spectrum]

    style A fill:#E3F2FD,stroke:#1976D2
    style C fill:#FFF3E0,stroke:#F57C00
    style E fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#7B1FA2
    style G fill:#E8F5E9,stroke:#388E3C
    style J fill:#FFEBEE,stroke:#C62828
    style K fill:#E0F7FA,stroke:#00695C
```

### 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — AR(p) Process Generation and Estimation

The following Mermaid diagram shows how the AR process is *generated* in nature (forward model) and how the Yule-Walker method *inverts* it (estimation path).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    WN[White noise wn variance sigma_w squared] --> FGEN[AR Filter H of z equals 1 over A of z]
    FGEN --> OUTS[Observed process xn]
    OUTS --> ACEST[Autocorrelation Estimator r hat xx of m]
    ACEST --> YWSOL[Yule Walker Solver Linear System]
    YWSOL --> ARCOEF[Estimated AR coefficients a hat 1 to a hat p]
    YWSOL --> SIGEST[Estimated sigma hat w squared]
    ARCOEF --> PSDCOMP[All pole PSD Evaluator]
    SIGEST --> PSDCOMP
    PSDCOMP --> PSDFIN[Final PSD estimate P hat xx of omega]

    style WN fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#0277BD
    style FGEN fill:#FFF9C4,stroke:#F9A825
    style OUTS fill:#FFCCBC,stroke:#E64A19
    style ACEST fill:#DCEDC8,stroke:#558B2F
    style YWSOL fill:#F8BBD0,stroke:#AD1457
    style PSDCOMP fill:#D1C4E9,stroke:#512DA8
    style PSDFIN fill:#B2DFDB,stroke:#00695C
```

### 4.3 Conceptual Linkage — Periodogram vs. Yule-Walker

The following Mermaid diagram contrasts the two approaches visually for student intuition.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph NONPARAM[Non Parametric Path - Periodogram]
        D1[Data xn] --> D2[Window wn]
        D2 --> D3[FFT Computation]
        D3 --> D4[Periodogram Pxx of omega]
    end
    subgraph PARAM[Parametric Path - Yule Walker AR]
        E1[Data xn] --> E2[Autocorrelation rxx of m]
        E2 --> E3[Form Toeplitz Matrix R]
        E3 --> E4[Solve Linear System for a hat k]
        E4 --> E5[All Pole Model A of z]
        E5 --> E6[Analytical PSD sigma squared over vert A vert squared]
    end
    D4 -.-> CMP[Compare Resolution and Variance]
    E6 -.-> CMP

    style NONPARAM fill:#FFFDE7,stroke:#FBC02D
    style PARAM fill:#E8EAF6,stroke:#3F51B5
    style CMP fill:#F3E5F5,stroke:#6A1B9A
```
