<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`**  
Define the **averaging periodogram** (Bartlett's method) for power spectral estimation. State the formula and mention the key variance-reduction property.

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> The averaging periodogram is a non-parametric PSD estimation method in which a data sequence $x(n)$ of length $N$ is divided into $K$ non-overlapping segments, each of length $M = N/K$. The periodogram of each segment is computed, and the $K$ periodograms are averaged to produce the final estimate:
> 
> $$\hat{P}_{\text{avg}}(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \hat{P}_{M}^{(i)}(f)$$
> 
> **Key property:** The variance of the estimate is reduced by a factor of $K$ compared to the standard periodogram, i.e., $\text{Var}[\hat{P}_{\text{avg}}(f)] \approx P_{xx}^{2}(f) / K$. *(3 marks: 1 for definition, 1 for formula, 1 for variance property)*

---

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`**  
What is the main drawback of the standard periodogram, and how does the averaging periodogram address it?

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

> **Drawback of the standard periodogram:** Its variance does not decrease as the data length $N$ increases — it is asymptotically equal to $P_{xx}^{2}(f)$, making it an **inconsistent** estimator.
> 
> **How averaging periodogram fixes it:** By splitting the data into $K$ non-overlapping segments of length $M = N/K$ and averaging the $K$ periodograms, the variance is reduced by a factor of $K$ (since averaging independent estimates reduces variance). The estimator becomes consistent as both $K \to \infty$ and $M \to \infty$. *(3 marks: 1 for identifying the drawback, 1 for the fix mechanism, 1 for the consistency property)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice Pattern)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **averaging periodogram** estimator $\hat{P}_{\text{Bartlett}}(f)$ starting from the definition of the periodogram of a single segment. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A signal $x(n)$ of length $N = 2048$ is processed using the averaging periodogram with $K = 16$ segments. Compute the **segment length**, the **variance-reduction factor**, and the **frequency resolution**. Compare these with the standard periodogram of the full data. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Derivation (7 Marks):**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Define segmentation. The $N$-point data $x(n)$ is partitioned into $K$ non-overlapping segments:
$$x_i(n) = x(n + iM), \quad i = 0, 1, \ldots, K-1, \quad n = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1$$
where $M = N/K$. **Assumption:** $N$ is divisible by $K$.

- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Define the $M$-point DTFT of each segment:
$$X_i(f) = \sum_{n=0}^{M-1} x_i(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n}$$

- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Define the periodogram of each segment:
$$\hat{P}_{M}^{(i)}(f) = \frac{1}{M}\lvert X_i(f)\rvert^2$$

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** State the averaging operation. The Bartlett estimate is the arithmetic mean of the $K$ segment periodograms:
$$\hat{P}_{\text{Bartlett}}(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \hat{P}_{M}^{(i)}(f)$$

- **Step 5 (1 mark):** Substituting the periodogram expression:
$$\hat{P}_{\text{Bartlett}}(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \frac{1}{M}\left|\sum_{n=0}^{M-1} x_i(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n}\right|^2$$

- **Step 6 (1 mark):** Simplify using $KM = N$:
$$\hat{P}_{\text{Bartlett}}(f) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1}\left|\sum_{n=0}^{M-1} x_i(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n}\right|^2$$

- **Step 7 (1 mark):** State the **assumptions**: (i) WSS process, (ii) segments are approximately independent realizations, (iii) $N$ divisible by $K$ (or use floor/truncation).

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation (7 Marks):**

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** Segment length:
$$M = \frac{N}{K} = \frac{2048}{16} = 128 \text{ samples}$$

- **Step 2 (2 marks):** Variance-reduction factor:
$$\text{Factor} = K = 16 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Variance} = \frac{P_{xx}^{2}(f)}{16}$$
This is **16× lower** variance than the standard periodogram.

- **Step 3 (2 marks):** Frequency resolution of the averaging periodogram:
$$\Delta f_{\text{Bartlett}} = \frac{1}{M} = \frac{1}{128} \approx 7.8125 \times 10^{-3} \text{ cycles/sample}$$

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Comparison with standard periodogram of full data:
$$\Delta f_{\text{std}} = \frac{1}{N} = \frac{1}{2048} \approx 4.88 \times 10^{-4} \text{ cycles/sample}$$
$$\text{Resolution loss} = \frac{\Delta f_{\text{Bartlett}}}{\Delta f_{\text{std}}} = \frac{N}{M} = K = 16 \times$$

**Conclusion:** Variance reduced by 16×, but resolution degraded by 16× — classic trade-off.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Starting from the **variance expression of the standard periodogram**, derive the **variance of the averaging periodogram** and show that it is reduced by a factor of $K$. State the assumptions. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **bias–variance trade-off** in the averaging periodogram with reference to the segment length $M$ and the number of segments $K$. Sketch a qualitative graph of variance and squared bias as functions of $K$ (with $N$ fixed). **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Variance Derivation (7 Marks):**

- **Step 1 (1 mark):** Standard periodogram variance. For a Gaussian WSS process, the variance of the standard $N$-point periodogram is:
$$\text{Var}[\hat{P}_{\text{per}}(f)] \approx P_{xx}^{2}(f)$$

- **Step 2 (1 mark):** Independence assumption. For non-overlapping segments of a WSS process, the periodograms $\hat{P}_{M}^{(i)}(f)$ and $\hat{P}_{M}^{(j)}(f)$ for $i \neq j$ are approximately **uncorrelated**.

- **Step 3 (1 mark):** Apply the variance-of-a-sum formula for uncorrelated random variables:
$$\text{Var}\left[\frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1} \hat{P}_{M}^{(i)}(f)\right] = \frac{1}{K^{2}}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1}\text{Var}\left[\hat{P}_{M}^{(i)}(f)\right]$$

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** Since each segment periodogram has the same variance $P_{xx}^{2}(f)$ (approximately):
$$= \frac{1}{K^{2}} \cdot K \cdot P_{xx}^{2}(f)$$

- **Step 5 (2 marks):** Therefore:
$$\boxed{\text{Var}[\hat{P}_{\text{Bartlett}}(f)] \approx \frac{P_{xx}^{2}(f)}{K}}$$

- **Step 6 (1 mark):** **Assumptions**: (i) Gaussian WSS process, (ii) segments are approximately independent, (iii) $M$ is large enough for the periodogram to be a stable estimate of $P_{xx}(f)$.

**Part (b) — Bias–Variance Trade-off (7 Marks):**

- **Step 1 (2 marks):** **Variance behavior.** As $K$ increases (with $N$ fixed), $M = N/K$ decreases. Variance $\propto 1/K$, so variance **decreases monotonically** as $K$ increases.

- **Step 2 (2 marks):** **Bias behavior.** Bias is governed by the Fejér-kernel smoothing of width $\sim 1/M$. As $M$ decreases (i.e., $K$ increases), the smoothing kernel broadens, so the **squared bias increases**.

- **Step 3 (2 marks):** **Qualitative graph:**

```
   |  B^2(f)  (Squared Bias)  ──── increases
   |          ╱
   |         ╱
   |        ╱
   |       ╱
   |      ╱         σ^2(f) (Variance) ──── decreases
   |─────╱────────╲
   |    ╱          ╲
   |___╱____________╲______ K
   1               N/M
```

- **Step 4 (1 mark):** The total **mean-square error** $\text{MSE} = \text{Bias}^2 + \text{Variance}$ has a **minimum at some optimal $K$** that balances the two effects. The choice of $K = 4$ to $16$ is typical in practice.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the normalization factor $1/M$** in the segment periodogram. The correct form is $\hat{P}_{M}^{(i)}(f) = \frac{1}{M}\lvert X_i(f)\rvert^2$, **not** $\lvert X_i(f)\rvert^2$. Examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 
> 2. **Confusing Bartlett's and Welch's methods.** Bartlett uses **non-overlapping** segments with **no windowing**. Welch uses **overlapping** segments with a **window function** (e.g., Hamming). A 14-mark question on this is a high-risk trap.
> 
> 3. **Stating the variance reduction incorrectly.** The variance is reduced to $P_{xx}^{2}(f) / K$, not to $P_{xx}^{2}(f) - K$ or any other expression. The factor $K$ goes in the **denominator**.
> 
> 4. **Ignoring the resolution trade-off.** Whenever you mention variance reduction by factor $K$, you **must** also mention that the frequency resolution degrades by the same factor $K$. Examiners look for this trade-off explicitly.
> 
> 5. **Confusing bias and consistency.** A finite-$K$, finite-$M$ averaging periodogram is **biased** for any finite $M$, but it is **asymptotically unbiased** and **consistent** as $M, K \to \infty$. Be precise with these terms.
> 
> 6. **Sign errors in the DTFT exponent.** KTU expects $e^{-j2\pi f n}$ in the forward transform (and $e^{+j2\pi f n}$ in the inverse). Reversed signs will mark the entire derivation wrong.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Summary — Memorize Before Every Exam**

- **Definition:** Averaging periodogram = average of $K$ periodograms computed from $K$ non-overlapping segments of length $M = N/K$.

- **Core formula:** 
$$\hat{P}_{\text{Bartlett}}(f) = \frac{1}{K}\sum_{i=0}^{K-1}\frac{1}{M}\left|\sum_{n=0}^{M-1} x_i(n)\, e^{-j2\pi f n}\right|^2$$

- **Bias:** Asymptotically unbiased; for finite $M$, the bias is governed by the Fejér-kernel smoothing of width $\sim 1/M$.

- **Variance:** $\text{Var}[\hat{P}_{\text{Bartlett}}(f)] \approx P_{xx}^{2}(f) / K$ — **reduced by factor $K$**.

- **Resolution:** $\Delta f = 1/M = K/N$ — **degraded by factor $K$** compared to full periodogram.

- **Consistency:** Yes, if both $M \to \infty$ and $K \to \infty$ (with $M \cdot K = N \to \infty$).

- **Independence assumption:** The $K$ segment periodograms are assumed approximately uncorrelated (valid for non-overlapping segments of a WSS process).

- **Bartlett vs. Welch:** Bartlett = non-overlapping, no window. Welch = overlapping (typically 50%), windowed. Welch gives better trade-off in practice.

- **Key trade-off identity:** Variance $\times$ (resolution bandwidth) $\approx$ constant. This is the spectral analog of the **uncertainty principle**.

- **Typical choices in practice:** $K = 4, 8, 16$ or $32$ are common. $K$ is rarely chosen $> 64$ because the segments become too short for reliable periodograms.

- **MATLAB command equivalent:** `pwelch(x, hanning(M), overlap, NFFT, Fs)` (this is Welch's extension, not pure Bartlett, but the conceptual link is direct).

- **Exam memory anchor:** Whenever you see "averaging periodogram" in a question, immediately write down the four items: **definition → formula → bias → variance + resolution trade-off**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Linear prediction filters - Forward and backward predictors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT746 - ADVANCED DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Linear prediction filters - Forward and backward predictors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Linear Prediction (LP)** is a fundamental mathematical tool in Digital Signal Processing in which a future (or current) sample of a discrete-time stochastic or deterministic signal is estimated as a **linear combination of its past samples**. The device that performs this estimation is called a **Linear Prediction Filter**, and the difference between the actual sample and its predicted value is termed the **Prediction Error** (or residual).

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, the formal definition is:

> A **p-th order linear predictor** is a linear discrete-time filter with coefficients $\{a_1, a_2, \dots, a_p\}$ that produces an output $\hat{x}(n)$ approximating $x(n)$ as:
>
> $$\hat{x}(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k \, x(n-k)$$

The negative sign convention is standard in KTU-board textbooks (Oppenheim & Schafer, Proakis & Manolakis) because the predictor is often embedded in an **all-pole (AR) inverse filter** where the coefficients form a polynomial $A(z)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Terminology Note**
> Always maintain the **negative sign convention** for predictor coefficients. Using the convention $\hat{x}(n) = \sum w_k x(n-k)$ (without the negative sign) is acceptable only if explicitly mentioned. Board examiners expect the sign-negative form as it connects directly to the Yule-Walker / Normal equations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Weather Forecaster

Imagine a meteorologist trying to predict **tomorrow's maximum temperature** $T(n+1)$ using the temperatures of the last $p$ days: $T(n), T(n-1), \dots, T(n-p+1)$.

- The meteorologist assigns **weights** (e.g., yesterday matters more than the day before) to each past observation.
- The **predicted** value is a weighted sum of the past.
- The **error** is the difference between what actually happens and what was predicted.
- The goal of a "smart" forecaster is to **choose the weights** so that the **mean squared error** is minimized over many years of data.

The Linear Prediction Filter is exactly this "smart forecaster" but for signals: audio samples, ECG waveforms, seismic data, or speech signals. The weights $\{a_k\}$ are chosen to minimize the average squared prediction error.

| Real-World Analogy | DSP Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Past temperature values | Past signal samples $x(n-1), \dots, x(n-p)$ |
| Influence weights (e.g., 0.7, 0.2, 0.1) | Predictor coefficients $a_1, a_2, \dots, a_p$ |
| Predicted tomorrow's temperature | Predicted sample $\hat{x}(n)$ |
| Actual minus predicted (error) | Prediction error $e(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}(n)$ |
| Long-term best weights | Wiener / Optimum solution |

## 1.3 Forward and Backward Predictors — The Two Complementary Lenses

The KTU Module-4 syllabus explicitly distinguishes two symmetric perspectives:

### A. **Forward Linear Predictor (FLP)**
Predicts the **current (or next) sample** $x(n)$ using the **immediately preceding $p$ samples** $\{x(n-1), x(n-2), \dots, x(n-p)\}$.

$$\hat{x}^{f}_{p}(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{f}_{p}(k) \, x(n-k)$$

The superscript $f$ denotes "forward" and the subscript $p$ denotes the **order** of the predictor.

### B. **Backward Linear Predictor (BLP)**
Predicts the **oldest (most remote) sample** $x(n-p)$ using the **$p$ most recent samples** $\{x(n), x(n-1), \dots, x(n-p+1)\}$.

$$\hat{x}^{b}_{p}(n-p) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{b}_{p}(k) \, x(n-p+k)$$

The superscript $b$ denotes "backward" and the summation runs over the recent $p$ samples leading up to $x(n)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Two Predictors?**
> The **forward predictor** is used for **encoding, whitening, and spectral estimation** (e.g., LPC vocoders in speech processing). The **backward predictor** is essential for the **Lattice filter realization** and the **Levinson-Durbin algorithm**, which together form the computational backbone of almost every modern speech codec (CELP, MELP, GSM, Speex).

## 1.4 The Prediction Error Filter (PEF)

A closely related concept is the **Prediction Error Filter**, which produces the residual error signal $e(n)$:

$$e^{f}_{p}(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}^{f}_{p}(n) = x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{f}_{p}(k) \, x(n-k)$$

In the $z$-domain, the forward PEF has the transfer function:

$$A^{f}_{p}(z) = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{f}_{p}(k) \, z^{-k}$$

This filter is **all-zero (FIR)** and, when the input is an AR(p) process, produces a **white noise** output — the reason LP is the foundation of linear predictive coding (LPC).

## 1.5 Key Physical / Engineering Constants & Metrics

- **Order $p$**: dimensionless integer; must satisfy $p \ll N$ (where $N$ is the data length) for statistical reliability. Typical values: $p = 8$ to $16$ for speech (8 kHz sampling), $p = 4$ to $20$ for ECG, $p = 50$ to $200$ for wideband audio.
- **Mean Squared Error (MSE)**: $J_p = E\{\vert e(n) \vert^{2}\}$, measured in **watts** (signal-power units).
- **Prediction Gain** $R_p = \sigma_{x}^{2} / \sigma_{e}^{2}$: dimensionless ratio, in **decibels (dB)** when expressed as $10 \log_{10}(R_p)$. Higher gain → better predictor.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Forward Linear Prediction Problem — Step by Step

The forward predictor of order $p$ produces an estimate:

$$\hat{x}^{f}_{p}(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{f}_{p}(k) \, x(n-k)$$

**Step 1 — Define the prediction error** (scalar signal at time $n$):
$$e^{f}_{p}(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}^{f}_{p}(n) = x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{f}_{p}(k) \, x(n-k)$$

**Step 2 — Define the cost function** (Mean Squared Error, MSE):
$$J^{f}_{p} = E\left\{\left(e^{f}_{p}(n)\right)^{2}\right\} = E\left\{\left(x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{f}_{p}(k) \, x(n-k)\right)^{2}\right\}$$

**Step 3 — Minimize $J^{f}_{p}$** with respect to each coefficient $a^{f}_{p}(m)$ by setting the partial derivative to zero (orthogonality principle):

$$\frac{\partial J^{f}_{p}}{\partial a^{f}_{p}(m)} = 2 \, E\left\{e^{f}_{p}(n) \, x(n-m)\right\} = 0 \quad \text{for } m = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

**Step 4 — The Forward Normal Equations (Yule-Walker for AR)**:
$$r_{x}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{f}_{p}(k) \, r_{x}(m-k) = 0 \quad \text{for } m = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

where $r_{x}(k) = E\{x(n) x(n-k)\}$ is the **autocorrelation** of $x(n)$ at lag $k$.

**Step 5 — Minimum MSE** (using the orthogonality, $E\{e \cdot x\} = 0$):
$$J^{f}_{p,\min} = r_{x}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{f}_{p}(k) \, r_{x}(k)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **The Orthogonality Principle**
> At the optimum, the prediction error $e^{f}_{p}(n)$ is **orthogonal** to every past sample used in prediction. This is the cornerstone of all Wiener filtering and is a high-yield KTU conceptual question.

## 2.2 The Backward Linear Prediction Problem

The backward predictor of order $p$ estimates the sample $p$ steps in the past:

$$\hat{x}^{b}_{p}(n-p) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{b}_{p}(k) \, x(n-p+k)$$

**Backward error**:
$$e^{b}_{p}(n) = x(n-p) - \hat{x}^{b}_{p}(n-p) = x(n-p) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{b}_{p}(k) \, x(n-p+k)$$

By the stationarity assumption, minimizing $E\{(e^{b}_{p})^{2}\}$ leads to the **Backward Normal Equations**:

$$r_{x}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{b}_{p}(k) \, r_{x}(m-k) = 0 \quad \text{for } m = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

## 2.3 The Beautiful Symmetry: Forward = Backward Coefficients

Comparing the two normal equation sets, it becomes evident they are **identical** because the autocorrelation function $r_{x}(k)$ is **even** (real, stationary signals): $r_{x}(-k) = r_{x}(k)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KEY KTU BOARD RESULT**
> For a **real, wide-sense stationary (WSS)** random process, the optimum forward and backward linear predictor coefficients are **identical**:
>
> $$a^{f}_{p}(k) = a^{b}_{p}(k) = a_{p}(k) \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$
>
> This is why, throughout the rest of Module 4, we simply write $a_p(k)$ without the $f$ or $b$ superscript.

## 2.4 Augmented Normal Equations (Matrix Form)

Combining the orthogonality conditions for $m = 0, 1, \dots, p$ into a single matrix equation (this is the form used in Levinson-Durbin):

$$
\begin{bmatrix}
r_{x}(0) & r_{x}(1) & r_{x}(2) & \cdots & r_{x}(p) \\
r_{x}(1) & r_{x}(0) & r_{x}(1) & \cdots & r_{x}(p-1) \\
r_{x}(2) & r_{x}(1) & r_{x}(0) & \cdots & r_{x}(p-2) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
r_{x}(p) & r_{x}(p-1) & r_{x}(p-2) & \cdots & r_{x}(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
1 \\
a_{p}(1) \\
a_{p}(2) \\
\vdots \\
a_{p}(p)
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
J_{p,\min} \\
0 \\
0 \\
\vdots \\
0
\end{bmatrix}
$$

This is a **Toeplitz system** (constant along each diagonal), which is exactly why the Levinson-Durbin algorithm is so efficient — it solves it in $O(p^{2})$ instead of the $O(p^{3})$ of Gaussian elimination.

## 2.5 Relationship to AR Modeling and Whitening

If $x(n)$ is the output of an **all-pole (AR) filter** driven by white noise $w(n)$ of variance $\sigma_{w}^{2}$:

$$x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} \alpha_{k} \, x(n-k) + w(n)$$

then the LP coefficients $a_{p}(k) = \alpha_{k}$ — the predictor coefficients are the **AR model parameters** themselves. The forward prediction error filter $A_p(z) = 1 + \sum a_p(k) z^{-k}$ is the **inverse** of the AR generator, and its output is the **white noise** $w(n)$ itself.

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| **Quantity** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Significance / Units** |
|---|---|---|
| Forward prediction estimate | $\hat{x}^{f}_{p}(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, x(n-k)$ | Approximation of $x(n)$; dimensionless |
| Forward prediction error | $e^{f}_{p}(n) = x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, x(n-k)$ | Residual; same units as $x(n)$ |
| Backward prediction estimate | $\hat{x}^{b}_{p}(n-p) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, x(n-p+k)$ | Approximation of $x(n-p)$ |
| Backward prediction error | $e^{b}_{p}(n) = x(n-p) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, x(n-p+k)$ | Time-reversed residual |
| Forward PE filter | $A_{p}(z) = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, z^{-k}$ | FIR, all-zero, $p$-th order |
| Autocorrelation | $r_{x}(k) = E\{x(n) x(n-k)\}$ | Real, even: $r_{x}(-k) = r_{x}(k)$ |
| Normal equation (Yule-Walker) | $r_{x}(m) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, r_{x}(m-k)$ | Holds for $m = 1, \dots, p$ |
| Min. forward MSE | $J_{p,\min} = r_{x}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, r_{x}(k)$ | Variance of error; units of $x^{2}$ |
| Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma_{p} = a_{p}(p)$ | Bridge between order $p-1$ and $p$ |
| Prediction gain | $R_{p} = \sigma_{x}^{2} \, / \, J_{p,\min}$ | Dimensionless; in dB: $10 \log_{10} R_{p}$ |
| Whitening property | $A_{p}(z) \, X(z) = W(z)$ | $w(n)$ is white noise of variance $J_{p,\min}$ |

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech Coding (LPC, CELP, MELP)**: $p = 8$ to $16$ coefficients compress speech to **2.4 kbps** (military MELP) or **4.75–12.2 kbps** (3GPP AMR).
- **Spectral Estimation**: Replaces the (unknown) true PSD with the all-pole estimate $\hat{P}_{AR}(f) = J_{p,\min} \, / \, \vert A_{p}(e^{j2\pi f}) \vert^{2}$, giving sharp spectral peaks from short data records.
- **Audio Coding (MPEG-4 HVXC, Speex)**: Wideband LP analysis for low-bit-rate voice storage and VoIP.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing**: ECG compression and arrhythmia detection using $p \approx 8$ to $20$.
- **Seismic Data Compression**: LP-based prediction residuals reduce data volume for storage/transmission.
- **Channel Equalization & Echo Cancellation**: Adaptive LP variants (NLMS, RLS) are used in modern acoustic-echo cancelers.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Forward Normal Equations from MSE Minimization

We want to find $\{a_{p}(k)\}$ that minimize $J_{p} = E\{(e_{p}^{f}(n))^{2}\}$.

**Start** by writing the squared error as a product:

$$J_{p} = E\left\{\left[x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) x(n-k)\right]^{2}\right\}$$

**Expand** the squared product (binomial expansion in summation form):

$$J_{p} = E\left\{x^{2}(n) + 2 \, x(n) \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) x(n-k) + \sum_{k=1}^{p}\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) a_{p}(j) \, x(n-k) x(n-j)\right\}$$

**Apply expectation** (linearity of $E\{\cdot\}$ and use $r_x(m) = E\{x(n) x(n-m)\}$):

$$J_{p} = r_{x}(0) + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, r_{x}(k) + \sum_{k=1}^{p}\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, a_{p}(j) \, r_{x}(k-j)$$

**Differentiate** $J_{p}$ with respect to coefficient $a_{p}(m)$ (for $m = 1, \dots, p$):

$$\frac{\partial J_{p}}{\partial a_{p}(m)} = 2 \, r_{x}(m) + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, r_{x}(m-k) = 0$$

**Divide by 2 and rearrange** to obtain the **Forward Normal Equations**:

$$
\boxed{\;
\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, r_{x}(m-k) = -r_{x}(m) \quad \text{for } m = 1, 2, \dots, p
\;}
$$

**Derive the minimum MSE** by substituting the optimum coefficients back. Since $E\{e_p(n) x(n-m)\} = 0$ for $m = 1, \dots, p$ (orthogonality), only the $r_x(0)$ term survives:

$$J_{p,\min} = r_{x}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, r_{x}(k)$$

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Backward Normal Equations

Starting point — backward prediction error:

$$e^{b}_{p}(n) = x(n-p) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}^{b}(k) \, x(n-p+k)$$

Define cost $J^{b}_{p} = E\{(e^{b}_{p})^{2}\}$:

$$J^{b}_{p} = E\left\{\left[x(n-p) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}^{b}(k) \, x(n-p+k)\right]^{2}\right\}$$

Differentiate w.r.t. $a_{p}^{b}(m)$:

$$\frac{\partial J^{b}_{p}}{\partial a_{p}^{b}(m)} = 2 \, E\{e^{b}_{p}(n) \, x(n-p+m)\} = 0 \quad (m = 1, \dots, p)$$

Expanding using $E\{x(n-p) x(n-p+m)\} = r_x(m)$ and $E\{x(n-p+k) x(n-p+m)\} = r_x(m-k)$:

$$r_{x}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}^{b}(k) \, r_{x}(m-k) = 0 \quad \text{for } m = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

**Conclusion**: The forward and backward normal equations are **identical** in form. Since the autocorrelation matrix is **symmetric positive-definite**, the unique solution must satisfy:

$$a_{p}^{b}(k) = a_{p}^{f}(k) \quad \text{for all } k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Forward and Backward Error Cross-Correlation

An important relation used in **Levinson-Durbin** and **lattice filters** is the cross-correlation between the forward and backward prediction errors of order $p$:

$$\Delta_{p} = E\{e^{f}_{p}(n) \, e^{b}_{p}(n-1)\}$$

**Expand** the two error definitions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
e^{f}_{p}(n) &= x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, x(n-k) \\
e^{b}_{p}(n-1) &= x(n-1-p) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} a_{p}(j) \, x(n-1-p+j)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Form the product** and apply expectation:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{p} = E\left\{x(n) x(n-1-p) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} a_{p}(j) \, x(n) x(n-1-p+j) \right. \\
\left. + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, x(n-k) x(n-1-p) + \sum_{k=1}^{p}\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) a_{p}(j) x(n-k) x(n-1-p+j)\right\}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Substitute** $E\{x(n-m) x(n-\ell)\} = r_x(\ell - m)$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta_{p} = r_x(1+p) + \sum_{j=1}^{p} a_{p}(j) r_x(1+p-j) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) r_x(1+p-k) \\
+ \sum_{k=1}^{p}\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) a_{p}(j) r_x(1+p-j+k)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Apply the forward normal equation** with $m = 1+p$ (the case $m = 1+p$ is the **upper-row Yule-Walker** relation):

$$r_x(1+p) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, r_x(1+p-k) = 0$$

This is the **augmented** normal equation. With this substitution, the first two sums above are **zero** (the first sum is the normal eq. with index shift; the second is identical to it). Reorganizing the double sum with index $\ell = k - j$:

$$
\Delta_{p} = \sum_{k=1}^{p}\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) a_{p}(j) \, r_x(1+p-j+k)
$$

Let $\ell = j - 1$, then $1 + p - j + k = (p - \ell) + k$. After careful index manipulation and using symmetry of $r_x$, one obtains the closed form:

$$
\boxed{\; \Delta_{p} = \sum_{k=0}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, r_x(p+1-k) \;}
$$

(with the convention $a_p(0) = 1$). This quantity $\Delta_p$ is the **bridge term** used in the Levinson-Durbin recursion to compute the new reflection coefficient:

$$\Gamma_{p+1} = -\frac{\Delta_{p}}{J_{p,\min}}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Order-Update Relations (Levinson-Durbin Foundation)

A **fundamental result** of LP theory: the coefficients of order $p$ are related to those of order $p-1$ by a single new parameter — the **reflection coefficient** $\Gamma_p$:

$$
\boxed{\;
a_{p}(k) = a_{p-1}(k) + \Gamma_{p} \, a_{p-1}(p-k) \quad \text{for } k = 1, 2, \dots, p-1
\;}
$$

and for the new coefficient:

$$a_{p}(p) = \Gamma_{p}$$

with the minimum MSE updating as:

$$J_{p,\min} = J_{p-1,\min} \left(1 - \Gamma_{p}^{2}\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this matters for KTU**
> The Levinson-Durbin recursion enables $O(p^{2})$ computation and is the bridge to the **lattice filter** realization. The reflection coefficient $\Gamma_p$ must satisfy $\vert \Gamma_{p} \vert < 1$ for the autocorrelation matrix to be positive-definite — a board-favorite true/false question.

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example

Let $x(n)$ be a stationary process with autocorrelations:
$$r_x(0) = 1, \quad r_x(1) = 0.5, \quad r_x(2) = 0.3$$

**Predictor of order $p = 1$**:

The Yule-Walker equation:
$$r_x(1) + a_1(1) \, r_x(0) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 0.5 + a_1(1) \cdot 1 = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_1(1) = -0.5$$

Minimum MSE: $J_{1,\min} = r_x(0) + a_1(1) r_x(1) = 1 + (-0.5)(0.5) = 0.75$

Reflection coefficient: $\Gamma_1 = a_1(1) = -0.5$ (satisfies $\vert \Gamma_1 \vert < 1$ ✓)

**Predictor of order $p = 2$**:

Using the order-update:
$$a_2(2) = \Gamma_2$$
$$a_2(1) = a_1(1) + \Gamma_2 \cdot a_1(1) = a_1(1)(1 + \Gamma_2)$$

But to find $\Gamma_2$, we need $\Delta_1$ and $J_{1,\min}$:
$$\Delta_1 = r_x(2) + a_1(1) r_x(1) = 0.3 + (-0.5)(0.5) = 0.05$$
$$\Gamma_2 = -\frac{\Delta_1}{J_{1,\min}} = -\frac{0.05}{0.75} = -0.0667$$

Therefore:
$$a_2(1) = -0.5 + (-0.0667)(-0.5) = -0.5 + 0.0333 = -0.4667$$
$$a_2(2) = -0.0667$$

Minimum MSE: $J_{2,\min} = 0.75 \cdot (1 - 0.0667^{2}) = 0.75 \cdot 0.9956 = 0.7467$

**Verification** by direct Yule-Walker for $p = 2$:

For $m = 1$: $r_x(1) + a_2(1) r_x(0) + a_2(2) r_x(1) = 0$
$$0.5 + a_2(1)(1) + a_2(2)(0.5) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; a_2(1) + 0.5 a_2(2) = -0.5$$

For $m = 2$: $r_x(2) + a_2(1) r_x(1) + a_2(2) r_x(0) = 0$
$$0.3 + 0.5 a_2(1) + a_2(2) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; 0.5 a_2(1) + a_2(2) = -0.3$$

Solving: from equation 1, $a_2(1) = -0.5 - 0.5 a_2(2)$. Substituting:
$$0.5(-0.5 - 0.5 a_2(2)) + a_2(2) = -0.3$$
$$-0.25 - 0.25 a_2(2) + a_2(2) = -0.3$$
$$0.75 a_2(2) = -0.05 \;\Rightarrow\; a_2(2) = -0.0667 \quad ✓$$
$$a_2(1) = -0.5 - 0.5(-0.0667) = -0.4667 \quad ✓$$

The Levinson-Durbin and direct Yule-Walker methods produce **identical** results, confirming the theory.

## 3.6 Analytical Summary Matrix

| **Derivation** | **Result** | **Used In** |
|---|---|---|
| Min. MSE → orthogonality | $E\{e_p(n) x(n-m)\} = 0$ for $m = 1..p$ | Wiener filter theory |
| Orthogonality → Yule-Walker | $\sum a_p(k) r_x(m-k) = -r_x(m)$ | LP coefficient solving |
| Augmented equation ($m = 0$) | $J_{p,\min} = r_x(0) + \sum a_p(k) r_x(k)$ | Performance metric |
| Forward = Backward (WSS) | $a_p^{f}(k) = a_p^{b}(k)$ | Lattice filter realization |
| Order recursion | $a_p(k) = a_{p-1}(k) + \Gamma_p a_{p-1}(p-k)$ | Levinson-Durbin |
| MSE update | $J_{p,\min} = J_{p-1,\min}(1 - \Gamma_p^{2})$ | Boundedness check |
| Reflection coefficient | $\Gamma_p = -\Delta_{p-1} / J_{p-1,\min}$ | Step-1 of recursion |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Forward Linear Prediction Filter — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeIn["INPUT x(n)"] --> nodeDelay1["DELAY z^-1"]
    nodeIn --> nodeSum["SUMMATION JUNCTION"]
    nodeDelay1 --> nodeDelay2["DELAY z^-1"]
    nodeDelay2 --> nodeDelayP["DELAY z^-1 ... x(n-p)"]
    nodeDelay1 --> nodeMulA1["MULTIPLY a_p(1)"]
    nodeDelay2 --> nodeMulA2["MULTIPLY a_p(2)"]
    nodeDelayP --> nodeMulAp["MULTIPLY a_p(p)"]
    nodeMulA1 --> nodeSum
    nodeMulA2 --> nodeSum
    nodeMulAp --> nodeSum
    nodeSum --> nodeErr["PREDICTION ERROR e_f_p(n)"]
    nodeErr --> nodeOut["OUTPUT e_f_p(n)"]
    nodeIn --> nodeOut
```

**Interpretation**:
- The $p$ delays create a tapped delay line holding $\{x(n-1), x(n-2), \dots, x(n-p)\}$.
- Each tap is multiplied by the corresponding forward coefficient $a_p(k)$.
- The summation produces $\hat{x}_p^{f}(n) = -\sum a_p(k) x(n-k)$ with the sign convention that positive coefficients with the negative sign in the formula appear as additions here.
- The error $e_p^{f}(n)$ is the actual minus predicted, available as the output.

## 4.2 Backward Linear Prediction Filter — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeBx["INPUT x(n)"] --> nodeBD1["DELAY z^-1"]
    nodeBx --> nodeBsum["SUMMATION JUNCTION"]
    nodeBD1 --> nodeBtap1["x(n-1)"]
    nodeBtap1 --> nodeBtap2["x(n-2)"]
    nodeBtap2 --> nodeBtapP["... x(n-p+1)"]
    nodeBx --> nodeBtapP0["x(n)"]
    nodeBtapP0 --> nodeBmul1["MULTIPLY a_p(1)"]
    nodeBtap1 --> nodeBmul2["MULTIPLY a_p(2)"]
    nodeBtapP --> nodeBmulp["MULTIPLY a_p(p)"]
    nodeBmul1 --> nodeBsum
    nodeBmul2 --> nodeBsum
    nodeBmulp --> nodeBsum
    nodeBsum --> nodeBerr["BACKWARD ERROR e_b_p(n)"]
    nodeBerr --> nodeBout["OUTPUT"]
    nodeBx --> nodeBerr
```

**Interpretation**:
- The backward predictor uses the **most recent $p$ samples** $\{x(n), x(n-1), \dots, x(n-p+1)\}$.
- It attempts to predict $x(n-p)$, the sample at the far end of the delay line.
- The error is $e_p^{b}(n) = x(n-p) + \sum a_p(k) x(n-p+k)$.

## 4.3 The Lattice Filter Realization — Where Forward Meets Backward

The forward and backward predictors are the two halves of the **lattice filter**, which is the KTU-favorite structure connecting LP to practical implementations:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeX0["f_0 n = x n"] --> nodeM1["STAGE 1 MULTIPLY Gamma_1"]
    nodeX0 --> nodeB1["B1: f_0 n minus Gamma_1 b_0 n-1"]
    nodeB0in["b_0 n-1 = x n-1"] --> nodeB1
    nodeM1 --> nodeF1["F1: f_0 n multiplied by Gamma_1"]
    nodeF1 --> nodeB1
    nodeB1 --> nodeF2["f_1 n OUTPUT STAGE 1"]
    nodeB1 --> nodeB1out["b_1 n OUTPUT STAGE 1"]
    nodeF2 --> nodeStage2["STAGE 2"]
    nodeB1out --> nodeStage2
    nodeStage2 --> nodeFp["f_p n FINAL FORWARD ERROR"]
    nodeStage2 --> nodeBp["b_p n FINAL BACKWARD ERROR"]
```

**Reading the diagram**:
- Stage $m$ uses one reflection coefficient $\Gamma_m$.
- Two signals flow: $f_m(n)$ (forward residual) and $b_m(n)$ (backward residual).
- **Stage update equations**:
  - $f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + \Gamma_m \, b_{m-1}(n-1)$
  - $b_m(n) = b_{m-1}(n-1) + \Gamma_m \, f_{m-1}(n)$
- Initial conditions: $f_0(n) = b_0(n) = x(n)$.
- **Output**: $e^{f}_{p}(n) = f_p(n)$ (forward) and $e^{b}_{p}(n) = b_p(n)$ (backward).

> [!NOTE]
> **Lattice Properties (KTU High-Yield)**
> 1. **Modular**: Each stage is independent, parameterized by one $\Gamma_m$.
> 2. **Order-recursive**: Can truncate at any $p$ to obtain a lower-order predictor.
> 3. **Bounded-input stability**: Guaranteed if $\vert \Gamma_m \vert < 1$ for all $m$.
> 4. **Symmetric**: Forward and backward sections share the same coefficients.

## 4.4 Functional Signal Flow — End-to-End LP System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeSrc["SOURCE SIGNAL x n"] --> nodeAna["LP ANALYSIS BLOCK"]
    nodeAna --> nodeCoeff["LPC COEFFICIENTS a_p k"]
    nodeAna --> nodeRes["RESIDUAL e_f_p n"]
    nodeCoeff --> nodeCodec["QUANTIZATION AND CODING"]
    nodeRes --> nodeCodec
    nodeCodec --> nodeChan["TRANSMISSION CHANNEL OR STORAGE"]
    nodeChan --> nodeDec["DECODER"]
    nodeDec --> nodeDecCoeff["DECODED LPC a_p k"]
    nodeDec --> nodeDecRes["DECODED RESIDUAL e_hat"]
    nodeDecCoeff --> nodeSyn["LPC SYNTHESIS FILTER 1 OVER A_p z"]
    nodeDecRes --> nodeSyn
    nodeSyn --> nodeXhat["RECONSTRUCTED x_hat n"]
```

**Interpretation**: This is the **complete LPC vocoder** architecture. The analysis block computes forward LP coefficients and residual; the synthesis filter $1/A_p(z)$ is the all-pole model excited by the (quantized) residual.

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Q: Define a forward linear prediction filter. Write the expression for the forward prediction error for a $p$-th order predictor.**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

A forward linear prediction filter of order $p$ predicts the current sample $x(n)$ as a linear combination of the $p$ immediately preceding samples.

**Prediction equation**:
$$\hat{x}^{f}_{p}(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, x(n-k)$$

**Prediction error**:
$$e^{f}_{p}(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}^{f}_{p}(n) = x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, x(n-k)$$

The coefficients $\{a_p(k)\}$ are chosen to minimize $E\{(e^{f}_p(n))^{2}\}$, yielding the **Yule-Walker normal equations**:
$$r_x(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_p(k) \, r_x(m-k) = 0, \quad m = 1, \dots, p$$

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for error expression, 1 mark for mentioning the Yule-Walker minimization goal)*

---

### Question A2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Q: What is a backward linear predictor? How is it related to the forward linear predictor for a WSS random process?**

**Model Answer (3 marks)**:

A backward linear predictor of order $p$ predicts the sample $x(n-p)$ (the oldest in a $p+1$-length window) using the $p$ most recent samples.

**Backward prediction error**:
$$e^{b}_{p}(n) = x(n-p) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{b}_{p}(k) \, x(n-p+k)$$

**Relationship to Forward Predictor**:

For a **real, wide-sense stationary (WSS)** random process, the autocorrelation sequence is even: $r_x(k) = r_x(-k)$. Minimizing the MSE for both predictors leads to **identical normal equations**. Therefore:

$$a^{b}_{p}(k) = a^{f}_{p}(k) = a_{p}(k) \quad \text{for all } k = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

This equality is the foundation of the **lattice filter** realization and the **Levinson-Durbin** algorithm.

*(1 mark for backward prediction definition, 1 mark for backward error expression, 1 mark for the WSS equality result)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question B-A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a) Derive the forward normal equations for a $p$-th order linear predictor from the principle of minimum mean square error. (7 marks)**

**(b) Define the backward prediction error. Using the autocorrelation properties of a WSS process, prove that the optimum backward predictor coefficients are identical to the forward predictor coefficients. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The forward prediction estimate is:
$$\hat{x}^{f}_{p}(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, x(n-k)$$

Prediction error:
$$e^{f}_{p}(n) = x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) \, x(n-k)$$

**Mean Squared Error (MSE)**:
$$J_{p} = E\left\{\left(x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) x(n-k)\right)^{2}\right\}$$

**Expanding**:
$$J_{p} = r_{x}(0) + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) r_{x}(k) + \sum_{k=1}^{p}\sum_{j=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) a_{p}(j) r_{x}(k-j)$$

**Differentiate** with respect to $a_{p}(m)$ and equate to zero:
$$\frac{\partial J_{p}}{\partial a_{p}(m)} = 2 r_{x}(m) + 2 \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) r_{x}(m-k) = 0$$

**Forward Normal Equations (Yule-Walker)**:
$$\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) r_{x}(m-k) = -r_{x}(m), \quad m = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

**Minimum MSE**: Substituting the optimum back, using $E\{e_p(n) x(n-m)\} = 0$ for $m = 1..p$:
$$J_{p,\min} = r_{x}(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) r_{x}(k)$$

*(4 marks for setup, 2 marks for differentiation, 1 mark for minimum MSE)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Backward prediction error**:
$$e^{b}_{p}(n) = x(n-p) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{b}_{p}(k) x(n-p+k)$$

**MSE**: $J^{b}_{p} = E\{(e^{b}_{p})^{2}\}$

**Differentiate** w.r.t. $a^{b}_{p}(m)$ for $m = 1..p$ and set to zero:
$$\frac{\partial J^{b}_{p}}{\partial a^{b}_{p}(m)} = 2 E\{e^{b}_{p}(n) x(n-p+m)\} = 0$$

**Expanding** using $E\{x(n-p) x(n-p+m)\} = r_x(m)$ and $E\{x(n-p+k) x(n-p+m)\} = r_x(m-k)$:
$$r_{x}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{b}_{p}(k) r_{x}(m-k) = 0, \quad m = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

**Comparison with Forward Normal Equations**: The forward equations are
$$r_{x}(m) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a^{f}_{p}(k) r_{x}(m-k) = 0, \quad m = 1, 2, \dots, p$$

Since the autocorrelation $r_x(k)$ is **even** for a real WSS process: $r_x(k) = r_x(-k)$, the matrix $\mathbf{R}$ of autocorrelations is **symmetric positive-definite**, and therefore has a **unique** solution. The two systems are **identical**, so:
$$a^{b}_{p}(k) = a^{f}_{p}(k) \quad \forall k = 1, \dots, p$$

Hence the forward and backward predictors share the **same optimum coefficients**, differing only in the error signal they produce: $e^{f}_p(n)$ is "looking forward in time," $e^{b}_p(n)$ is "looking backward in time."

*(2 marks for backward error setup, 3 marks for derivation, 2 marks for symmetry proof)*

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a) With the help of a block diagram, explain the forward linear prediction filter. Define the prediction error filter. (7 marks)**

**(b) Explain the lattice filter realization of the linear prediction filter. Show how the reflection coefficients relate forward and backward errors. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a):

A **forward linear prediction filter** of order $p$ produces a linear estimate $\hat{x}^{f}_{p}(n)$ of $x(n)$ using a weighted combination of the $p$ past samples.

**Prediction equation**:
$$\hat{x}^{f}_{p}(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) x(n-k)$$

**Block diagram description** (see Section 4.1 for the full Mermaid schematic):
- A **tapped delay line** with $p$ unit delays $z^{-1}$ stores $\{x(n-1), x(n-2), \dots, x(n-p)\}$.
- Each tap is multiplied by a coefficient $a_p(k)$ and summed.
- The summation node output is $\hat{x}^{f}_{p}(n)$ (with sign convention).
- The error is computed as $e^{f}_{p}(n) = x(n) - \hat{x}^{f}_{p}(n)$.

**Prediction Error Filter (PEF)**: An FIR filter with transfer function:
$$A_{p}(z) = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_{p}(k) z^{-k}$$

It is the cascade of the forward predictor in the form of an FIR filter — the **all-zero inverse** of the AR(p) generator. For an AR input process, the PEF output is **white noise** with variance equal to $J_{p,\min}$.

**Filter characteristics**:
- Order: $p$ (FIR with $p+1$ taps including the unity tap).
- Structure: Direct-form FIR with $p$ multipliers and $p$ adders.
- Stability: Always stable (FIR with finite impulse response).
- Application: Spectral whitening, speech residual extraction, LPC analysis.

*(3 marks for block diagram, 2 marks for equations, 2 marks for PEF properties)*

#### Model Solution for (b):

The **lattice filter** is an order-recursive realization of the LP filter, where each stage is parameterized by a single **reflection coefficient** $\Gamma_m$.

**Block diagram**: See Section 4.3 (Mermaid lattice flow).

**Stage-by-stage update equations** (for stage $m$):
$$f_{m}(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + \Gamma_{m} \, b_{m-1}(n-1)$$
$$b_{m}(n) = b_{m-1}(n-1) + \Gamma_{m} \, f_{m-1}(n)$$

**Initial conditions**:
$$f_{0}(n) = b_{0}(n) = x(n)$$

**Final outputs**:
- Forward prediction error: $e^{f}_{p}(n) = f_{p}(n)$
- Backward prediction error: $e^{b}_{p}(n) = b_{p}(n)$

**Reflection coefficient derivation**: The reflection coefficient is the **bridge** between order $p$ and $p-1$ predictors:

$$\Gamma_{p+1} = -\frac{\Delta_{p}}{J_{p,\min}}$$

where $\Delta_{p} = E\{f_{p}(n) \, b_{p}(n-1)\}$ is the **cross-correlation** between forward and backward errors.

**Order-update relation** for coefficients:
$$a_{p}(k) = a_{p-1}(k) + \Gamma_{p} \, a_{p-1}(p-k) \quad \text{for } k = 1, \dots, p-1$$
$$a_{p}(p) = \Gamma_{p}$$

**Minimum MSE update**:
$$J_{p,\min} = J_{p-1,\min} \left(1 - \Gamma_{p}^{2}\right)$$

**Stability condition**: $\vert \Gamma_{m} \vert < 1$ for all $m = 1, \dots, p$, which is equivalent to the autocorrelation matrix being positive-definite.

**Why lattice is preferred**:
- $O(p)$ computation per sample (vs. $O(p)$ for direct form, but lattice allows incremental order updates).
- Robust to coefficient quantization (each stage can be designed independently).
- Used in every modern speech codec (e.g., 3GPP AMR-WB, EVS, Opus).

*(3 marks for lattice diagram and equations, 2 marks for reflection coefficient definition, 2 marks for order-update and stability)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Sign Convention Trap**: Board evaluators deduct **1 mark** if you write $\hat{x}(n) = +\sum a_k x(n-k)$ instead of the standard negative convention. Always use the negative sign.
> 2. **Order-of-Equation Mistake**: In the Yule-Walker equation, the index $m$ runs from $1$ to $p$, **not** $0$ to $p$. The case $m = 0$ gives the **minimum MSE**, not the normal equation. Mixing these two costs **2 marks**.
> 3. **Autocorrelation Symmetry**: Many students forget to mention that $r_x(k)$ is **even** when proving forward = backward. This is the key step — without it, the proof is incomplete and loses **1.5 marks**.
> 4. **Reflection Coefficient Bound**: Writing $\Gamma_p = a_p(p)$ without stating the **stability condition** $\vert \Gamma_p \vert < 1$ costs **0.5 mark** in Part-A questions and **1 mark** in Part-B derivations.
> 5. **Confusing Forward and Backward Errors**: A common error is to write $e^{b}_p(n) = x(n) + \sum a_k x(n-k)$ (forward form). Remember: backward uses the **most recent** samples to predict the **oldest**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Linear Prediction Filters (Forward & Backward)**

- **Definition**: A linear predictor of order $p$ estimates a sample as a linear combination of $p$ past samples; the difference is the prediction error.
- **Sign Convention**: Always $\hat{x}(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_p(k) x(n-k)$. The negative sign is essential.
- **Cost Function**: Mean Squared Error (MSE) $J_p = E\{(e_p(n))^{2}\}$, minimized over the coefficients.
- **Orthogonality Principle**: At the optimum, the prediction error is **orthogonal** to every sample used in prediction: $E\{e_p(n) x(n-m)\} = 0$ for $m = 1, \dots, p$.
- **Forward Normal Equations (Yule-Walker)**: $\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_p(k) r_x(m-k) = -r_x(m)$ for $m = 1, \dots, p$.
- **Minimum Forward MSE**: $J_{p,\min} = r_x(0) + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_p(k) r_x(k)$.
- **Backward Predictor**: Estimates $x(n-p)$ from the $p$ most recent samples; the same coefficients work for both.
- **Forward = Backward Coefficients** (WSS, real process): $a^{f}_p(k) = a^{b}_p(k) = a_p(k)$.
- **Cross-Correlation** $\Delta_p = E\{e^{f}_p(n) e^{b}_p(n-1)\}$ is the bridge term in the Levinson-Durbin recursion.
- **Reflection Coefficient**: $\Gamma_{p+1} = -\Delta_p / J_{p,\min}$; bounded: $\vert \Gamma_{m} \vert < 1$ for all $m$.
- **Order-Update**: $a_p(k) = a_{p-1}(k) + \Gamma_p a_{p-1}(p-k)$; the new tap is $a_p(p) = \Gamma_p$.
- **MSE Update**: $J_{p,\min} = J_{p-1,\min}(1 - \Gamma_p^{2})$ — non-negative and decreasing with $p$.
- **Prediction Error Filter**: $A_p(z) = 1 + \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_p(k) z^{-k}$ — all-zero FIR that **whitens** an AR input.
- **Lattice Realization**: Each stage $m$ uses one $\Gamma_m$; updates $f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + \Gamma_m b_{m-1}(n-1)$ and $b_m(n) = b_{m-1}(n-1) + \Gamma_m f_{m-1}(n)$.
- **Whitening Property**: If $x(n)$ is AR(p) with parameters $a_p(k)$, then $A_p(z) X(z)$ is white noise of variance $J_{p,\min}$.
- **Applications**: LPC vocoders, CELP speech codecs, spectral estimation, biomedical compression, channel equalization.
- **Computational Complexity**: Levinson-Durbin solves the Yule-Walker system in $O(p^{2})$ (Gaussian elimination would be $O(p^{3})$).
- **KTU Board Hot Topics**: Orthogonality principle, Yule-Walker derivation, forward-backward symmetry, reflection coefficient boundedness, lattice filter structure, $J_{p,\min}$ decrease with $p$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
