<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# MODULE 4 — LATTICE FILTER STRUCTURE

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned):**
> The **Lattice Filter Structure** is a modular, recursive realization of digital filters (both FIR and IIR) in which the signal propagates through a cascade of identical stages. Each stage is governed by a single parameter called the **Reflection Coefficient** (also called **PARCOR coefficient** or **Kumar coefficient**), denoted $k_m$, where $m$ is the stage index. It is derived directly from the theory of **linear prediction** and is mathematically equivalent to the direct-form realization.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Echoing Staircase"**
> Imagine shouting into a long stairwell. Your voice reaches the wall, reflects, comes back, and meets another echo, then reflects again. Each "bounce" of sound is analogous to one **stage** of the lattice filter. The **reflection coefficient** $k_m$ is like the *acoustic reflectivity* of the wall at bounce number $m$. A hard wall ($k_m \approx 1$) reflects almost all sound; an absorbing wall ($k_m \approx 0$) lets it pass. By stacking many such "acoustic walls," you can synthesize any desired echo pattern — exactly like how stacking lattice stages synthesizes any FIR/IIR filter response.

### 1.3 Why Lattice Structures Matter

| Property | Direct Form | Lattice Form |
|---|---|---|
| **Sensitivity** to coefficient quantization | High | Very Low |
| **Modularity** | Monolithic | Stage-by-stage buildable |
| **Stability Check** | Must check roots of $A(z)$ | Just check $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ |
| **Order Update** | Full recalculation | Easy $M \to M+1$ extension |
| **Numerical Robustness** | Poor for high order | Excellent for high order |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** The lattice structure is the *preferred* realization for adaptive filters (e.g., the **Gradient Adaptive Lattice (GAL)** and **Least-Squares Lattice (LSL)** algorithms) because its stage-by-stage decoupling allows independent, order-recursive adaptive updates.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Building Blocks — Linear Prediction Errors

The lattice structure is fundamentally a cascade of **forward** and **backward** linear predictors operating on the same data. Let us define these rigorously.

### 2.1.1 Forward Prediction Error $f_m(n)$

A **forward linear predictor** of order $m$ attempts to predict the *current* sample $x(n)$ from the $m$ past samples. The **forward prediction error** is the prediction residual:

$$
f_m(n) = x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} a_m(k)\, x(n-k)
$$

where $a_m(k)$ are the optimal **forward predictor coefficients** that minimize the mean-square forward prediction error $E[\vert f_m(n) \vert^2]$.

### 2.1.2 Backward Prediction Error $g_m(n)$

A **backward linear predictor** of order $m$ attempts to predict the *past* sample $x(n-m)$ from the $m$ future samples $x(n), x(n-1), \dots, x(n-m+1)$. The **backward prediction error** is:

$$
g_m(n) = x(n-m) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} a_m^{*}(m-k)\, x(n-k)
$$

For **real-valued** signals (the KTU 2024 default), the complex conjugation $a_m^{*}$ reduces to a simple $a_m$.

### 2.1.3 Stage Recursion (The Heart of the Lattice)

The defining recursion that converts one lattice stage to the next:

$$
f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m\, g_{m-1}(n-1)
$$

$$
g_m(n) = k_m^{*}\, f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)
$$

with initial conditions:

$$
f_0(n) = g_0(n) = x(n)
$$

### 2.2 Reflection Coefficient (PARCOR Coefficient) $k_m$

The coefficient $k_m$ is the **partial correlation** between $f_{m-1}(n)$ and $g_{m-1}(n-1)$ — i.e., the correlation between the forward and backward errors entering stage $m$:

$$
k_m = \frac{-E[\, f_{m-1}(n)\, g_{m-1}^{*}(n-1) \,]}{E[\,\vert g_{m-1}(n-1) \vert^2\,]}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stability Constraint:** For an **all-pole (IIR)** filter realized via lattice, stability demands
> $$\vert k_m \vert < 1 \quad \text{for all } m = 1, 2, \dots, M$$
> This is the **single most important condition** tested in KTU examinations.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

| # | Formula | Meaning / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $f_0(n) = g_0(n) = x(n)$ | Initial conditions |
| 2 | $f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m\, g_{m-1}(n-1)$ | Forward recursion |
| 3 | $g_m(n) = k_m^{*}\, f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)$ | Backward recursion |
| 4 | $a_m(k) = a_{m-1}(k) + k_m\, a_{m-1}^{*}(m-k)$ | Direct ↔ Lattice conversion |
| 5 | $k_m = -a_m(m)$ | $m$-th reflection coefficient equals negative of last predictor tap |
| 6 | $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ | **Stability** of IIR lattice |
| 7 | $P_m = (1 - \vert k_m \vert^{2})\, P_{m-1}$ | Forward error power recursion (Levinson–Durbin) |
| 8 | $H(z) = \dfrac{1}{A_M(z)}$ | All-pole transfer function (IIR lattice) |
| 9 | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} c_k\, g_k(n)$ | Ladder output for pole-zero lattice |
| 10 | $H(z) = \dfrac{C(z)}{A_M(z)}$ | Pole-zero transfer function |

> [!WARNING]
> **Pipe-Character Note:** All absolute-value notations use $\vert \cdot \vert$ (LaTeX vertical bar), **never** the markdown table pipe `|`, to preserve table integrity.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Speech Processing:** Lattice filters are the workhorse for **LPC (Linear Predictive Coding)** vocoders (e.g., GSM, LPC-10).
- **Adaptive Echo Cancellation:** The **Normalized Lattice** adapts one stage at a time, giving $O(M)$ instead of $O(M^2)$ complexity.
- **Channel Equalization:** Robustness to coefficient quantization makes them ideal for high-speed digital receivers.
- **Spectral Estimation:** Burg's method uses lattice reflection coefficients for maximum-entropy spectral estimation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Lattice Recursions (From First Principles)

**Goal:** Starting from the $m$-th order forward prediction error filter, derive the lattice update equations.

### Step 1 — Define Forward and Backward Predictors of Order $m$

The optimal forward predictor coefficients $\{a_m(k)\}_{k=1}^{m}$ are obtained by minimizing the mean-square forward prediction error:

$$
P_m^{f} = E[\,\vert f_m(n) \vert^2\,] = E\!\left[\,\Big\vert x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} a_m(k)\, x(n-k) \Big\vert^{2}\,\right]
$$

Setting $\partial P_m^{f} / \partial a_m^{*}(j) = 0$ yields the **Wiener–Hopf normal equations**:

$$
\sum_{k=1}^{m} a_m(k)\, R(k-j) = -R(j), \quad j = 1, 2, \dots, m
$$

where $R(k) = E[\,x(n)\,x^{*}(n-k)\,]$ is the autocorrelation.

### Step 2 — Write $f_m(n)$ and $g_m(n)$ Using Stage-$(m-1)$ Errors

Using the **Levinson–Durbin step-up** identity:

$$
a_m(k) = a_{m-1}(k) + k_m\, a_{m-1}(m-k), \quad k = 1, 2, \dots, m-1
$$

and the boundary condition $a_m(m) = k_m$.

Substitute this into the forward error expression:

$$
\begin{aligned}
f_m(n) &= x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} a_m(k)\, x(n-k) \\
&= x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1}\big[a_{m-1}(k) + k_m\, a_{m-1}(m-k)\big]\, x(n-k) + k_m\, x(n-m) \\
&= \Big[x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1}(k)\, x(n-k)\Big] \\
&\quad + k_m \Big[x(n-m) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1}(m-k)\, x(n-k)\Big]
\end{aligned}
$$

The bracketed first term is $f_{m-1}(n)$. The bracketed second term is $g_{m-1}(n-1)$.

> **Logic note:** The variable substitution $k \to m-k$ inside the second sum aligns the lags so that they read backwards from $n-1$, which is precisely the backward error definition shifted by one sample.

Therefore:

$$
\boxed{\,f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m\, g_{m-1}(n-1)\,}
$$

### Step 3 — Derive the Backward Recursion

By symmetry (and direct substitution into the $g_m(n)$ definition, using the same step-up identity):

$$
\begin{aligned}
g_m(n) &= x(n-m) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} a_m^{*}(m-k)\, x(n-k) \\
&= x(n-m) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1}^{*}(m-k)\, x(n-k) \\
&\quad + k_m^{*} \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1}^{*}(k)\, x(n-k) + k_m^{*}\, x(n) \\
&= g_{m-1}(n) + k_m^{*}\Big[x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1}(k)\, x(n-k)\Big] \\
&= g_{m-1}(n) + k_m^{*}\, f_{m-1}(n)
\end{aligned}
$$

This gives the second lattice equation:

$$
\boxed{\,g_m(n) = k_m^{*}\, f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)\,}
$$

(Note the shift: $g_{m-1}(n-1)$ arises because the forward predictor uses $x(n-1)$ as its "current" sample.)

### Step 4 — Initial Conditions

For $m = 0$, the predictor has no taps, so $f_0(n) = g_0(n) = x(n)$. These initialize the cascade.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard Problem)

**Problem:** Convert the FIR filter with direct-form coefficients $a_1 = -0.5$, $a_2 = 0.3$ into lattice form. Verify $\vert k_m \vert < 1$.

### Step 1 — Identify the predictor order

The predictor has order $M = 2$. We seek $\{k_1, k_2\}$.

### Step 2 — Extract $k_2$ (highest stage first)

From the relation $k_m = -a_m(m)$:

$$
k_2 = -a_2(2) = -0.3
$$

### Step 3 — Step-down (Reverse Levinson–Durbin) to obtain $a_1(k)$

Using:

$$
a_{m-1}(k) = \frac{a_m(k) - k_m\, a_m(m-k)}{1 - \vert k_m \vert^{2}}, \quad k = 1, \dots, m-1
$$

Substitute $m = 2$, $k_2 = -0.3$, $a_2(1) = -0.5$, $a_2(2) = 0.3$:

$$
1 - \vert k_2 \vert^{2} = 1 - 0.09 = 0.91
$$

$$
a_1(1) = \frac{a_2(1) - k_2\, a_2(1)}{0.91} = \frac{-0.5 - (-0.3)(-0.5)}{0.91} = \frac{-0.5 - 0.15}{0.91} = \frac{-0.65}{0.91} \approx -0.7143
$$

### Step 4 — Extract $k_1$

$$
k_1 = -a_1(1) = 0.7143
$$

### Step 5 — Stability Check

$$
\vert k_1 \vert = 0.7143 < 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

$$
\vert k_2 \vert = 0.3 < 1 \quad \checkmark
$$

The system is **stable** as an all-pole realization.

### Summary Table

| Stage $m$ | $a_m(1)$ | $a_m(2)$ | $k_m$ | $\vert k_m \vert$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $-0.7143$ | — | $0.7143$ | $0.7143 < 1$ ✓ |
| 2 | $-0.5$ | $0.3$ | $-0.3$ | $0.3 < 1$ ✓ |

---

## 3.3 IIR (All-Pole) Lattice — Recurrence for $x(n)$

For an all-pole system governed by:

$$
x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{M} a^{*}(k)\, x(n-k) + w(n)
$$

the lattice output recursion is derived by *inverting* the forward recursion:

$$
\boxed{\,f_{m-1}(n) = f_m(n) - k_m^{*}\, g_{m-1}(n-1)\,}
$$

Combined with $g_m(n) = k_m^{*}\, f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)$, this forms a computable two-stage loop at every lattice index $m = M, M-1, \dots, 1$, with terminal condition $f_M(n) = w(n)$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid — FIR Lattice Predictor (Block Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inX["Input x(n)"]:::ioNode
    s0["Stage 0 f0 g0 equal to x(n)"]:::stg
    s1["Stage 1 k1 mult and add f1 g1"]:::stg
    s2["Stage 2 k2 mult and add f2 g2"]:::stg
    sM["Stage M kM mult and add fM gM"]:::stg
    outF["Output fM n forward error"]:::ioNode
    outG["Output gM n backward error"]:::ioNode

    inX --> s0
    s0 --> s1
    s1 --> s2
    s2 --> sM
    sM --> outF
    sM --> outG

    classDef ioNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stg fill:#B6D7FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading guide:** Each colored stage represents one lattice section characterized by a single scalar $k_m$. The signals $f_m(n)$ and $g_m(n)$ flow top-down through the cascade, with cross-coupling of $f \leftrightarrow g$ at every stage.

## 4.2 Mermaid — Single Lattice Stage (Internal Signal Routing)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    fPrev["f sub m-1 of n"]:::sig
    gPrev["g sub m-1 of n minus 1"]:::sig
    mulA["k_m multiply"]:::op
    mulB["k_m conjugate multiply"]:::op
    sumA["Adder: fPrev plus mulA"]:::op
    sumB["Adder: mulB plus gPrev"]:::op
    fOut["f sub m of n"]:::sig
    gOut["g sub m of n"]:::sig

    fPrev --> sumA
    gPrev --> mulA
    mulA --> sumA
    sumA --> fOut
    fPrev --> mulB
    mulB --> sumB
    gPrev --> sumB
    sumB --> gOut

    classDef sig fill:#E0FFE0,stroke:#060,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef op fill:#FFE0E0,stroke:#900,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading guide:** This is one lattice section. Note the two multipliers ($k_m$ and $k_m^{*}$), two adders, and the delay of $g_{m-1}(n-1)$ — a single unit-delay element.

## 4.3 Mermaid — IIR (All-Pole) Lattice Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    wIn["Excitation w n"]:::ioNode
    fmOut["Output x n equals fM n"]:::ioNode
    gmTap["Backward errors g0 g1 dots gM feed ladder taps"]:::tap
    sM["Stage M kM"]:::stg
    sMsub1["Stage M-1 kM-1"]:::stg
    s1["Stage 1 k1"]:::stg
    s0["Stage 0"]:::stg

    wIn --> sM
    sM --> sMsub1
    sMsub1 --> s1
    s1 --> s0
    s0 --> fmOut
    s0 --> gmTap

    classDef ioNode fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef stg fill:#B6D7FF,stroke:#003366,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef tap fill:#E5CCFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
```

**Reading guide:** The signal flows from the excitation $w(n)$ *backwards* through the stages (this is the inverted recursion of Section 3.3), producing the actual output $x(n)$ at the final stage. The backward-error taps $g_m(n)$ are simultaneously available for use in a *lattice-ladder* configuration when a pole-zero response is needed.

## 4.4 Component Count Summary

| Component | Per Stage | Total for Order $M$ |
|---|---|---|
| Multipliers ($k_m$ and $k_m^{*}$) | 2 | $2M$ |
| Adders | 2 | $2M$ |
| Unit Delays | 1 (on $g$ path) | $M$ |
| **Total Memory** | — | **$M$ delays** |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Define the term "reflection coefficient" in a lattice filter. State the condition for stability of an all-pole lattice filter.**

**Model Answer:**
> The reflection coefficient $k_m$ at the $m$-th stage of a lattice filter is the **partial correlation coefficient** between the forward prediction error $f_{m-1}(n)$ and the delayed backward prediction error $g_{m-1}(n-1)$ at that stage. It governs the strength of cross-coupling between the two error signal paths.
>
> **Stability Condition:** For the all-pole (IIR) lattice realization to be stable, the magnitudes of all reflection coefficients must be strictly less than unity:
> $$\vert k_m \vert < 1 \quad \text{for } m = 1, 2, \dots, M$$
> If any $\vert k_m \vert \geq 1$, the lattice represents an unstable system.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Differentiate between the FIR lattice predictor and the IIR (all-pole) lattice structure in terms of signal flow direction.**

**Model Answer:**
> - **FIR Lattice Predictor:** Signal flows *forward* through the stages from the input $x(n)$, with each stage producing higher-order forward $f_m(n)$ and backward $g_m(n)$ prediction errors. Output is typically the final forward error $f_M(n)$.
> - **IIR (All-Pole) Lattice:** Signal flows *backward* (from excitation $w(n)$ through stages $M, M-1, \dots, 1$ to the output $x(n)$). The forward recursion is mathematically inverted to compute $f_{m-1}(n)$ from $f_m(n)$ and $g_{m-1}(n-1)$.
> - **Direction Inversion:** FIR = forward computation; IIR = backward (inverse) computation along the same stage topology.

---

## PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [14 Marks] — Lattice from Direct-Form FIR

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**Derive the lattice filter structure for an FIR predictor of order $M$, starting from the definition of forward and backward prediction errors. Clearly state the stage-update recursions and the initial conditions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Define Forward Prediction Error of Order $m$:**
$$
f_m(n) = x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} a_m(k)\, x(n-k)
$$

**Step 2 — Define Backward Prediction Error of Order $m$:**
$$
g_m(n) = x(n-m) + \sum_{k=1}^{m} a_m(m-k)\, x(n-k)
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Levinson–Durbin Step-Up:**
$$
a_m(k) = a_{m-1}(k) + k_m\, a_{m-1}(m-k), \quad 1 \leq k \leq m-1
$$
with $a_m(m) = k_m$.

**Step 4 — Substitute into $f_m(n)$:**
$$
\begin{aligned}
f_m(n) &= x(n) + \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1}(k) x(n-k) + k_m \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1}(m-k) x(n-k) + k_m x(n-m) \\
&= f_{m-1}(n) + k_m\, g_{m-1}(n-1)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into $g_m(n)$ and similarly derive:**
$$
g_m(n) = k_m^{*}\, f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)
$$

**Step 6 — State Initial Conditions:**
$$
f_0(n) = g_0(n) = x(n)
$$

**Conclusion:** These recursions, when cascaded over $m = 1, 2, \dots, M$, form the FIR lattice predictor with $M$ stages, each parameterized by a single reflection coefficient $k_m$. **(7 marks fully awarded with clear block diagram referenced.)**

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**For an FIR filter with coefficients $a_1 = 1.2$, $a_2 = -0.5$, $a_3 = 0.1$, compute the lattice reflection coefficients $k_1, k_2, k_3$ and verify stability.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $a_3(1) = 1.2$, $a_3(2) = -0.5$, $a_3(3) = 0.1$, $M = 3$.

**Step 1 — Compute $k_3$:**
$$
k_3 = a_3(3) = 0.1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert k_3 \vert = 0.1 < 1 \checkmark
$$

**Step 2 — Step-Down to Order 2:**
$$
1 - \vert k_3 \vert^{2} = 1 - 0.01 = 0.99
$$

$$
a_2(1) = \frac{a_3(1) - k_3\, a_3(2)}{0.99} = \frac{1.2 - (0.1)(-0.5)}{0.99} = \frac{1.2 + 0.05}{0.99} = \frac{1.25}{0.99} \approx 1.2626
$$

$$
a_2(2) = \frac{a_3(2) - k_3\, a_3(1)}{0.99} = \frac{-0.5 - (0.1)(1.2)}{0.99} = \frac{-0.62}{0.99} \approx -0.6263
$$

**Step 3 — Compute $k_2$:**
$$
k_2 = a_2(2) \approx -0.6263 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \vert k_2 \vert \approx 0.6263 < 1 \checkmark
$$

**Step 4 — Step-Down to Order 1:**
$$
1 - \vert k_2 \vert^{2} = 1 - 0.3922 = 0.6078
$$

$$
a_1(1) = \frac{a_2(1) - k_2\, a_2(1)}{0.6078} = \frac{a_2(1)(1 - k_2)}{0.6078} = \frac{1.2626(1 + 0.6263)}{0.6078} = \frac{1.2626 \times 1.6263}{0.6078} \approx 3.379
$$

**Step 5 — Compute $k_1$:**
$$
k_1 = a_1(1) \approx 3.379
$$

**Step 6 — Stability Check:**
$$
\vert k_1 \vert = 3.379 > 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{UNSTABLE!}
$$

**Conclusion:** Although the direct-form coefficients appear innocuous, the lattice conversion reveals $\vert k_1 \vert > 1$, exposing **hidden instability** — a key advantage of the lattice diagnostic.

---

### Question B [14 Marks] — IIR Lattice & Levinson–Durbin

#### Part (a) [7 Marks]
**With the aid of a block-level functional flow diagram, explain the IIR (all-pole) lattice structure. Derive the output recursion $f_{m-1}(n) = f_m(n) - k_m^{*} g_{m-1}(n-1)$.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — All-Pole System Equation:**
$$
x(n) = -\sum_{k=1}^{M} a^{*}(k)\, x(n-k) + w(n) \quad \Rightarrow \quad H(z) = \frac{1}{A_M(z)}
$$

**Step 2 — Define Forward Error at Stage $M$ as Excitation:**
$$
f_M(n) = w(n)
$$

**Step 3 — Starting Point: Forward Recursion (from Section 2):**
$$
f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m\, g_{m-1}(n-1)
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $f_{m-1}(n)$ (the unknown at the *lower* stage):**
$$
f_{m-1}(n) = f_m(n) - k_m\, g_{m-1}(n-1)
$$

For real signals (and complex in general with conjugate on $k$), this becomes:
$$
\boxed{\,f_{m-1}(n) = f_m(n) - k_m^{*}\, g_{m-1}(n-1)\,}
$$

**Step 5 — Pair with the Backward Recursion (no inversion needed):**
$$
g_m(n) = k_m^{*}\, f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)
$$

**Step 6 — Block Diagram Interpretation:**

| Block | Computation |
|---|---|
| Stage $M$ | Input $= w(n)$, output $f_{M-1}(n)$ via inversion |
| Stage $m$ | Receives $f_m(n)$ and $g_{m-1}(n-1)$, produces $f_{m-1}(n)$ and $g_m(n)$ |
| Stage $1$ | Produces $f_0(n) = x(n)$ as the **actual system output** |

The signal thus propagates from stage $M$ down to stage $1$ — **backward** relative to the FIR lattice.

#### Part (b) [7 Marks]
**State and derive the Levinson–Durbin algorithm. Show how $k_m$ and the mean-square error $P_m$ are computed recursively.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Recursion on Predictor Coefficients:**
$$
a_m(k) = a_{m-1}(k) + k_m\, a_{m-1}(m-k), \quad k = 1, \dots, m-1
$$

**Step 2 — Reflection Coefficient Update (derived from minimization of $P_m$):**
$$
k_m = -\frac{\Delta_m}{P_{m-1}}, \quad \text{where} \quad \Delta_m = -\sum_{k=0}^{m-1} a_{m-1}(k)\, R(m-k)
$$

**Step 3 — Minimum Mean-Square Error Recursion:**
$$
P_m = P_{m-1}\,(1 - \vert k_m \vert^{2})
$$

> **Logic note:** As $|k_m| \to 1$, $P_m \to 0$ (perfect prediction); as $|k_m| \to 0$, $P_m = P_{m-1}$ (no new information gained).

**Step 4 — Initialization:**
$$
a_0(0) = 1, \quad P_0 = R(0)
$$

**Step 5 — Iterative Table (typical KTU exam format):**

For each $m = 1, 2, \dots, M$:
1. Compute $\Delta_m = -\sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_{m-1}(k) R(m-k)$.
2. Compute $k_m = \Delta_m / P_{m-1}$.
3. Update $a_m(k) = a_{m-1}(k) + k_m\, a_{m-1}(m-k)$.
4. Update $P_m = (1 - k_m^{2}) P_{m-1}$.

**Step 6 — Connection to Lattice Filter:**
> The $k_m$ values computed by Levinson–Durbin are **identical** to the lattice reflection coefficients at stage $m$. Thus the Levinson–Durbin algorithm is the *analytical* counterpart of the *physical* lattice filter realization.

---

## 5.1 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Lattice-Filter Questions:**
>
> 1. **Sign Convention Error:** The convention $k_m = a_m(m)$ vs. $k_m = -a_m(m)$ varies between textbooks. **Always state your convention explicitly** at the start of the solution — KTU examiners deduct 1 mark for ambiguity.
>
> 2. **Forgetting the Conjugate:** When signals are *real*, $k_m^{*} = k_m$, but students often write the wrong sign on the multiplication term. For $g_m(n)$, the **multiplier on $f_{m-1}(n)$ is $k_m$**, not $k_m^{*}$, in the *real-valued* case (no conjugate).
>
> 3. **Stability Not Checked:** Numerical problems often end without the student verifying $\vert k_m \vert < 1$. This is a **mandatory final step** worth 1–2 marks.
>
> 4. **Confusing FIR and IIR Lattice:** The FIR lattice cascades *forward*; the IIR lattice cascades *backward* (inverted recursion). Marking schemes specifically test this distinction.
>
> 5. **Skipping the Block Diagram:** A "neat labelled block diagram" is worth **2 marks** on its own in 14-mark questions. Never omit it.

---

## 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Lattice filter** = cascade of identical stages, each governed by one **reflection coefficient** $k_m$.
- [x] **Two recursions per stage:** $f_m(n) = f_{m-1}(n) + k_m g_{m-1}(n-1)$ and $g_m(n) = k_m^{*} f_{m-1}(n) + g_{m-1}(n-1)$.
- [x] **Initial conditions:** $f_0(n) = g_0(n) = x(n)$.
- [x] **Conversion identity:** $k_m = a_m(m)$ (with consistent sign convention).
- [x] **Levinson–Durbin recursion:** $a_m(k) = a_{m-1}(k) + k_m\, a_{m-1}(m-k)$.
- [x] **Step-down (reverse) recursion:** $a_{m-1}(k) = [a_m(k) - k_m\, a_m(m-k)] / (1 - \vert k_m \vert^2)$.
- [x] **Stability:** $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ for all $m = 1, \dots, M$ in the all-pole realization.
- [x] **Mean-square error recursion:** $P_m = (1 - \vert k_m \vert^2) P_{m-1}$.
- [x] **IIR Lattice (All-Pole):** Output recursion $f_{m-1}(n) = f_m(n) - k_m^{*} g_{m-1}(n-1)$ with $f_M(n) = w(n)$.
- [x] **Pole-Zero (Lattice-Ladder):** $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M} c_k\, g_k(n)$.
- [x] **Modularity advantage:** Stages can be added one-by-one without recalculating earlier coefficients.
- [x] **Numerical robustness:** Far less sensitive to coefficient quantization than direct form.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Relationship between linear filter coefficients and reflection coefficients
Normal equations for optimum filter design. Adaptive filters - Weiner filter design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT746 - ADVANCED DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Normal equations for optimum filter design. Adaptive filters - Weiner filter design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Wiener Filter — Formal Definition

The **Wiener Filter** is the optimal linear, time-invariant (or time-varying in the adaptive sense) discrete-time filter that produces an output $y(n)$ as close as possible to a *desired* (reference) signal $d(n)$, where "close" is rigorously quantified using the **Mean Square Error (MSE)** criterion.

Formally, given an input sequence $x(n)$ and a desired response $d(n)$, the Wiener filter of order $M$ selects the tap weights $w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{M-1}$ such that the expected value of the squared error,

$$J = E\bigl[\, \vert e(n) \vert^{2}\,\bigr]$$

is **minimized**, where $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ and $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} w_k\, x(n-k)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Wiener-Hopf Equation (the "Normal Equation"):**
> The minimization of $J$ leads to the celebrated matrix equation
> $$\mathbf{R}\, \mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{p}$$
> where $\mathbf{R}$ is the $M \times M$ input autocorrelation matrix and $\mathbf{p}$ is the $M \times 1$ cross-correlation vector between $d(n)$ and $x(n)$.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Headphones" Concept

Imagine standing near a noisy road, and you want to listen to your friend speaking. The ideal noise-cancelling headphone must:
1. Measure the road noise $x(n)$ via a reference microphone.
2. Generate an *anti-noise* $y(n)$ that is the **best linear estimate** of the unwanted noise component embedded in the music/voice signal $d(n)$.
3. The "best" means the residual error after subtraction should carry the **least possible energy**.

The Wiener filter is the mathematical embodiment of this anti-noise generator. The optimal weights are analogous to the precise gains the headphone's digital signal processor must apply so that the output is a perfectly inverted replica of the noise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** The Wiener filter is **non-adaptive** in its classical form — the weights are computed once from autocorrelation statistics. The **adaptive filter** (LMS / RLS) is its real-time cousin that tracks changing statistics by continuously updating its weights from incoming data.

## 1.3 Engineering Significance

The Wiener framework underpins virtually every modern estimation problem:

- **Channel equalization** in digital communications.
- **System identification** and plant modelling in control systems.
- **Echo and noise cancellation** in speech processing.
- **Spectral estimation** and line enhancer design.
- **Beamforming** in radar and sonar arrays.

The optimum error $J_{\min}$ is a quantitative benchmark — any practical (sub-optimal) filter's MSE is compared against $J_{\min}$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Optimization Problem

Consider an FIR transversal filter of order $M$:

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} w_k\, x(n-k) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$$

where $\mathbf{w} = [w_0, w_1, \ldots, w_{M-1}]^{T}$ and $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-M+1)]^{T}$.

The **error** signal is

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n).$$

The performance function (mean-square error) is

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\bigl[\,e^{2}(n)\,\bigr].$$

Expanding,

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\bigl[d^{2}(n)\bigr] - 2\,\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}.$$

Here:
- $\sigma_d^{2} = E[d^{2}(n)]$ is the variance (power) of the desired signal.
- $\mathbf{p} = E[\,x(n)\,d(n)\,]$ is the cross-correlation vector.
- $\mathbf{R} = E[\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\,]$ is the input autocorrelation matrix.

## 2.2 The Orthogonality Principle

> [!NOTE]
> **Orthogonality Principle:** At the optimum, the error $e_{\text{opt}}(n)$ is **orthogonal** to every tap input:
> $$E\bigl[\,e_{\text{opt}}(n)\, x(n-k)\,\bigr] = 0, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1.$$
> This is the mathematical statement that no further linear combination of the input can reduce the residual error energy.

## 2.3 Deriving the Normal Equation

Setting the gradient of $J$ to zero (first-order necessary condition for unconstrained minimum):

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}.$$

Hence,

$$\boxed{\,\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{p}\,} \quad \text{(Wiener-Hopf or Normal Equation)}.$$

Provided $\mathbf{R}$ is non-singular (i.e., the input is persistently exciting),

$$\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}.$$

## 2.4 The Minimum Mean-Square Error (MMSE)

Substituting $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$ back into $J(\mathbf{w})$:

$$J_{\min} = \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}.$$

This is the irreducible residual error for any linear filter operating on the given statistics.

## 2.5 The Adaptive Filter — From Wiener to LMS

When the statistics $E[\cdot]$ are unknown or time-varying, we cannot form $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{p}$ directly. We replace them by **instantaneous estimates**:

$$\hat{\mathbf{R}}(n) \approx \mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n), \qquad \hat{\mathbf{p}}(n) \approx \mathbf{x}(n)\,d(n).$$

The steepest-descent update becomes the **LMS (Least Mean Squares) algorithm**:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n).$$

where $\mu$ is the **step-size** governing stability and convergence speed.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stability bound for LMS (in mean):**
> $$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}}$$
> where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}$.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Equation | Symbol / Unit |
|---|----------|---------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Filter output | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} w_k\, x(n-k)$ | Linear convolution |
| 2 | Error signal | $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ | Scalar |
| 3 | Performance index | $J = E[\vert e(n)\vert^{2}]$ | Power (W) |
| 4 | Quadratic form of $J$ | $J = \sigma_d^{2} - 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ | Real scalar |
| 5 | Autocorrelation matrix | $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)]$ | Symmetric, Toeplitz |
| 6 | Cross-correlation vector | $\mathbf{p} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\,d(n)]$ | Column vector |
| 7 | Normal equation (Wiener-Hopf) | $\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{p}$ | Linear system |
| 8 | Optimal weight vector | $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | $M \times 1$ vector |
| 9 | MMSE | $J_{\min} = \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | Scalar (W) |
| 10 | Orthogonality condition | $E[e_{\text{opt}}(n)\,x(n-k)] = 0,\ \forall k$ | Constraint |
| 11 | LMS update | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Recursive |
| 12 | LMS step-size bound | $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$ | Stability range |
| 13 | LMS misadjustance (approx.) | $\mathcal{M} \approx \mu\,\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ | Dimensionless |
| 14 | Normalized LMS (NLMS) update | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \dfrac{2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}{\varepsilon + \mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)}$ | Bounded $\mu \in (0,1)$ |
| 15 | RLS forgetting factor | $0 \ll \lambda \le 1$ (typ. 0.98–1.0) | Scalar |
| 16 | Autocorrelation lag | $r_{xx}(\ell) = E[x(n)\,x(n+\ell)]$ | Power |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering utility of the normal equation:** It transforms an *infinite-memory* statistical problem (computing expected values) into a **finite linear system** that can be solved efficiently using the Levinson-Durbin algorithm (when $\mathbf{R}$ is Toeplitz), with $O(M^{2})$ complexity instead of $O(M^{3})$ for generic matrix inversion.

## 2.7 Performance Surface Geometry

Because $J(\mathbf{w})$ is **quadratic and convex** in $\mathbf{w}$ (since $\mathbf{R}$ is positive semi-definite), the cost surface is an **ellipsoidal bowl** with a unique minimum at $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$. The principal axes align with the eigenvectors of $\mathbf{R}$, and the eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ govern the curvature (steepness) along each axis. A large eigenvalue spread $\lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$ (high *condition number*) makes the bowl elongated, slowing LMS convergence — this motivates NLMS and RLS.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Normal Equations

We derive the **Wiener-Hopf equation** from first principles, keeping every algebraic step explicit.

**Step 1 — Define the filter output vectorially.**

$$y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{M-1} w_k\, x(n-k) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n).$$

*Justification:* The transversal FIR structure forms a linear combination of $M$ delayed input samples with weights $w_k$.

**Step 2 — Define the error and expand its square.**

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n).$$

Squaring,

$$e^{2}(n) = d^{2}(n) - 2\,d(n)\,\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n) + \bigl(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)\bigr)^{2}.$$

**Step 3 — Take the statistical expectation (ensemble average).**

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E\bigl[e^{2}(n)\bigr] = E\bigl[d^{2}(n)\bigr] - 2\,\mathbf{w}^{T}\,E\bigl[\mathbf{x}(n)\,d(n)\bigr] + \mathbf{w}^{T}\,E\bigl[\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\bigr]\,\mathbf{w}.$$

Define the three statistical quantities:

$$\sigma_d^{2} \triangleq E\bigl[d^{2}(n)\bigr], \quad \mathbf{p} \triangleq E\bigl[\mathbf{x}(n)\,d(n)\bigr], \quad \mathbf{R} \triangleq E\bigl[\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\bigr].$$

Therefore the compact quadratic form is

$$\boxed{\,J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^{2} - 2\,\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}.\,}$$

**Step 4 — Differentiate $J$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ (gradient vector).**

Using matrix calculus identities $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{p}) = \mathbf{p}$ and $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}) = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ (since $\mathbf{R}$ is symmetric):

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(\mathbf{w}) = -2\,\mathbf{p} + 2\,\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}.$$

**Step 5 — Set the gradient to zero for an extremum.**

$$-2\,\mathbf{p} + 2\,\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{0}.$$

Dividing by $2$:

$$\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{p}.$$

This is the **Wiener-Hopf equation** (also called the **normal equation** of linear MMSE estimation). It is a system of $M$ linear equations in $M$ unknowns.

**Step 6 — Solve for $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$.**

Provided $\mathbf{R}$ is non-singular,

$$\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\,\mathbf{p}.$$

**Step 7 — Confirm minimum via second-order test.**

The Hessian of $J$ is

$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}^{2} J(\mathbf{w}) = 2\,\mathbf{R}.$$

Since $\mathbf{R}$ is positive definite for a persistently exciting input, the critical point is indeed a **strict global minimum**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of $J_{\min}$

Starting from $J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^{2} - 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ evaluated at $\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
J_{\min} &= \sigma_d^{2} - 2\,\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}^{T}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}^{T}\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} \\
&= \sigma_d^{2} - 2\,(\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p})^{T}\mathbf{p} + (\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p})^{T}\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}) \\
&= \sigma_d^{2} - 2\,\mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p} \\
&= \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}.
\end{aligned}$$

*Explanation of each line:*
- Line 1 substitutes the optimum into the general expression.
- Line 2 replaces $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$ with $\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ (using symmetry of $\mathbf{R}$ so $(\mathbf{R}^{-1})^{T} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}$).
- Line 3 simplifies $\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{R}^{-1} = \mathbf{I}$.
- Line 4 combines the two middle terms.

Hence,

$$\boxed{\,J_{\min} = \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}.\,}$$

## 3.3 Derivation of the LMS Algorithm from Steepest Descent

The steepest-descent recursion is

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J\bigl(\mathbf{w}(n)\bigr).$$

Substituting $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\,\mathbf{p} + 2\,\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}$:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\bigl[\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}(n)\bigr].$$

When $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{p}$ are unavailable, we use the **instantaneous unbiased estimates**:

$$\hat{\mathbf{R}}(n) = \mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n), \qquad \hat{\mathbf{p}}(n) = \mathbf{x}(n)\,d(n).$$

Plugging these estimates into the steepest-descent recursion gives the celebrated LMS update:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}(n+1) &= \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\bigl[\mathbf{x}(n)\,d(n) - \mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{w}(n)\bigr] \\
&= \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,\mathbf{x}(n)\bigl[d(n) - \mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{w}(n)\bigr] \\
&= \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n).
\end{aligned}$$

The instantaneous gradient estimate is unbiased: $E[-2\mathbf{x}(n)e(n)] = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n)$, exactly the true gradient. Hence LMS converges *in the mean* to $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$.

## 3.4 Worked Example — A 2-Tap Wiener Filter

Let $x(n)$ be WSS with $r_{xx}(0) = 1$, $r_{xx}(1) = 0.5$, and cross-correlation $r_{xd}(0) = 0.8$, $r_{xd}(1) = 0.4$. For $M = 2$:

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} r_{xx}(0) & r_{xx}(1) \\ r_{xx}(1) & r_{xx}(0) \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 1 & 0.5 \\ 0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix}, \quad \mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix}.$$

Determinant: $\det(\mathbf{R}) = 1 - 0.25 = 0.75$. Inverse:

$$\mathbf{R}^{-1} = \frac{1}{0.75}\begin{bmatrix} 1 & -0.5 \\ -0.5 & 1 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 4/3 & -2/3 \\ -2/3 & 4/3 \end{bmatrix}.$$

Optimal weights:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} &= \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} 4/3 & -2/3 \\ -2/3 & 4/3 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ 0.4 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} (4/3)(0.8) - (2/3)(0.4) \\ -(2/3)(0.8) + (4/3)(0.4) \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \begin{bmatrix} 3.2/3 - 0.8/3 \\ -1.6/3 + 1.6/3 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 2.4/3 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix}.
\end{aligned}$$

Minimum MSE (with $\sigma_d^{2} = 1$ say):

$$J_{\min} = 1 - \begin{bmatrix} 0.8 & 0.4 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 0.8 \\ 0 \end{bmatrix} = 1 - 0.64 = 0.36.$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Wiener Filter — Block-Level Architecture (Mermaid)

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeSrc["Input Signal x(n)"] --> nodeDelay["Tapped Delay Line x(n), x(n-1), ..., x(n-M+1)"]
    nodeDelay --> nodeMult["Weight Multipliers w0, w1, ..., w(M-1)"]
    nodeMult --> nodeSum["Adder"]
    nodeSum --> nodeOut["Filter Output y(n)"]
    nodeRef["Desired Signal d(n)"] --> nodeDiff
    nodeOut --> nodeDiff
    nodeDiff["Subtractor d(n) - y(n)"] --> nodeErr["Error e(n)"]
    nodeErr --> nodeCost["Performance Index J = E[e^2]"]
    nodeCost --> nodeOpt["Wiener-Hopf Solver R w = p"]
    nodeOpt --> nodeMult
```

```mermaid
    subA --> errNode["Error e(n)"]

    inB["Reference Input x(n)"] --> tapLine["Tapped Delay Line x(n) ... x(n-M+1)"]
    tapLine --> gain["LMS Update w n+1 = w n + 2 mu e x"]

    gain --> adaptOut
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**State and explain the orthogonality principle in Wiener filter theory.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The orthogonality principle states that at the optimum Wiener filter setting, the estimation error $e_{\text{opt}}(n)$ is orthogonal to every input sample used in estimation. Mathematically,

$$E\bigl[\,e_{\text{opt}}(n)\, x(n-k)\,\bigr] = 0, \quad k = 0, 1, \ldots, M-1.$$

In vector form, $E[\mathbf{x}(n)\, e_{\text{opt}}(n)] = \mathbf{0}$. This is a direct consequence of setting the gradient $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = \mathbf{0}$ and is the necessary and sufficient condition for $J$ to attain its minimum. Physically, it means the filter has extracted every linearly-predictable component from $d(n)$; the residual $e_{\text{opt}}(n)$ is uncorrelated with $\mathbf{x}(n)$ and carries only the irreducible (non-linear) error power.

---

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]`
**Write the LMS algorithm update equation. Explain the role of the step-size $\mu$.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The LMS weight-update equation is

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n).$$

**Role of $\mu$:**
- $\mu$ is the adaptation constant (step-size).
- It controls the trade-off between **convergence speed** and **steady-state misadjustance**.
- Small $\mu \Rightarrow$ slow convergence, low misadjustance, stable.
- Large $\mu \Rightarrow$ fast convergence, high misadjustance, may become unstable if $\mu > 1/\lambda_{\max}$.
- Practical bound: $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$, with $\lambda_{\max}$ being the largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}$. In NLMS, $\mu \in (0,1)$ and is independent of input power.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Derive the normal (Wiener-Hopf) equations for the optimum FIR filter of order $M$. Clearly define the autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ and the cross-correlation vector $\mathbf{p}$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An adaptive noise-cancellation system uses an FIR Wiener filter with $M=2$. The reference noise $x(n)$ has autocorrelations $r_{xx}(0)=2$, $r_{xx}(1)=1$, and the cross-correlations with the primary signal are $r_{xd}(0)=1.5$, $r_{xd}(1)=0.5$. Determine the optimal weight vector $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$ and the minimum mean-square error $J_{\min}$, assuming $\sigma_d^{2}=2$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Formulation.* Filter output $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$, error $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{x}(n)$.

*Step 2 — MSE expansion.*

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E[e^{2}(n)] = \sigma_d^{2} - 2\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}.$$

with $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)]$ ($M \times M$ symmetric Toeplitz) and $\mathbf{p} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)d(n)]$ ($M \times 1$).

*Step 3 — Gradient.* $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}$.

*Step 4 — Optimum.* Setting gradient to zero and assuming $\mathbf{R}$ is non-singular:

$$\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{p} \implies \mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}.$$

*Step 5 — MMSE.* $J_{\min} = \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$.

*Step 6 — Orthogonality.* $E[e_{\text{opt}}(n)x(n-k)] = 0,\ \forall k \in \{0,\ldots,M-1\}$.

*(Each step carries marks; the matrix definitions and orthogonality are explicitly required.)*

**(b) Numerical problem (7 marks):**

$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \mathbf{p} = \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix}, \qquad \sigma_d^{2} = 2.$$

Determinant: $\det(\mathbf{R}) = 4 - 1 = 3$. Inverse:

$$\mathbf{R}^{-1} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}.$$

Optimal weights:

$$\begin{aligned}
\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} &= \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2 & -1 \\ -1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix} 1.5 \\ 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \\
&= \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 3 - 0.5 \\ -1.5 + 1 \end{bmatrix} = \frac{1}{3}\begin{bmatrix} 2.5 \\ -0.5 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 5/6 \\ -1/6 \end{bmatrix}.
\end{aligned}$$

Minimum MSE:

$$J_{\min} = \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p} = 2 - \begin{bmatrix} 1.5 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix} \cdot \begin{bmatrix} 5/6 \\ -1/6 \end{bmatrix} = 2 - \left(\tfrac{7.5}{6} - \tfrac{0.5}{6}\right) = 2 - \tfrac{7}{6} = \tfrac{5}{6} \approx 0.833.$$

**Answer:** $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = [\,5/6,\ -1/6\,]^{T}$ and $J_{\min} = 5/6$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Supplementary]`

**(a)** Explain the principle of the LMS adaptive filter. Starting from the steepest-descent method, derive the LMS update equation and state the stability condition for the step-size $\mu$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With reference to the performance surface of the Wiener filter, explain why the LMS algorithm converges more slowly when the input autocorrelation matrix has a large eigenvalue spread. Describe how the Normalized LMS (NLMS) algorithm overcomes this limitation. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

**(a) LMS Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 — Steepest descent.* $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu[\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n)]$.

*Step 2 — Replace statistics with instantaneous estimates.* $\mathbf{R} \to \mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)$, $\mathbf{p} \to \mathbf{x}(n)d(n)$.

*Step 3 — Recognize* $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\mathbf{w}(n)$ and substitute:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n).$$

*Step 4 — Stability.* Taking expectation under the independence assumption, the mean weight vector converges iff all eigenvalues $\lambda_i$ of $\mathbf{R}$ satisfy $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$.

*Step 5 — Computational simplicity.* LMS requires only $2M+1$ multiplications per iteration — far cheaper than direct inversion of $\mathbf{R}$.

**(b) Eigenvalue spread and NLMS (7 marks):**

*Eigenvalue spread problem.* The MSE surface is an ellipsoid with principal axes along the eigenvectors of $\mathbf{R}$ and axis lengths proportional to $1/\lambda_i$. When $\lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min} \gg 1$, the bowl is highly elongated; steepest-descent and LMS zig-zag across the narrow valley, producing slow convergence. Convergence time-constants are $\tau_i \approx 1/(4\mu\lambda_i)$, so the slowest mode $\tau_{\max} \approx 1/(4\mu\lambda_{\min})$.

*NLMS solution.* Normalize the update direction by the instantaneous input power $\mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \frac{2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}{\varepsilon + \mathbf{x}^{T}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)}.$$

This makes the effective step-size independent of input power — convergence speed becomes uniform across eigen-directions, eliminating zig-zag. A small $\varepsilon > 0$ prevents division-by-zero.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the symmetry of $\mathbf{R}$.** Many students drop the factor $2$ in $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}(\mathbf{w}^{T}\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}) = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ — costing 1 mark.
> 2. **Confusing $\mathbf{p}$ definition.** $\mathbf{p} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)d(n)]$, not $E[d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)]$ (though the transpose is fine because $\mathbf{p}$ is a column vector).
> 3. **Stability bound misquoted.** The LMS bound is $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$, *not* $1/\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$. The trace gives a *conservative* sufficient condition only.
> 4. **Order of $\mathbf{R}$.** When asked for a $M$-tap filter, $\mathbf{R}$ is $M \times M$ — not $M+1$. Taps are indexed $0$ to $M-1$.
> 5. **Sign errors.** $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ gives the LMS update $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$. Reversing the sign of $e$ reverses the adaptation direction.
> 6. **NLMS range.** In NLMS, $0 < \mu < 1$ (not $1/\lambda_{\max}$) — this is the whole point of normalization.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Wiener filter** = optimum linear MMSE filter for WSS inputs with known second-order statistics.
- **Normal (Wiener-Hopf) equation:** $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{p}$ — a linear system whose solution is $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$.
- **Orthogonality principle:** $E[e_{\text{opt}}(n)x(n-k)] = 0,\ \forall k$ — equivalent to the normal equation.
- **MMSE:** $J_{\min} = \sigma_d^{2} - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$.
- **$\mathbf{R}$ is symmetric, Toeplitz, positive definite** (for persistently exciting input).
- **Performance surface** is a convex quadratic bowl; unique minimum at $\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}}$.
- **LMS** uses instantaneous gradient estimates — simple, robust, but noisy and slow for large eigenvalue spread.
- **LMS stability bound:** $0 < \mu < 1/\lambda_{\max}$; **misadjustance** $\mathcal{M} \approx \mu\,\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$.
- **NLMS** normalizes by input power $\mathbf{x}^{T}\mathbf{x}$; step-size in $(0,1)$.
- **RLS** converges in $O(M)$ iterations at cost $O(M^{2})$ — preferred for fast-tracking applications.
- **Applications:** system identification, channel equalization, noise cancellation, prediction, adaptive beamforming, line enhancement.
- **Standard exam traps:** missing factor of 2 in gradient, wrong sign of error, confusing $\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ with $\lambda_{\max}$, off-by-one in tap indexing, forgetting $\mathbf{R}$ symmetry in the inverse step.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Adaptive filters for adaptive channel equalization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEECT746 - ADVANCED DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Adaptive filters for adaptive channel equalization # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!NOTE] > **Adaptive Channel Equalization** is the process of using a self-adjusting (adaptive) digital filter placed at the receiver of a digital communication system to reverse (i.e., *equalize*) the distortive effects of a dispersive communication channel, thereby mitigating **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** caused by bandwidth-limited and multipath-prone transmission media. In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the adaptive equalizer is a **transversal FIR (tapped-delay-line) filter** whose coefficients $\mathbf{w}(n) = [w_0(n), w_1(n), \ldots, w_{N-1}(n)]^{T}$ are updated recursively using an adaptive algorithm (e.g., **LMS** or **RLS**) so that the combined impulse response of **Channel + Equalizer** approaches an ideal **target response** (typically a unit pulse with a single tap, or a delayed delta function). ## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy > [!IMPORTANT] > **Analogy — "The Echoing Hallway and the Smart Hearing Aid":** > Imagine you are speaking to a friend through a long, narrow stone hallway. Your friend hears your voice, but it arrives smeared and echoed because the sound waves bounce off the walls and arrive at slightly different times — making syllables overlap and blur into each other. The friend cannot understand the words clearly (this is **Inter-Symbol Interference**, or ISI). > > A **hearing aid with adaptive noise cancellation** is placed at your friend's ear. This hearing aid listens to the smeared sound, *learns* the acoustic signature of the hallway over time, and then produces an *inverse-filtered* version of the sound that **undoes the smearing**. The friend now hears crisp, clear speech. > > The adaptive equalizer plays exactly the same role for a digital receiver: the **stone hallway = the communication channel**, the **smearing = ISI**, and the **hearing aid = adaptive equalizer**. The "learning" is done by the LMS or RLS algorithm as data symbols stream through. ## 1.3 Why Equalization Is Required — The Channel Distortion Problem In a band-limited, dispersive channel (e.g., twisted-pair copper line, wireless multipath channel, optical fiber with chromatic dispersion), the transmitted symbol pulses spread in time and overlap with adjacent symbol periods. This overlap is called **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)**. | Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Purpose | |---|---|---|---| | **Symbol Period** | $T$ | depends on baud rate | Time allocated per symbol | | **Channel Impulse Response Length** | $L_h$ | 3 – 20 taps | Memory span of the channel | | **Signal-to-Noise Ratio** | $\text{SNR}$ | **10 dB – 30 dB** | Quality of received signal | | **Bit Error Rate target** | $\text{BER}$ | $\leq 10^{-6}$ | Communication reliability | | **Eye opening** | — | to be maximized | Visual quality measure | > [!NOTE] > The **eye diagram** is the classical visual diagnostic for ISI. A wide-open eye = little ISI; a closed eye = severe ISI. The equalizer's job is to *re-open the eye* adaptively. ## 1.4 Types of Adaptive Equalizers Used in Practice 1. **Linear Transversal Equalizer (LTE)** — a simple FIR filter. 2. **Decision-Feedback Equalizer (DFE)** — uses a feedback filter over past decisions to cancel post-cursor ISI; outperforms LTE on severe channels. 3. **Fractionally Spaced Equalizer (FSE)** — samples at ≥ 2× the symbol rate, robust to sampling phase. 4. **Adaptive Channel Equalizer based on LMS / NLMS / RLS / Kalman** — these are *algorithms*, not structures. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Communication System Model A baseband-equivalent digital communication system is mathematically modelled as: $$ y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{L_h-1} h(k)\, s(n-k) + v(n) $$ where $s(n)$ is the transmitted symbol sequence (e.g., from a QAM or PAM constellation), $h(k)$ is the **discrete-time equivalent channel impulse response**, $v(n)$ is **Additive White Gaussian Noise (AWGN)** with variance $\sigma_v^{2}$, and $y(n)$ is the received signal entering the equalizer. > [!IMPORTANT] > The **received signal $y(n)$** is the only quantity available at the equalizer input. Neither $h(k)$ nor $s(n)$ is known exactly. Hence, the equalizer coefficients $\mathbf{w}(n)$ must be **estimated online** using an adaptive algorithm. ## 2.2 Linear Transversal Equalizer — Operation The LTE produces an estimate of the transmitted symbol: $$ \hat{s}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} w_i(n)\, y(n-i) \;=\; \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{y}(n) $$ The tap-input vector is: $$ \mathbf{y}(n) = [y(n),\; y(n-1),\; \ldots,\; y(n-N+1)]^{T} $$ The **error signal** during the *training phase* is: $$ e(n) = s(n) \;-\; \hat{s}(n) = s(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{y}(n) $$ where $s(n)$ is the locally generated known training sequence. The LMS algorithm then updates: $$ \mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{y}(n) $$ ## 2.3 Decision-Directed Adaptation (Tracking Mode) After the training sequence ends, the receiver switches to a **decision-directed** mode: $$ e(n) = \tilde{s}(n) - \hat{s}(n), \qquad \tilde{s}(n) = \text{dec}\!\left(\hat{s}(n)\right) $$ where $\text{dec}(\cdot)$ quantizes $\hat{s}(n)$ to the nearest symbol of the constellation. This is feasible **only when the eye is already open** (i.e., training has converged); otherwise decisions are too noisy and the equalizer diverges. ## 2.4 Decision-Feedback Equalizer (DFE) The DFE has a **feedforward FIR** $\mathbf{w}_f(n)$ of length $N_f$ and a **feedback FIR** $\mathbf{w}_b(n)$ of length $N_b$ operating on **past decided symbols**: $$ \hat{s}(n) = \underbrace{\sum_{i=0}^{N_f-1} w_{f,i}(n)\, y(n-i)}_{\text{feedforward}} \;-\; \underbrace{\sum_{j=1}^{N_b} w_{b,j}(n)\, \tilde{s}(n-j)}_{\text{feedback}} $$ $$ \text{or compactly:} \quad \hat{s}(n) = \mathbf{w}_f^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{y}(n) - \mathbf{w}_b^{T}(n)\,\tilde{\mathbf{s}}(n-1) $$ > [!NOTE] > The minus sign on the feedback path *cancels post-cursor ISI* (the smearing of future symbols into the current one), which is impossible for a purely causal LTE. ## 2.5 Convergence Condition for LMS-Equalizer The LMS equalizer converges in the mean if the step-size satisfies: $$ 0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{R})} $$ where $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{y}(n)\mathbf{y}^{T}(n)]$ is the autocorrelation of the equalizer input, and $\lambda_{\max}$ is its largest eigenvalue. A safer, input-independent bound is: $$ 0 < \mu < \frac{2}{N \sigma_y^{2}}, \quad \sigma_y^{2} = E[y^{2}(n)] $$ ## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Table 2.1 — High-Yield Formulas for Adaptive Channel Equalization** | \# | Quantity / Equation | Formula | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Received signal model | $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{L_h-1} h(k) s(n-k) + v(n)$ | Channel output + noise | | 2 | LTE output | $\hat{s}(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{y}(n)$ | Equalizer estimate | | 3 | Training error | $e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n)$ | Error w.r.t. known sequence | | 4 | LMS weight update | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n) \mathbf{y}(n)$ | Adaptive recursion | | 5 | LMS convergence bound | $0 < \mu < 2 \,/\, \lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{R})$ | Mean-square convergence | | 6 | Stable step-size | $0 < \mu < 2 \,/\, (N \sigma_y^{2})$ | Input-independent bound | | 7 | Mean-square error | $J(n) = E[\vert e(n) \vert^{2}]$ | Cost function | | 8 | Wiener (optimum) weights | $\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | $\mathbf{p} = E[\mathbf{y}(n) s(n)]$ | | 9 | Minimum MSE | $J_{\min} = \sigma_s^{2} - \mathbf{p}^{T} \mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}}$ | Floor of cost | | 10 | Misadjustment (LMS) | $\mathcal{M} \approx \mu N \sigma_y^{2}/2$ | Steady-state excess MSE ratio | | 11 | NLMS step-size | $\mu(n) = \alpha \,/\, (\epsilon + \Vert\mathbf{y}(n)\Vert^{2})$ | Normalized $\mu$ | | 12 | RLS forgetting factor | $0 < \lambda < 1$ (typ. $0.98$ – $0.999$) | Exponential weighting | | 13 | RLS gain vector | $\mathbf{k}(n) = \dfrac{\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{y}(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{y}^{T}(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{y}(n)}$ | Kalman-style update | | 14 | DFE output | $\hat{s}(n) = \mathbf{w}_f^{T}\mathbf{y}(n) - \mathbf{w}_b^{T}\tilde{\mathbf{s}}(n-1)$ | FFF + FBF | | 15 | Eigenvalue-spread sensitivity | $\rho(\mathbf{R}) = \lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$ | Slows LMS convergence | > **Engineering Utility:** Equalizers using these formulas are embedded inside every modern **DSL modem** (ADSL/VDSL), **4G/5G LTE** handsets, **Wi-Fi (802.11)** chips, and **satellite transponder** receivers. Without adaptive equalization, broadband digital communication would be impossible over multipath or band-limited media. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the LMS Weight-Update Equation Applied to the Equalizer **Step 1 — Define the equalizer cost function.** The most widely used cost is the **Mean-Square Error (MSE)**: $$ J(n) = E[\, e^{2}(n)\,] $$ **Step 2 — Express the error signal in terms of the weights.** With $e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n)$ and $\hat{s}(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\mathbf{y}(n)$: $$ e(n) = s(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\,\mathbf{y}(n) $$ **Step 3 — Differentiate the cost w.r.t. the weight vector.** We use the instantaneous-gradient approximation $\hat{\nabla} J(n) = \partial e^{2}(n)/\partial \mathbf{w}(n)$, the method of *steepest descent on an instantaneous* (LMS) cost surface: $$ \frac{\partial e^{2}(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = 2 e(n)\, \frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = -2\,e(n)\,\mathbf{y}(n) $$ **Step 4 — Apply the steepest-descent recursion with step-size $\mu$.** $$ \mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \frac{\mu}{2}\, \frac{\partial e^{2}(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{y}(n) $$ > [!NOTE] > The factor $\tfrac{1}{2}$ cancels with the $2$ in the derivative, giving the clean LMS form. This is the *exact* update used in the equalizer implementation. ## 3.2 Derivation of the Convergence Condition $0 < \mu < 2 / \lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{R})$ **Step 1 — Take the expectation of the weight recursion.** Let $\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}}$ be the weight-error vector. Using the **independence assumption** (Widrow-Hoff) and $E[e_{\mathrm{opt}}(n)\mathbf{y}(n)]=\mathbf{0}$: $$ E[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}(n+1)] = \left(\mathbf{I} - \mu \mathbf{R}\right) E[\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}(n)] $$ **Step 2 — Diagonalise $\mathbf{R}$ using its eigen-decomposition $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{Q}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{Q}^{T}$.** Define $\mathbf{v}(n) = \mathbf{Q}^{T}\boldsymbol{\varepsilon}(n)$. Then each decoupled mode satisfies: $$ v_k(n+1) = (1 - \mu \lambda_k)\, v_k(n) $$ **Step 3 — Demand geometric decay, i.e., $\vert 1 - \mu \lambda_k \vert < 1$ for every $k$.** This requires: $$ -1 < 1 - \mu \lambda_k < 1 \quad\Longrightarrow\quad 0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_k} $$ The most stringent constraint comes from the largest eigenvalue $\lambda_{\max}$: $$ \boxed{\; 0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{R})} \;} $$ ## 3.3 Derivation of the Optimum Wiener Equalizer Weights The cost $J = E[e^{2}(n)]$ is quadratic; minimising it gives the **Wiener-Hopf equation** for the equalizer: $$ \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -2 E[\mathbf{y}(n)\,s(n)] + 2\,E[\mathbf{y}(n)\mathbf{y}^{T}(n)]\,\mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0} $$ Solving: $$ \boxed{\; \mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}, \quad \mathbf{R}=E[\mathbf{y}\mathbf{y}^{T}],\ \mathbf{p}=E[\mathbf{y}\,s] \;} $$ The corresponding minimum MSE is: $$ J_{\min} = E[s^{2}(n)] - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p} = \sigma_s^{2} - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The LMS algorithm is a *stochastic*, real-time approximation of the Wiener solution. As $n \to \infty$ and $\mu \to 0$, $\mathbf{w}(n) \to \mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}}$ in the mean. ## 3.4 LMS Equalizer Convergence Analysis (Excess MSE) **Steady-state excess MSE** (misadjustment) of the LMS equalizer is: $$ J_{\mathrm{ex}}(\infty) \;\approx\; \frac{\mu N \sigma_y^{2} J_{\min}}{2 - \mu N \sigma_y^{2}} \;\approx\; \frac{\mu N \sigma_y^{2} J_{\min}}{2} \quad (\text{small } \mu) $$ **Time-constant** of the slowest mode (worst-case convergence speed): $$ \tau_{\max} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{\mu \lambda_{\min}} $$ > [!NOTE] > If the channel $h(k)$ has deep nulls, $\lambda_{\min}(\mathbf{R})$ becomes very small, $\rho(\mathbf{R})$ becomes huge, and **LMS converges extremely slowly** (the classic spectral-spread problem). This is the primary motivation for using the **RLS algorithm** in equalizers, whose convergence is essentially independent of $\rho(\mathbf{R})$. ## 3.5 RLS-Based Equalizer (Derivation Sketch) **Step 1 — Define the exponentially-weighted least-squares cost:** $$ J_{\mathrm{RLS}}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i}\, \bigl\vert e(i)\bigr\vert^{2}, \quad e(i) = s(i) - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\mathbf{y}(i) $$ **Step 2 — Minimise w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}(n)$** (set $\partial J/\partial \mathbf{w} = \mathbf{0}$): $$ \mathbf{w}(n) = \mathbf{R}^{-1}(n)\,\mathbf{p}(n) $$ with $$ \mathbf{R}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n}\lambda^{n-i}\mathbf{y}(i)\mathbf{y}^{T}(i), \qquad \mathbf{p}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n}\lambda^{n-i}\mathbf{y}(i)s(i) $$ **Step 3 — Use the matrix inversion lemma** (Woodbury identity) to update $\mathbf{P}(n)=\mathbf{R}^{-1}(n)$ recursively: $$ \mathbf{P}(n) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\!\left[\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n)\mathbf{y}^{T}(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)\right] $$ with gain vector $$ \mathbf{k}(n) = \frac{\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{y}(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{y}^{T}(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{y}(n)} $$ **Step 4 — Final RLS weight update** (computationally equivalent to Kalman filter): $$ \mathbf{w}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n)\, e(n), \quad e(n) = s(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n-1)\mathbf{y}(n) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Comparison Summary for KTU 14-mark answers:** | Feature | LMS Equalizer | RLS Equalizer | |---|---|---| | Complexity / tap | $2N$ multiplies | $\sim 4N^{2}$ multiplies | | Convergence speed | Slow, depends on $\rho(\mathbf{R})$ | Fast, $\sim 2N$ iterations (independent of $\rho$) | | Steady-state MSE | $J_{\min} + \mathcal{M} J_{\min}$ | $J_{\min}(1 + \mathcal{M}_{\mathrm{RLS}})$ | | Numerical stability | Excellent | Needs $\mathbf{P}(n)$ reset / rescue | | Best use | Slow time-varying channels | Fast-converging burst equalization | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Communication System with Adaptive Equalizer ```mermaid flowchart LR srcA[Source s of n] --> txA[Transmitter
Pulse Shaping] txA --> chA[Channel h of n
ISI plus AWGN v of n] chA --> rxA[Receiver
Match Filter] rxA --> eqA[Adaptive Equalizer
w of n] trnA[(Training Sequence
or Decision Device)] -.->|s tilde n| eqA eqA --> errA[Error Generator
e of n equals s minus s hat] errA -->|gradient| eqA eqA --> decA[Symbol Decoder] decA --> outA[Output s hat n] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Linear Transversal Adaptive Equalizer (Internal) ```mermaid flowchart LR y0[Input y of n] --> d0[Delay z to minus 1] d0 --> y1[Tap y of n minus 1] y1 --> d1[Delay z to minus 1] d1 --> y2[Tap y of n minus 2] y2 --> d2[Delay z to minus 1] d2 --> yN[Tap y of n minus N plus 1] y0 --> m0[Multiplier w0] y1 --> m1[Multiplier w1] y2 --> m2[Multiplier w2] yN --> mN[Multiplier wN minus 1] m0 --> sumA[Summation Node] m1 --> sumA m2 --> sumA mN --> sumA sumA --> sHat[Equalizer Output s hat of n] trnB[Reference s of n] --> eGen[Subtractor] sHat --> eGen eGen --> eSig[Error e of n] eSig --> lmsCore[LMS Update Core] y0 --> lmsCore y1 --> lmsCore y2 --> lmsCore yN --> lmsCore lmsCore -->|new weights| m0 lmsCore -->|new weights| m1 lmsCore -->|new weights| m2 lmsCore -->|new weights| mN ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Decision-Feedback Adaptive Equalizer ```mermaid flowchart LR ffIn[Input y of n] --> ffFilt[Feedforward FIR
wf of n] ffFilt --> sumDFE[Summer] fbIn[Past Decisions s tilde of n minus 1 ...] --> fbFilt[Feedback FIR
wb of n] fbFilt --> sumDFE sumDFE --> sHatDFE[Soft Estimate s hat of n] sHatDFE --> slicer[Slicer / Decision Device] slicer --> sTilde[Hard Decision s tilde of n] refDFE[Reference s of n during training] --> errDFE[Error e of n] sTilde --> errDFE sHatDFE --> errDFE errDFE --> lmsDFE[LMS or RLS Update] ffIn --> lmsDFE fbIn --> lmsDFE lmsDFE -->|wf| ffFilt lmsDFE -->|wb| fbFilt ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagrams:** Every rectangular block represents an operation (delay, multiply, sum, slice, adapt). Data flows left-to-right along the top "signal" rail; the bottom "weight-update" rail carries the *error-driven* adaptation back to the multipliers. The minus sign on the feedback summer is a *convention* indicating that the FBF output is **subtracted** from the FFF output to cancel post-cursor ISI. ## 4.4 Adaptive-Equalizer Operational State Machine ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> TrainingMode TrainingMode --> TrackingMode : Training sequence ends
Switch to decision-directed TrackingMode --> TrainingMode : Channel change detected
Re-trigger training TrainingMode --> Converged : MSE below threshold Converged --> TrackingMode : Steady-state tracking TrackingMode --> Diverged : Severe channel change Diverged --> TrainingMode : Re-initialise weights ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define adaptive channel equalization. What is the role of the training sequence in an adaptive equalizer?** **Model Answer:** > **Adaptive channel equalization** is a receiver-side signal-processing technique that uses a self-adjusting digital filter (whose coefficients are updated iteratively by an algorithm such as LMS or RLS) to *invert* the distortive impulse response of a band-limited, dispersive communication channel, thereby suppressing **Inter-Symbol Interference (ISI)** at the demodulated output. > > **Role of the training sequence:** > 1. It provides a *locally known reference* $s(n)$ at the receiver, against which the equalizer output $\hat{s}(n)$ is compared. > 2. The error $e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n)$ is fed back to the LMS/RLS update to **steer the filter weights toward the Wiener solution**. > 3. Without a training sequence, the equalizer has *no ground truth* to learn from (in supervised mode) and must rely on blind techniques (CMA, Sato, etc.). > 4. After the training period, the equalizer typically switches to **decision-directed mode** for ongoing tracking. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Compare Linear Transversal Equalizer (LTE) and Decision-Feedback Equalizer (DFE) in terms of structure and ISI-cancellation capability.** **Model Answer:** | Feature | Linear Transversal Equalizer (LTE) | Decision-Feedback Equalizer (DFE) | |---|---|---| | **Structure** | Single FIR filter $\mathbf{w}(n)$ of $N$ taps on the received signal | Feedforward FIR $\mathbf{w}_f$ + Feedback FIR $\mathbf{w}_b$ on past decided symbols | | **ISI it cancels** | Both pre-cursor and post-cursor ISI, but with noise enhancement on spectral nulls | Efficiently cancels *post-cursor* ISI without boosting noise | | **Error propagation** | Not applicable | Vulnerable — one wrong decision corrupts subsequent FBF taps for several symbols | | **Convergence** | Slower, especially for severe channels | Slightly faster, since FBF is linear in past *known* decisions | | **Best suited for** | Mild ISI, channels with shallow nulls | Severe ISI, channels with deep spectral nulls (e.g., voice-band modems) | > The DFE is structurally more powerful but introduces the *error-propagation* problem, which is absent in the LTE. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### Question A — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Question]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the LMS weight-update equation for an adaptive transversal equalizer. Clearly state the assumptions used. > > **(b) [7 Marks]** A communication channel has impulse response $h(n) = [\,0.3,\; 1.0,\; 0.3\,]$ and the equalizer input SNR is $20\ \text{dB}$. Design a 3-tap LMS equalizer. With step-size $\mu = 0.05$ and initial weights $\mathbf{w}(0) = [0,\ 0,\ 0]^{T}$, compute the first two weight updates when a training symbol $s(0)=+1$ is transmitted and the received samples are $y(0) = 0.30,\ y(-1) = 1.00,\ y(-2) = 0.30$ (use these to form the equalizer input vector at $n=0$). #### Model Solution **Part (a) — LMS Derivation [7 Marks]** > [!NOTE] > **Key Assumptions (must be stated for full marks):** > 1. The equalizer cost is $J(n) = E[e^{2}(n)]$. > 2. The *instantaneous gradient* $\hat{\nabla} J(n) = \partial e^{2}(n)/\partial \mathbf{w}(n)$ is a noise-free estimate of the true gradient — this is the **Widrow-Hoff approximation**. > 3. Input $\mathbf{y}(n)$ and error $e(n)$ are *statistically independent* (this gives the unbiased convergence proof). > 4. The step-size $\mu$ is small enough that the weight update is "adiabatic". **Derivation:** $$ \hat{s}(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\mathbf{y}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} w_i(n)\,y(n-i) $$ $$ e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n) $$ $$ \frac{\partial e^{2}(n)}{\partial w_i(n)} = 2 e(n) \frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial w_i(n)} = -2 e(n)\,y(n-i) $$ Stacking for all taps: $$ \nabla_{\mathbf{w}} e^{2}(n) = -2 e(n)\,\mathbf{y}(n) $$ Applying steepest descent: $$ \boxed{\;\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \frac{\mu}{2}\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} e^{2}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{y}(n)\;} $$ **Part (b) — Numerical Computation [7 Marks]** Given: $\mu = 0.05$, $\mathbf{w}(0) = [0,\ 0,\ 0]^{T}$, $s(0)=+1$, $y(0) = 0.30$, $y(-1) = 1.00$, $y(-2) = 0.30$. **Iteration $n = 0$:** $$ \mathbf{y}(0) = [\,y(0),\ y(-1),\ y(-2)\,]^{T} = [\,0.30,\ 1.00,\ 0.30\,]^{T} $$ $$ \hat{s}(0) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(0)\,\mathbf{y}(0) = 0 $$ $$ e(0) = s(0) - \hat{s}(0) = 1 - 0 = 1 $$ $$ \mathbf{w}(1) = \mathbf{w}(0) + \mu\,e(0)\,\mathbf{y}(0) = 0 + 0.05 \cdot 1 \cdot [\,0.30,\ 1.00,\ 0.30\,]^{T} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\mathbf{w}(1) = [\,0.015,\ 0.050,\ 0.015\,]^{T}\;} $$ **Iteration $n = 1$:** For the next symbol we assume $s(1)=+1$ and the channel output gives $y(1)=0.30,\ y(0)=0.30,\ y(-1)=1.00$ (continuing the steady-state pattern): $$ \mathbf{y}(1) = [\,0.30,\ 0.30,\ 1.00\,]^{T} $$ $$ \hat{s}(1) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(1)\,\mathbf{y}(1) = 0.015(0.30) + 0.050(0.30) + 0.015(1.00) = 0.0045 + 0.0150 + 0.0150 = 0.0345 $$ $$ e(1) = 1 - 0.0345 = 0.9655 $$ $$ \mathbf{w}(2) = \mathbf{w}(1) + 0.05 \cdot 0.9655 \cdot [\,0.30,\ 0.30,\ 1.00\,]^{T} $$ $$ \mathbf{w}(2) = [\,0.015,\ 0.050,\ 0.015\,]^{T} + [\,0.01448,\ 0.01448,\ 0.04828\,]^{T} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\mathbf{w}(2) \approx [\,0.0295,\ 0.0645,\ 0.0633\,]^{T}\;} $$ > The weights are seen to grow towards an inverse-filter of the channel $[0.3, 1, 0.3]$ — the classical equalizer behaviour. --- ### Question B — **[KTU University Exam – May 2023 Model Question]** > **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the structure and operation of a Decision-Feedback Equalizer (DFE). Why is the DFE preferred over the Linear Transversal Equalizer (LTE) for channels with severe amplitude distortion? > > **(b) [7 Marks]** Starting from the exponentially-weighted least-squares cost, derive the RLS weight-update equations for the equalizer, clearly indicating the role of the *forgetting factor* $\lambda$ and the *gain vector* $\mathbf{k}(n)$. #### Model Solution **Part (a) — DFE Structure & Operation [7 Marks]** **Structure:** The DFE has two filters: 1. **Feedforward filter (FFF)** $\mathbf{w}_f(n) = [w_{f,0}, w_{f,1}, \ldots, w_{f,N_f-1}]^{T}$ operating on *received samples* $y(n), y(n-1), \ldots$ 2. **Feedback filter (FBF)** $\mathbf{w}_b(n) = [w_{b,1}, w_{b,2}, \ldots, w_{b,N_b}]^{T}$ operating on *past decided symbols* $\tilde{s}(n-1), \tilde{s}(n-2), \ldots$ **Operation:** $$ \hat{s}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{N_f-1} w_{f,i}(n) y(n-i) - \sum_{j=1}^{N_b} w_{b,j}(n) \tilde{s}(n-j) $$ 1. The FFF removes **pre-cursor ISI** (smearing from future symbols — cancelled by using future received samples). 2. The FBF removes **post-cursor ISI** (smearing from past symbols — cancelled because past decisions are effectively *known* symbols fed back). 3. The slicer produces $\tilde{s}(n) = \text{dec}(\hat{s}(n))$. 4. The error $e(n) = \tilde{s}(n) - \hat{s}(n)$ drives LMS/RLS adaptation of both $\mathbf{w}_f$ and $\mathbf{w}_b$. **Why DFE is preferred for severe channels:** > [!IMPORTANT] > A Linear Equalizer must **invert the channel frequency response** $H(\omega)$. If the channel has a deep null ($H(\omega_0) \approx 0$), the equalizer must provide a huge gain $1/H(\omega_0)$, which **amplifies noise catastrophically** at that frequency — the *noise-enhancement* problem. > > The DFE, in contrast, **does not invert the channel**. It subtracts the *known ISI* using past decisions, avoiding the spectral inversion. Hence DFE tolerates deep channel nulls with much smaller noise enhancement. This is the classical result of **Austin (1967)**. **Part (b) — RLS Derivation [7 Marks]** **Step 1 — Cost function:** $$ J_{\mathrm{RLS}}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} \vert e(i) \vert^{2}, \quad 0 < \lambda \le 1 $$ **Step 2 — Substitute error:** $e(i) = s(i) - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\mathbf{y}(i)$. Differentiate w.r.t. $\mathbf{w}(n)$ and set to $\mathbf{0}$: $$ \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -2 \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} \mathbf{y}(i) \bigl[ s(i) - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\mathbf{y}(i)\bigr] = \mathbf{0} $$ $$ \boxed{\;\mathbf{w}(n) = \mathbf{R}^{-1}(n)\,\mathbf{p}(n)\;} $$ where $$ \mathbf{R}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} \mathbf{y}(i)\mathbf{y}^{T}(i), \quad \mathbf{p}(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{n} \lambda^{n-i} \mathbf{y}(i) s(i) $$ **Step 3 — Recursive time update of $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{p}$:** $$ \mathbf{R}(n) = \lambda \mathbf{R}(n-1) + \mathbf{y}(n)\mathbf{y}^{T}(n) $$ $$ \mathbf{p}(n) = \lambda \mathbf{p}(n-1) + \mathbf{y}(n) s(n) $$ **Step 4 — Use the Woodbury matrix-inversion lemma** on $\mathbf{P}(n)=\mathbf{R}^{-1}(n)$: $$ \mathbf{P}(n) = \frac{1}{\lambda}\!\left[\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \frac{\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{y}(n)\mathbf{y}^{T}(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)}{\lambda + \mathbf{y}^{T}(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{y}(n)}\right] $$ **Step 5 — Define the gain vector** $\mathbf{k}(n)$: $$ \boxed{\;\mathbf{k}(n) = \frac{\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{y}(n)}{\lambda + \mathbf{y}^{T}(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{y}(n)}\;} $$ **Step 6 — Final RLS weight update:** $$ \boxed{\;\mathbf{w}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n)\,\bigl[s(n) - \mathbf{w}^{T}(n-1)\mathbf{y}(n)\bigr]\;} $$ **Role of $\lambda$:** > The *forgetting factor* $\lambda$ exponentially down-weights old data so that $\mathbf{P}(n)$ tracks a sliding window of effective length $\approx 1/(1-\lambda)$. A typical value is $\lambda = 0.99$, giving an effective memory of $\approx 100$ samples. **Smaller $\lambda$** → faster tracking, but noisier steady-state; **larger $\lambda$** → smoother estimates, but slower tracking of channel changes. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls** > > 1. **Confusing the equalizer's $e(n)$ sign with LMS update sign:** The error is defined as $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$; the LMS update is *additive* $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n)\mathbf{y}(n)$. Students often write a *minus* sign and lose 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting the stability bound** $0 < \mu < 2 / \lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{R})$ — full credit demands the bound plus a sentence explaining its origin (eigenvalue analysis). > 3. **Mixing up the FFF and FBF roles in DFE:** The FFF removes *pre-cursor* ISI; the FBF removes *post-cursor* ISI. Conflating these is a frequent error. > 4. **Omitting assumptions in LMS derivation** — losing 1 mark. Always state the *Widrow-Hoff independence assumption*. > 5. **Numerical-computation sign errors** — be careful with $e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n)$ when $s$ is bipolar (e.g., $s=\pm 1$). > 6. **Stating the RLS update without the Woodbury lemma** — derivations must mention the *matrix-inversion lemma* to receive full credit. > 7. **Stating that LTE and DFE both invert the channel** — they don't. The DFE *avoids channel inversion*, which is exactly why it outperforms LTE on spectrally-nulled channels. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Recap — "Must-Know" Points for KTU 2024 PEECT746** - **Adaptive channel equalization** is the *receiver-side adaptive filtering* technique used to mitigate **ISI** caused by a dispersive channel $h(n)$. - **Two main equalizer structures:** (1) **Linear Transversal Equalizer (LTE)** — single FIR; (2) **Decision-Feedback Equalizer (DFE)** — feedforward + feedback pair. - **Received signal model:** $y(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{L_h-1} h(k)s(n-k) + v(n)$. - **LTE output:** $\hat{s}(n) = \mathbf{w}^{T}(n)\mathbf{y}(n)$. - **Training-mode error:** $e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n)$. - **LMS equalizer update:** $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu e(n)\mathbf{y}(n)$. - **LMS convergence bound:** $\boxed{0 < \mu < 2 / \lambda_{\max}(\mathbf{R})}$. - **Wiener (optimum) weights:** $\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$. - **Minimum MSE floor:** $J_{\min} = \sigma_s^{2} - \mathbf{p}^{T}\mathbf{w}_{\mathrm{opt}}$. - **Misadjustment (LMS):** $\mathcal{M} \approx \mu N \sigma_y^{2}/2$. - **Slowest time-constant:** $\tau_{\max} \approx 1 / (\mu \lambda_{\min})$ — large eigenvalue spread = slow LMS. - **NLMS step-size:** $\mu(n) = \alpha / (\epsilon + \Vert\mathbf{y}\Vert^{2})$ — input-power-independent. - **RLS weight update:** $\mathbf{w}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n-1) + \mathbf{k}(n)\,e(n)$. - **RLS gain vector:** $\mathbf{k}(n) = \mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{y}(n) / [\lambda + \mathbf{y}^{T}(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)\mathbf{y}(n)]$. - **RLS inverse-correlation update:** $\mathbf{P}(n) = \lambda^{-1}[\mathbf{P}(n-1) - \mathbf{k}(n)\mathbf{y}^{T}(n)\mathbf{P}(n-1)]$. - **Forgetting factor:** typically $\lambda = 0.98$ to $0.999$. - **DFE output:** $\hat{s}(n) = \mathbf{w}_f^{T}\mathbf{y}(n) - \mathbf{w}_b^{T}\tilde{\mathbf{s}}(n-1)$. - **DFE advantage:** avoids *noise enhancement* on channels with spectral nulls. - **DFE disadvantage:** *error propagation* due to past-decision feedback. - **Operational modes:** *Training mode* (uses known $s(n)$) → *Decision-directed mode* (uses $\tilde{s}(n)$). - **Adaptive algorithms hierarchy (speed vs. complexity):** LMS < NLMS < RLS < Kalman (in $N^{2}$ cost). - **LMS is $\mathcal{O}(N)$; RLS is $\mathcal{O}(N^{2})$; Fast-RLS (FTF/FTA) is $\mathcal{O}(N)$.** - **Convergence-speed comparison:** RLS converges in $\approx 2N$ iterations; LMS needs $\approx 1/(\mu\lambda_{\min})$. - **Eigenvalue-spread problem:** DFE is *less sensitive* to $\rho(\mathbf{R})$ than LTE because it does not invert the spectrum. - **KTU-favourite question patterns:** (i) "Derive LMS update" (14 marks); (ii) "Compute 2-3 weight iterations numerically" (14 marks); (iii) "Compare LTE vs DFE" (7-14 marks); (iv) "Why RLS over LMS?" (3-7 marks). - **Key Assumptions to ALWAYS state in derivations:** (a) independence of $\mathbf{y}(n)$ and $e_{\mathrm{opt}}(n)$ (Widrow-Hoff); (b) $\mu$ small enough for the LMS recursion to be stable; (c) channel is linear and time-invariant over the observation window (or slowly varying for tracking). - **Real-world applications of adaptive equalization:** 4G/5G handsets, Wi-Fi 802.11 a/g/n/ac/ax, ADSL/VDSL modems, satellite transponders, magnetic-recording read channels, high-speed serial links (PCIe, SerDes).

topics.title: Adaptive noise cancellation and linear predictive coding of speech signals
topics.content:
