> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT746 - ADVANCED DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Adaptive noise cancellation and linear predictive coding of speech signals

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Adaptive Noise Cancellation (ANC)

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Adaptive Noise Cancellation (ANC)** is a signal processing technique in which an **adaptive filter** dynamically adjusts its coefficients to estimate and subtract an unwanted noise component from a primary signal, using a *secondary reference signal* that is correlated with the noise but uncorrelated with the desired signal.

Mathematically, the adaptive filter $W(z)$ is iteratively updated so that the error signal
$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$$
is minimized in the **mean-square sense**, i.e., $E\{e^2(n)\}$ is minimized, where $d(n)$ is the primary (signal + noise) input and $y(n)$ is the filter's estimate of the contaminating noise.

### Intuitive Analogy
Imagine you are trying to have a phone conversation inside a moving train. The rumble of the wheels is your **noise**, and your friend's voice is the **signal**. A microphone near your ear (the *reference*) hears mostly the train rumble but almost none of your friend's voice. An ANC system is like an *active equalizer* that listens to that rumble and creates an **anti-rumble** (anti-noise) of exactly opposite phase to cancel the wheel sound — but it leaves your friend's voice untouched because it never "knew" about it. The "adaptive" part means the system learns how the train sound changes with speed and adjusts its anti-rumble on the fly.

### Key Metrics and Standard Quantities
- **Sampling Rate ($F_s$):** Typically **8 kHz** (telephony) to **44.1 kHz** (audio).
- **Adaptive Filter Order ($N$):** Commonly **8 to 64** taps depending on noise bandwidth.
- **Step-Size Bound (LMS):** $0 < \mu < \dfrac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}$ where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the maximum eigenvalue of the input autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$.

---

## 1.2 Linear Predictive Coding (LPC) of Speech Signals

### Formal Definition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Linear Predictive Coding (LPC)** is a parametric speech analysis method that models each speech sample $\hat{s}(n)$ as a **linear combination of its $p$ past samples**, with prediction error minimized over short stationary frames (typically **20–30 ms**).

The transfer function of the all-pole vocal-tract model is:
$$H(z) = \frac{G}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$$
where $G$ is the gain and $\{a_k\}$ are the **Linear Prediction Coefficients (LPCs)**.

### Intuitive Analogy
Think of the human vocal tract as a tube whose shape determines what sound comes out. The throat, tongue, and lips change the shape to make different vowels/consonants. LPC is essentially a *mathematical mold* that captures this shape. Given the last few "air-pressure readings" at the mouth, LPC guesses the next one. The prediction **error** is what the lungs must push out as air — a buzz (voiced) or hiss (unvoiced). So LPC neatly separates **what shape the mouth is making** (the filter) from **what kind of air is being pushed** (the excitation).

### Standard Quantities Used in LPC of Speech
- **Typical LPC Order ($p$):** **8 to 16** (commonly $p = 10$ for speech sampled at 8 kHz).
- **Frame Length:** **20–30 ms** (160–240 samples @ 8 kHz).
- **Pitch Range:** **60 Hz – 400 Hz** (voiced speech).
- **Bandwidth:** **300 Hz – 3.4 kHz** (telephony-grade speech).

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Belong Together (KTU Perspective)

Both techniques exploit **linear models with time-varying coefficients**, solved using **mean-square-error minimization**. ANC removes undesired components adaptively; LPC **compresses** a speech signal by representing it via a small set of slowly-varying parameters and a residual excitation — a foundational concept behind every modern speech codec (LPC-10, CELP, MELP, etc.).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Adaptive Noise Cancellation — Operational Block Logic

The ANC system has **two inputs** and **one adaptive filter**. The flow is:

1. **Primary input** $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$ — the desired signal corrupted by noise.
2. **Reference input** $x(n) = n_1(n)$ — a noise signal **correlated** with $n_0(n)$ but **uncorrelated** with $s(n)$.
3. **Adaptive filter $W(z)$** produces $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$ — an estimate of $n_0(n)$.
4. **Subtracter** yields error $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
5. **Adaptive algorithm (LMS / RLS / NLMS)** updates weights:
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

### Why this works (the "How" and "Why")
- Because $x(n)$ is correlated with $n_0(n)$, the filter learns a transfer function that *mimics the noise path* from reference to primary.
- Because $x(n)$ is uncorrelated with $s(n)$, the filter cannot "cancel" the signal — it remains in $e(n)$.
- After convergence, $e(n) \to s(n)$, giving a clean output.

### The LMS Algorithm (Widrow-Hoff, 1960)
The **Least Mean Square (LMS)** algorithm replaces the costly exact gradient by an instantaneous estimate:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

- $\mu$ — step size (controls speed vs stability).
- Computational cost per iteration: **$2N + 1$ multiplications** — extremely cheap.
- Stability condition: $0 < \mu < \dfrac{1}{N \cdot P_x}$ where $P_x$ is the reference signal power.

### Real-world utility of ANC
- **Aviation headsets** (Bose, David Clark) — cancel propeller/engine drone.
- **Hearing aids** — amplify speech, cancel ambient noise.
- **ECG/EEG biomedical monitors** — remove 50/60 Hz mains interference.
- **Automotive cabins** — active road-noise cancellation.

---

## 2.2 Linear Predictive Coding — Operational Block Logic

The LPC analysis pipeline on a frame of speech $\{s(n)\}_{n=0}^{N-1}$:

1. **Pre-emphasis** filter: $s'(n) = s(n) - \alpha s(n-1)$ with $\alpha \approx 0.95$.
2. **Windowing** (Hamming/Hanning) to taper frame edges.
3. **Compute autocorrelation** $R(k) = \sum_{n=0}^{N-1-k} s'(n)\, s'(n+k)$.
4. **Solve Yule-Walker equations** via **Levinson-Durbin** algorithm for predictor coefficients $a_k$.
5. **Compute residual** $e(n) = s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$.
6. **Detect Voiced/Unvoiced** and estimate **pitch** $F_0$.
7. **Quantize** $\{a_k\}, G, F_0$, Voicing flag for transmission/storage.

### The Prediction Error Filter (Inverse Filter)
$$A(z) = 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}$$

It *whitens* the speech frame — output is nearly white noise if model order is sufficient.

### Why LPC is so powerful
- Reduces ~16,000 samples/sec (at 8 kHz) to about **50–100 parameters/frame** (≈ 2.4–4.8 kbps LPC-10).
- **White-noise test:** A correct-order LPC leaves a flat-spectrum residual — the all-pole model captured everything.

### Engineering Applications of LPC
- **LPC-10 / STANAG 4198** military secure voice.
- **CELP, MELP, ACELP, Speex, Opus** — modern descendants.
- **Speech recognition front-ends** (MFCC pipeline begins with LPC-like whitening).
- **Speaker identification / verification.**

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (No Vertical Pipes Inside Cells)

| **Quantity / Symbol** | **Formula / Expression** | **Meaning / Use** | **Units / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| ANC error | $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Filter output residual | Volts (signal units) |
| LMS weight update | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Coefficient adaptation | Vector update |
| LMS stability | $0 < \mu < \dfrac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}$ | Step-size bound | $\lambda_{\max}$ of $\mathbf{R}$ |
| MSE cost | $J(n) = E\{e^2(n)\}$ | Performance criterion | Power units |
| LMS misadjustance | $\mathcal{M} = \dfrac{\mu}{2}\,\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ | Excess MSE / min MSE | Dimensionless |
| LPC predictor | $\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$ | $p$-th order prediction | Sample index |
| Prediction error | $e(n) = s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$ | LPC residual / excitation | Sample units |
| All-pole model | $H(z) = \dfrac{G}{1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k z^{-k}}$ | Vocal-tract filter | Transfer function |
| Autocorrelation | $R(k) = \sum_{n=p}^{N-1} s'(n)\, s'(n-k)$ | LPC Yule-Walker RHS | Energy units |
| Normal Yule-Walker | $\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(\vert k - m \vert) = R(m),\; m = 1,\ldots,p$ | Linear system for LPC | Autocorr. method |
| Levinson-Durbin recursion | $a_m^{(m)} = \dfrac{R(m) - \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_k^{(m-1)} R(m-k)}{E_{m-1}}$ | Order-recursive solver | Stable, $O(p^2)$ |
| Recursion error energy | $E_m = (1 - \{a_m^{(m)}\}^2)\,E_{m-1}$ | Residual energy after order $m$ | Monotone decreasing |
| Gain $G$ | $G^2 = E_p = R(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(k)$ | Excitation RMS gain | Sample units |
| Spectral flatness | $\text{SFM} = \dfrac{\exp\!\left(\tfrac{1}{2\pi}\int \ln S(\omega)d\omega\right)}{\tfrac{1}{2\pi}\int S(\omega)d\omega}$ | Residual whiteness measure | $0 \le \text{SFM} \le 1$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam hint:** Always state the autocorrelation-method Yule-Walker equations **before** invoking Levinson-Durbin in derivations — KTU examiners reward the explicit system $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{r}$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: LMS Update Rule (MMSE Gradient Descent)

### Step 1 — Define the cost function
The mean-square error at instant $n$ is defined as the expected square of the error signal:
$$J(n) = E\{e^2(n)\}$$

### Step 2 — Substitute the linear combiner output
For an FIR adaptive filter of order $N$, the output estimate of the noise is
$$y(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} w_i(n)\, x(n-i) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

The error (primary minus filter output) is
$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$$

### Step 3 — Square the error
$$e^2(n) = d^2(n) - 2d(n)\,\mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n) + \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\,\mathbf{w}(n)$$

### Step 4 — Take the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$
Using the matrix identity $\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} (\mathbf{w}^T \mathbf{A} \mathbf{w}) = 2\mathbf{A}\mathbf{w}$ for symmetric $\mathbf{A}$:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\,J(n) = -2 E\{d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)\} + 2 E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\,\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}\,\mathbf{w}(n)$$

Define the cross-correlation vector $\mathbf{p} = E\{d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\}$ and the autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R} = E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}$:
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\,J(n) = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}(n)$$

### Step 5 — Optimal Wiener solution (set gradient to zero)
$$\mathbf{w}_{\text{opt}} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$$

### Step 6 — Steepest-descent recursion
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}}\,J(n)$$
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\bigl[-2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}(n)\bigr]$$
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,\bigl[\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\,\mathbf{w}(n)\bigr]$$

### Step 7 — Replace exact gradient by instantaneous estimate (LMS)
Since $\hat{\mathbf{R}}(n) = \mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)$ and $\hat{\mathbf{p}}(n) = d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$, we obtain the **LMS rule**:
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the heart of every KTU LMS question. The 7 steps above are exactly what the examiner expects to see in a 7-mark derivation.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: LPC Yule-Walker Equations from Orthogonality Principle

### Step 1 — Define the prediction error energy
For a $p$-th order predictor, the mean-square prediction error over a stationary frame is
$$E_p = E\!\left\{\left[s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)\right]^2\right\}$$

### Step 2 — Set partial derivatives to zero (orthogonality)
For each coefficient $a_m$ ($m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$), optimality requires:
$$\frac{\partial E_p}{\partial a_m} = -2 E\!\left\{\left[s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)\right] s(n-m)\right\} = 0$$

### Step 3 — Apply linearity of expectation
$$E\!\left\{s(n)\,s(n-m)\right\} = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, E\!\left\{s(n-k)\,s(n-m)\right\}$$

### Step 4 — Substitute autocorrelation function
Using $R(\tau) = E\{s(n)\,s(n+\tau)\}$ (WSS assumption):
$$R(m) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, R(m - k), \quad m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

### Step 5 — Write in matrix form (Yule-Walker)
$$\begin{aligned}
\begin{bmatrix}
R(0) & R(1) & R(2) & \cdots & R(p-1) \\
R(1) & R(0) & R(1) & \cdots & R(p-2) \\
R(2) & R(1) & R(0) & \cdots & R(p-3) \\
\vdots & \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\
R(p-1) & R(p-2) & R(p-3) & \cdots & R(0)
\end{bmatrix}
\begin{bmatrix}
a_1 \\ a_2 \\ a_3 \\ \vdots \\ a_p
\end{bmatrix}
=
\begin{bmatrix}
R(1) \\ R(2) \\ R(3) \\ \vdots \\ R(p)
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}$$

This is the **Toeplitz autocorrelation system** $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{a} = \mathbf{r}$.

### Step 6 — Compute the minimum error energy
Substituting the optimum $\mathbf{a}$ back into $E_p$:
$$E_p = R(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, R(k)$$

### Step 7 — Levinson-Durbin Recursion (computational method)
Let $E_0 = R(0)$ and $a^{(0)} = 0$. For $m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$:
$$\begin{aligned}
k_m &= \frac{R(m) - \sum_{i=1}^{m-1} a_i^{(m-1)}\,R(m-i)}{E_{m-1}} \\
a_m^{(m)} &= k_m \\
a_i^{(m)} &= a_i^{(m-1)} - k_m\, a_{m-i}^{(m-1)}, \quad i = 1, \ldots, m-1 \\
E_m &= (1 - k_m^2)\, E_{m-1}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The quantities $k_m$ are called **PARCOR (Partial Correlation) coefficients**. The bound $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ guarantees the all-pole filter is **stable** (poles inside the unit circle).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example (KTU-Style Numerical Problem)

**Problem:** For a 2nd-order LPC analysis on a stationary frame, the autocorrelation values are $R(0) = 10,\; R(1) = 6,\; R(2) = 3$. Find the LPC coefficients and the residual energy.

### Solution

**Step 1:** Set up the 2nd-order Yule-Walker system:
$$\begin{bmatrix} R(0) & R(1) \\ R(1) & R(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R(1) \\ R(2) \end{bmatrix}$$
$$\begin{bmatrix} 10 & 6 \\ 6 & 10 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 6 \\ 3 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2:** Solve using Cramer's rule.
Determinant:
$$\Delta = 10 \cdot 10 - 6 \cdot 6 = 100 - 36 = 64$$
$$a_1 = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 6 & 6 \\ 3 & 10 \end{vmatrix}}{64} = \frac{60 - 18}{64} = \frac{42}{64} = 0.65625$$
$$a_2 = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 10 & 6 \\ 6 & 3 \end{vmatrix}}{64} = \frac{30 - 36}{64} = \frac{-6}{64} = -0.09375$$

**Step 3:** Verify with Levinson-Durbin.
- $E_0 = R(0) = 10$
- $k_1 = \dfrac{R(1)}{E_0} = \dfrac{6}{10} = 0.6$
- $E_1 = (1 - 0.6^2) \cdot 10 = (1 - 0.36) \cdot 10 = 6.4$
- $k_2 = \dfrac{R(2) - a_1^{(1)} R(1)}{E_1} = \dfrac{3 - 0.6 \cdot 6}{6.4} = \dfrac{-0.6}{6.4} = -0.09375$
- $E_2 = (1 - (-0.09375)^2) \cdot 6.4 = (1 - 0.008789) \cdot 6.4 \approx 6.3437$

**Step 4:** Final LPC coefficients and energy.
- $a_1 = 0.6$
- $a_2 = -0.09375$
- $E_2 \approx 6.3437$

> [!TIP]
> Both methods give identical $\{a_k\}$. Always cross-check using Levinson-Durbin — it is the **algorithmic form** examiners expect in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Adaptive Noise Cancellation — System Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Primary Input d of n equals s of n plus n0 of n] --> C[Summing Junction Subtracter]
    B[Reference Input x of n equals noise reference] --> D[Adaptive Filter W of z]
    D --> E[Filter Output y of n]
    E --> C
    C --> F[Error Output e of n equals s hat of n]
    F --> G[Weight Update Algorithm LMS RLS NLMS]
    G --> D
```

**Explanation of nodes:**
- `A`: Primary path carrying the desired signal plus the unwanted noise $n_0$.
- `B`: A second microphone that picks up noise correlated with the primary noise but uncorrelated with the speech.
- `D`: The adaptive filter $W(z)$ whose coefficients are stored in vector $\mathbf{w}(n)$.
- `C`: A simple subtracter performing $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
- `F`: Output — ideally a clean estimate $\hat{s}(n)$ of the desired signal.
- `G`: The LMS/RLS/NLMS algorithm block — uses $e(n)$ and $x(n)$ to update $\mathbf{w}$.

---

## 4.2 LPC Speech Analysis–Synthesis Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Input Speech s of n] --> S2[Pre emphasis High Pass Filter alpha equals 0.95]
    S2 --> S3[Frame Blocking 20 to 30 ms Hamming Window]
    S3 --> S4[Autocorrelation Computation R of k]
    S4 --> S5[Levinson Durbin Solver Yule Walker]
    S5 --> S6[LPC Coefficients a1 to ap]
    S5 --> S7[Residual Energy E_p]
    S5 --> S8[PARCOR Coefficients k1 to kp]
    S6 --> S9[Quantization and Bit Packing]
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S9
    S9 --> S10[Bitstream at 2.4 to 4.8 kbps LPC10]
    S10 --> S11[Decoder All Pole Filter plus Excitation]
    S11 --> S12[Synthesized Speech s hat of n]
```

**Explanation of nodes:**
- `S1`: Raw speech (typically 8 kHz, 16-bit PCM).
- `S2`: Pre-emphasis flattens the spectrum and reduces dynamic range.
- `S3`: Frames of 20–30 ms with overlap; stationary assumption holds.
- `S4`: Autocorrelation $R(k)$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, p$.
- `S5`: Numerical solver — exploits Toeplitz structure in $O(p^2)$.
- `S6`, `S7`, `S8`: Three equivalent parameter sets — all are derivable from each other.
- `S9`: Quantization block (typically log-area ratios for $\{k_m\}$).
- `S10`: Compressed bitstream ready for transmission/storage.
- `S11`: Receiver reconstructs speech by driving $1/A(z)$ with the appropriate excitation.

---

## 4.3 ANC Convergence Behavior — Functional Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N1[Initialize Weights w of zero equals zero vector] --> N2[Sample Inputs d of n and x of n]
    N2 --> N3[Compute Output y of n equals w transpose x]
    N3 --> N4[Compute Error e of n equals d minus y]
    N4 --> N5{Step Size mu Satisfies Stability}
    N5 -- Yes --> N6[Update w of n plus 1 equals w of n plus two mu e x]
    N6 --> N7[Increment n and Loop Back to Sample Inputs]
    N5 -- No --> N8[Divergence Warning Reduce mu]
```

This block diagram captures the inner **iteration loop** of the LMS algorithm and is the standard schematic KTU expects in ANC derivation questions.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**List the essential inputs required by an Adaptive Noise Cancellation system and state the role of each.**

**Model Answer:**
An ANC system requires two inputs:
1. **Primary input $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$** — contains the desired signal $s(n)$ corrupted by an unwanted noise $n_0(n)$.
2. **Reference input $x(n) = n_1(n)$** — a noise signal that is **correlated** with $n_0(n)$ but **uncorrelated** with $s(n)$.

The reference input is typically obtained from a secondary sensor located where the desired signal is weak but the noise is strong. The adaptive filter $W(z)$ uses $x(n)$ to generate an anti-noise $y(n)$ that cancels $n_0(n)$ in the primary input, leaving $e(n) \approx s(n)$. **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define the term "Linear Prediction" as applied to speech signals. What is the significance of the prediction error?**

**Model Answer:**
**Linear Prediction** is a technique in which the current speech sample $s(n)$ is modelled as a linear combination of its past $p$ samples:
$$\hat{s}(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)$$
where $\{a_k\}$ are the **Linear Prediction Coefficients (LPCs)**.

The **prediction error** $e(n) = s(n) - \hat{s}(n)$ represents the part of the speech signal that cannot be predicted from its past — equivalent to the **excitation** of the vocal tract. For voiced speech the residual is quasi-periodic; for unvoiced speech it is noise-like. Minimizing $E\{e^2(n)\}$ yields the LPCs that characterize the vocal-tract filter. **(3 marks)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:**
> - **Do not** skip the orthogonality step in the LPC derivation (Step 2 in Section 3.2). Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if you jump from the cost function directly to Yule-Walker without showing $\partial E_p / \partial a_m = 0$.
> - **Do not** confuse the LMS *misadjustance* with *steady-state error* — these are different quantities and misnaming them costs marks.
> - **Always state** the autocorrelation range when computing $R(k)$ — an unstated summation limit is the single most common marking deduction in LPC numericals.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Adaptive Noise Cancellation Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

#### (a) Derive the LMS adaptive algorithm used in an Adaptive Noise Cancellation system, starting from the mean-square-error criterion. Clearly state the stability condition on the step-size $\mu$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Cost function definition:**
$$J(n) = E\{e^2(n)\} = E\bigl\{[d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)]^2\bigr\}$$

**Step 2 — Gradient computation:**
$$\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) = -2 E\{d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\} + 2 E\{\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\}\mathbf{w}(n) = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n)$$

**Step 3 — Steepest descent recursion:**
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\,\nabla_{\mathbf{w}} J(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu[\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n)]$$

**Step 4 — Replace exact expectations with instantaneous values** (LMS approximation):
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\, e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 5 — Stability condition:**
For convergence in the mean, the eigenvalues of $(\mathbf{I} - 2\mu\mathbf{R})$ must lie inside the unit circle, giving:
$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{\max}} \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad 0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}$$
depending on the convention used. In practice the bound
$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{N \cdot P_x}$$
is widely cited. **(7 marks — 1 mark per step)**

---

#### (b) With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an Adaptive Noise Canceller. Mention two real-world applications. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block diagram:** Refer to the diagram in **Section 4.1** of these notes.

**Working principle:**
- The primary input $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$ enters the system via a summing junction.
- The reference input $x(n) = n_1(n)$ is fed to an adaptive filter $W(z)$.
- The filter output $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ is subtracted from $d(n)$, yielding the error $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
- The LMS algorithm updates the weights so that $E\{e^2(n)\}$ is minimized.
- Because $x(n)$ is correlated with $n_0(n)$ but uncorrelated with $s(n)$, the filter learns to model $n_0(n)$, and $y(n)$ tends to cancel it, leaving $e(n) \approx s(n)$.

**Two real-world applications:**
1. **Aviation headsets** — cancelling low-frequency engine and propeller noise.
2. **Biomedical instrumentation** — cancelling 50/60 Hz power-line interference in ECG and EEG recordings.

(Other valid answers: hearing aids, hands-free telephony, automotive active-noise control.) **(7 marks — 3 for diagram, 2 for working, 2 for applications)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Linear Predictive Coding Focus**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 / KTU Model Paper 2024]**

#### (a) Derive the Yule-Walker equations for Linear Predictive Coding of speech. Explain how the Levinson-Durbin algorithm solves these equations efficiently. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Prediction error energy:**
$$E_p = E\!\left\{\left[s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)\right]^2\right\}$$

**Step 2 — Orthogonality (optimality) condition:**
$$\frac{\partial E_p}{\partial a_m} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad E\!\left\{\left[s(n) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k s(n-k)\right] s(n-m)\right\} = 0$$
for $m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$.

**Step 3 — Substitute autocorrelation function:**
$$R(m) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k\, R(m-k), \quad m = 1, 2, \ldots, p$$

**Step 4 — Yule-Walker matrix form:**
$$\begin{bmatrix} R(0) & R(1) & \cdots & R(p-1) \\ R(1) & R(0) & \cdots & R(p-2) \\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots \\ R(p-1) & R(p-2) & \cdots & R(0) \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \\ \vdots \\ a_p \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} R(1) \\ R(2) \\ \vdots \\ R(p) \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 5 — Why Levinson-Durbin?**
The autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ is **Toeplitz** (constant along diagonals) and **positive definite**. The Levinson-Durbin algorithm exploits this structure to solve the system in **$O(p^2)$** operations instead of $O(p^3)$ for generic Gaussian elimination. It computes the solution **order-recursively**:
$$a_m^{(m)} = \frac{R(m) - \sum_{k=1}^{m-1} a_k^{(m-1)} R(m-k)}{E_{m-1}}$$
with the residual-energy update
$$E_m = (1 - \{a_m^{(m)}\}^2)\, E_{m-1}$$
which guarantees $\vert a_m^{(m)} \vert < 1$ and hence **filter stability**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### (b) Compute the LPC coefficients and the residual energy for a frame whose autocorrelation values are $R(0) = 12,\; R(1) = 8,\; R(2) = 4$. Assume LPC order $p = 2$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Set up the 2nd-order Yule-Walker system:**
$$\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 8 \\ 8 & 12 \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} a_1 \\ a_2 \end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix} 8 \\ 4 \end{bmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Determinant:**
$$\Delta = 12^2 - 8^2 = 144 - 64 = 80$$

**Step 3 — Solve:**
$$a_1 = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 8 & 8 \\ 4 & 12 \end{vmatrix}}{80} = \frac{96 - 32}{80} = \frac{64}{80} = 0.8$$
$$a_2 = \frac{\begin{vmatrix} 12 & 8 \\ 8 & 4 \end{vmatrix}}{80} = \frac{48 - 64}{80} = \frac{-16}{80} = -0.2$$

**Step 4 — Residual energy:**
$$E_2 = R(0) - a_1 R(1) - a_2 R(2) = 12 - 0.8(8) - (-0.2)(4)$$
$$E_2 = 12 - 6.4 + 0.8 = 6.4$$

**Step 5 — Verification with Levinson-Durbin:**
- $E_0 = 12$
- $k_1 = \dfrac{R(1)}{E_0} = \dfrac{8}{12} = \dfrac{2}{3}$
- $E_1 = (1 - 4/9)(12) = (5/9)(12) = 20/3$
- $k_2 = \dfrac{R(2) - a_1^{(1)} R(1)}{E_1} = \dfrac{4 - (2/3)(8)}{20/3} = \dfrac{4 - 16/3}{20/3} = \dfrac{-4/3}{20/3} = -\dfrac{1}{5} = -0.2$
- $E_2 = (1 - 0.04)(20/3) = 0.96 \times 6.667 \approx 6.4$ ✓

**Final Answer:** $a_1 = 0.8,\; a_2 = -0.2,\; E_2 = 6.4$. **(7 marks)**

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **ANC requires two inputs:** primary $d(n) = s(n) + n_0(n)$ and reference $x(n) = n_1(n)$ correlated only with the noise.
- ✅ **LMS update:** $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n) \mathbf{x}(n)$ — derived from instantaneous gradient of $E\{e^2(n)\}$.
- ✅ **LMS stability:** $0 < \mu < \dfrac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}$ of the reference autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$.
- ✅ **LMS cost:** $\approx 2N + 1$ multiplications per sample — making it the cheapest adaptive algorithm.
- ✅ **LPC predictor order $p$:** typically **10** for 8 kHz speech; **8** for narrowband, **16** for wideband.
- ✅ **Yule-Walker equations:** $\sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(\vert m - k \vert) = R(m)$ — foundation of the autocorrelation method.
- ✅ **Levinson-Durbin:** $O(p^2)$ recursive solver exploiting the Toeplitz structure of $\mathbf{R}$.
- ✅ **PARCOR (reflection) coefficients:** $k_m = a_m^{(m)}$ with $\vert k_m \vert < 1$ ⇒ all-pole filter is **stable**.
- ✅ **Residual energy update:** $E_m = (1 - k_m^2) E_{m-1}$ — monotonically decreasing with order.
- ✅ **LPC gain:** $G^2 = E_p = R(0) - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k R(k)$.
- ✅ **LPC applications:** LPC-10 (2.4 kbps), CELP, MELP, ACELP, Opus, speech recognition front-ends, speaker ID.
- ✅ **ANC applications:** aviation headsets, hearing aids, biomedical (ECG/EEG), automotive cabins, echo cancellation.
- ✅ **Frame length for LPC:** **20–30 ms** with Hamming/Hanning window and overlap.
- ✅ **Pre-emphasis:** $s'(n) = s(n) - 0.95 s(n-1)$ flattens the spectrum before LPC analysis.
- ✅ **Voiced speech excitation:** quasi-periodic pulse train at pitch $F_0 \in [60, 400]$ Hz.
- ✅ **Unvoiced speech excitation:** white Gaussian noise.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steepest descent algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT746 - ADVANCED DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Steepest descent algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Steepest Descent Algorithm** is a first-order iterative optimization technique used in adaptive signal processing to recursively compute the optimal tap-weight vector of a linear discrete-time filter by minimizing a quadratic performance surface (typically the Mean Square Error). At every iteration, the weight vector is updated along the *negative gradient* of the cost function $J(\mathbf{w})$, i.e., along the direction of **maximum rate of decrease** of the error surface.

Mathematically, the steepest descent update rule is expressed as:

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu \, \nabla J(\mathbf{w}(n))
$$

where $\mu$ is a small positive scalar called the **step-size (learning rate)** and $\nabla J(\mathbf{w})$ is the gradient of the cost function evaluated at the current weight vector.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PEECT746 – Module 4):**
> The steepest descent algorithm is the *theoretical foundation* upon which the LMS (Least Mean Square) algorithm is built. While LMS uses an instantaneous estimate of the gradient, steepest descent uses the *exact gradient* of the MSE surface. Mastering steepest descent is **mandatory** before tackling LMS and RLS.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – "The Blindfolded Mountain Hiker"

Imagine you are standing on a foggy mountain and you need to reach the valley (the minimum). You cannot see the terrain, but you have a tool that tells you the **slope under your feet** (the gradient). The strategy is simple:

- **Step 1:** Feel the ground (compute gradient).
- **Step 2:** Move in the direction the slope is steepest downward (negative gradient).
- **Step 3:** Repeat until the ground is flat (gradient is zero = optimum).

The size of each step is your **learning rate $\mu$**:
- Too large → you might overshoot the valley and oscillate.
- Too small → you take forever to reach the bottom.

In adaptive filtering, the "mountain" is the **MSE performance surface $J(\mathbf{w})$**, the "slope" is the gradient $\nabla J$, and the "valley" is the **Wiener solution $\mathbf{w}^\circ = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$**.

### 1.3 Physical Constants, Key Symbols & Standard Metrics

| Symbol | Meaning | Engineering Significance |
|---|---|---|
| $\mathbf{w}(n)$ | Tap-weight vector at iteration $n$ | Adaptive filter coefficients |
| $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)]$ | Autocorrelation matrix | Input signal statistics |
| $\mathbf{p} = E[d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)]$ | Cross-correlation vector | Reference–input correlation |
| $\mu$ | Step-size (learning rate) | Controls speed vs. stability |
| $\lambda_{\max}$ | Largest eigenvalue of $\mathbf{R}$ | Upper bound on $\mu$ |
| $\xi_{\min}$ | Minimum MSE | Achievable error floor |
| $\tau$ | Time constant | Convergence speed |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction (KTU Board Focus):**
> Steepest descent is a **deterministic, gradient-based** algorithm using the *true* gradient. The **LMS algorithm**, in contrast, uses a *stochastic, instantaneous* estimate of the gradient. The two are often confused in exams — be precise!

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The MSE Performance Surface

For a transversal FIR adaptive filter with input $\mathbf{x}(n) = [x(n), x(n-1), \ldots, x(n-N+1)]^T$ and desired response $d(n)$, the Mean Square Error is:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = E\left[ \vert e(n) \vert^2 \right] = E\left[ \vert d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n) \vert^2 \right]
$$

Expanding this expectation yields the celebrated **quadratic performance surface**:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}
$$

where:
- $\sigma_d^2 = E[d^2(n)]$ is the variance of the desired signal.
- $\mathbf{R}$ is the $N \times N$ symmetric positive-definite autocorrelation matrix.
- $\mathbf{p}$ is the $N \times 1$ cross-correlation vector.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because $\mathbf{R}$ is **positive definite**, the surface $J(\mathbf{w})$ is a convex hyperparaboloid with a **unique global minimum** — the Wiener solution $\mathbf{w}^\circ = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$.

### 2.2 The Gradient Vector

Taking the gradient of $J(\mathbf{w})$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:

$$
\nabla J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w} - 2\mathbf{p}
$$

Setting $\nabla J(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{0}$ recovers the **Wiener–Hopf equation**: $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}^\circ = \mathbf{p}$.

### 2.3 The Steepest Descent Update Rule

Substituting the gradient into the steepest descent recursion:

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu \left[ 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n) - 2\mathbf{p} \right]
$$

Simplifying by absorbing the factor of 2 into $\mu$ (redefine $\mu' = 2\mu$ for clarity):

$$
\boxed{\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu \left[ \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n) \right]}
$$

### 2.4 Convergence Condition

The algorithm is stable and converges to $\mathbf{w}^\circ$ if and only if:

$$
0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}
$$

Equivalently, defining the **eigenvalue spread** $\chi(\mathbf{R}) = \lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$, the algorithm converges for:

$$
0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}
$$

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Description / Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ | Quadratic MSE surface |
| 2 | $\nabla J(\mathbf{w}) = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w} - 2\mathbf{p}$ | Gradient of MSE |
| 3 | $\mathbf{w}^\circ = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | Wiener optimum solution |
| 4 | $\xi_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ | Minimum MSE (noise floor) |
| 5 | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu[\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n)]$ | Steepest descent update |
| 6 | $\varepsilon(n+1) = (\mathbf{I} - \mu\mathbf{R})\varepsilon(n)$ | Weight-error recursion |
| 7 | $v_k(n) = (1 - \mu\lambda_k)^n v_k(0)$ | Natural mode evolution |
| 8 | $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{\max}$ | Stability / convergence condition |
| 9 | $\tau_k \approx \dfrac{1}{2\mu\lambda_k}$ | Time constant of $k$-th mode |
| 10 | $J(n) = \xi_{\min} + \sum_{k=1}^{N} \lambda_k (1-\mu\lambda_k)^{2n} v_k^2(0)$ | Learning curve (transient MSE) |

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Role of Steepest Descent |
|---|---|
| **Adaptive Beamforming (Radar/5G)** | Optimal weight vector for nulling interference |
| **Channel Equalization** | Compensating ISI in digital communication |
| **Echo Cancellation (VoIP/Submarines)** | Adaptively modelling echo paths |
| **System Identification** | Identifying unknown plant dynamics |
| **Noise Cancellation (Headphones)** | Active Noise Control (ANC) systems |
| **Biomedical Signal Processing** | Adaptive filtering of ECG/EEG artefacts |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Quadratic MSE Surface

Starting from the error signal:

$$
e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)
$$

Squaring:

$$
e^2(n) = d^2(n) - 2d(n)\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n) + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)\mathbf{w}
$$

Taking the expectation operator $E[\cdot]$:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = E[d^2(n)] - 2\mathbf{w}^T E[d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)] + \mathbf{w}^T E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)] \, \mathbf{w}
$$

By definition:
- $E[d^2(n)] = \sigma_d^2$
- $E[d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)] = \mathbf{p}$
- $E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)] = \mathbf{R}$

$$
\therefore \quad J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation of the Gradient

Differentiating $J(\mathbf{w})$ term-by-term with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:

- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} (\sigma_d^2) = 0$
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} (-2\mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{w}) = -2\mathbf{p}$
- $\dfrac{\partial}{\partial \mathbf{w}} (\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}) = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$ (since $\mathbf{R}$ is symmetric)

$$
\nabla J(\mathbf{w}) = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w} - 2\mathbf{p}
$$

Setting $\nabla J(\mathbf{w}) = \mathbf{0}$:

$$
\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}^\circ = \mathbf{p} \quad \Longrightarrow \quad \mathbf{w}^\circ = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation of the Weight-Error Vector Recursion

Define the **weight-error vector**:

$$
\varepsilon(n) \triangleq \mathbf{w}^\circ - \mathbf{w}(n)
$$

Starting from the steepest descent update:

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\left[\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n)\right]
$$

Since $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}^\circ$:

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\left[\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}^\circ - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n)\right] = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\mathbf{R}\left[\mathbf{w}^\circ - \mathbf{w}(n)\right]
$$

Therefore:

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\mathbf{R}\varepsilon(n)
$$

Subtracting both sides from $\mathbf{w}^\circ$:

$$
\mathbf{w}^\circ - \mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}^\circ - \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\mathbf{R}\varepsilon(n)
$$

$$
\varepsilon(n+1) = \varepsilon(n) - \mu\mathbf{R}\varepsilon(n)
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon(n+1) = (\mathbf{I} - \mu\mathbf{R})\varepsilon(n)}
$$

### 3.4 Eigen-Mode (Decoupling) Analysis

Since $\mathbf{R}$ is symmetric positive definite, it admits an **eigendecomposition**:

$$
\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{Q}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{Q}^T
$$

where $\mathbf{Q}$ is an orthogonal matrix of eigenvectors and $\boldsymbol{\Lambda} = \text{diag}(\lambda_1, \lambda_2, \ldots, \lambda_N)$.

Define the **rotated error vector**:

$$
\mathbf{v}(n) = \mathbf{Q}^T \varepsilon(n)
$$

Substituting into the recursion:

$$
\mathbf{v}(n+1) = \mathbf{Q}^T(\mathbf{I} - \mu\mathbf{R})\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{v}(n) = (\mathbf{I} - \mu\boldsymbol{\Lambda})\mathbf{v}(n)
$$

Because $\boldsymbol{\Lambda}$ is diagonal, the modes **decouple**:

$$
v_k(n+1) = (1 - \mu\lambda_k) v_k(n)
$$

By geometric series iteration:

$$
\boxed{v_k(n) = (1 - \mu\lambda_k)^n \, v_k(0)}
$$

### 3.5 Convergence Criterion (Detailed)

For stability, each natural mode must decay to zero:

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} v_k(n) = 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \vert 1 - \mu\lambda_k \vert < 1
$$

This double inequality:

$$
-1 < 1 - \mu\lambda_k < 1
$$

Since $\lambda_k > 0$ (positive definite) and $\mu > 0$, the left inequality is automatically satisfied. The right inequality yields:

$$
\mu\lambda_k < 2 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_k}
$$

This must hold for **every** $k$, so the binding constraint is:

$$
\boxed{0 < \mu < \frac{2}{\lambda_{\max}}}
$$

### 3.6 Time Constant of the $k$-th Mode

The geometric decay factor $(1 - \mu\lambda_k)^n$ can be written as $e^{-n/\tau_k}$, where:

$$
e^{-1/\tau_k} = 1 - \mu\lambda_k
$$

For small $\mu\lambda_k$, using $\ln(1-x) \approx -x$:

$$
\tau_k \approx \frac{1}{\mu\lambda_k}
$$

The **slowest mode** (smallest $\lambda_{\min}$) dominates convergence:

$$
\tau_{\max} = \frac{1}{\mu\lambda_{\min}}
$$

### 3.7 The Learning Curve (Transient MSE)

Starting from $J(\mathbf{w}) = \xi_{\min} + (\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{w}^\circ)^T\mathbf{R}(\mathbf{w} - \mathbf{w}^\circ)$ and using $\varepsilon(n) = \mathbf{Q}\mathbf{v}(n)$:

$$
J(n) = \xi_{\min} + \sum_{k=1}^{N} \lambda_k \, v_k^2(n)
$$

$$
\boxed{J(n) = \xi_{\min} + \sum_{k=1}^{N} \lambda_k (1 - \mu\lambda_k)^{2n} \, v_k^2(0)}
$$

This is a **weighted sum of exponentials** — the famous "learning curve" of steepest descent. It is a geometrically decaying transient superimposed on the noise floor $\xi_{\min}$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Steepest Descent in Adaptive Filtering

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeInput["Input Signal xn"] --> nodeFIR["Adaptive FIR Filter wn"]
    nodeDes["Desired Signal dn"] --> nodeSub["Summing Junction"]
    nodeFIR --> nodeSub
    nodeSub --> nodeErr["Error Signal en"]
    nodeErr --> nodeSq["Square and Expectation"]
    nodeSq --> nodeMSE["MSE Surface J w"]
    nodeMSE --> nodeGrad["Compute Gradient 2R w minus 2p"]
    nodeGrad --> nodeGain["Multiply by step size mu"]
    nodeGain --> nodeUpdate["Weight Update w n+1 equals w n minus mu grad J"]
    nodeUpdate --> nodeFIR
    nodeMSE --> nodeCheck{"Gradient equals zero"}
    nodeCheck -- Yes --> nodeOpt["Optimal Wiener Solution w opt"]
    nodeCheck -- No --> nodeUpdate
```

### 4.2 Convergence Flowchart (eigen-mode decomposition perspective)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Initial Weight w0"] --> nodeB["Compute Error Vector e0 equals w opt minus w0"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Rotate into Modal Coordinates v0 equals Q transpose e0"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Apply Decoupled Recursion v k of n equals 1 minus mu lambda k to the n times v k of 0"]
    nodeD --> nodeE{"All modes within 0 less than mu less than 2 over lambda max"}
    nodeE -- Yes --> nodeF["Exponential Decay to Wiener Solution w opt"]
    nodeE -- No --> nodeG["Divergence or Oscillation"]
```

### 4.3 Performance Surface Contour Map (schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph PES["Quadratic MSE Performance Surface"]
        nodeTop["High MSE Region"]
        nodeRing["Elliptical Contour Rings"]
        nodeMid["Transitional Contour"]
        nodeBot["Optimal Point w opt at MSE equals xi min"]
    end
    nodeTop --> nodeRing
    nodeRing --> nodeMid
    nodeMid --> nodeBot
    nodeRing -.->|"Negative Gradient Direction"| nodeBot
    nodeMid -.->|"Negative Gradient Direction"| nodeBot
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The elliptical contours represent level sets of $J(\mathbf{w})$. The **steepest descent trajectory** moves *perpendicular* to these contours, i.e., along $\nabla J$. The shape of the ellipses is governed by the **eigenvalue spread** $\chi(\mathbf{R})$ — large spread → elongated ellipses → slow convergence.

### 4.4 Block Diagram of the Iterative Update Mechanism

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeWn["Weight Vector w of n"] --> nodeProd["Matrix Product R times w of n"]
    nodeP["Cross Correlation Vector p"] --> nodeDiff["Subtract R w of n from p"]
    nodeProd --> nodeDiff
    nodeDiff --> nodeScale["Scale by step size mu"]
    nodeScale --> nodeAdd["Add to previous weight w of n"]
    nodeWn --> nodeAdd
    nodeAdd --> nodeWnp1["Updated Weight w of n plus 1"]
    nodeWnp1 -. feedback loop .-> nodeWn
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the steepest descent algorithm. State the necessary and sufficient condition for its convergence.**

**Model Answer:**
The steepest descent algorithm is an iterative procedure that updates the tap-weight vector $\mathbf{w}(n)$ of an adaptive filter in the direction opposite to the gradient $\nabla J(\mathbf{w})$ of the mean-square-error performance surface:

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu \nabla J(\mathbf{w}(n))
$$

For a quadratic surface, this becomes $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu[\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n)]$. The convergence condition is $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{\max}$, where $\lambda_{\max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the input autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between steepest descent and the LMS algorithm.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Steepest Descent | LMS |
|---|---|---|
| Gradient | Exact: $\nabla J = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w} - 2\mathbf{p}$ | Instantaneous estimate: $\hat{\nabla}J = -2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ |
| Knowledge of $\mathbf{R}, \mathbf{p}$ | Requires prior knowledge | Not required (self-learning) |
| Noise in weights | Noise-free trajectory | Noisy stochastic trajectory |
| Computation per iteration | High (matrix-vector product) | Low ($2N$ multiplications) |
| Convergence | Deterministic geometric decay | Statistical (mean converges, variance remains) |
| Used as | Theoretical benchmark | Practical adaptive algorithm |

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Choice A — Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**
**(a)** Derive the steepest descent update equation for an adaptive filter that minimizes the mean square error $J(\mathbf{w}) = E[e^2(n)]$, where $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)$. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Starting from the recursion, derive the weight-error vector recursion and hence obtain the convergence condition. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Expansion of MSE:

$$
J(\mathbf{w}) = E\left[ \left( d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n) \right)^2 \right] = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{w} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}
$$

Gradient:

$$
\nabla J(\mathbf{w}) = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}
$$

Update rule:

$$
\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu \nabla J = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu(\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n))
$$

**(b)** Define $\varepsilon(n) = \mathbf{w}^\circ - \mathbf{w}(n)$. Using $\mathbf{p} = \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}^\circ$:

$$
\varepsilon(n+1) = \varepsilon(n) - \mu\mathbf{R}\varepsilon(n) = (\mathbf{I} - \mu\mathbf{R})\varepsilon(n)
$$

Eigendecomposition: $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{Q}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{Q}^T$. Let $\mathbf{v}(n) = \mathbf{Q}^T\varepsilon(n)$, then $v_k(n) = (1 - \mu\lambda_k)^n v_k(0)$.

For convergence: $|1 - \mu\lambda_k| < 1$, yielding $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{\max}$.

---

#### **Choice B — Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2023]**
**(a)** With the help of the rotated error vector in eigen-modal coordinates, derive the expression for the $k$-th natural mode of the steepest descent algorithm. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** For a 2-tap adaptive filter with input autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 1 \\ 1 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$, determine the range of $\mu$ for which the steepest descent algorithm is convergent. Comment on the time constant. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a)** Given $\varepsilon(n+1) = (\mathbf{I} - \mu\mathbf{R})\varepsilon(n)$, define $\mathbf{v}(n) = \mathbf{Q}^T\varepsilon(n)$.

$$
\mathbf{v}(n+1) = \mathbf{Q}^T(\mathbf{I} - \mu\mathbf{R})\mathbf{Q}\mathbf{v}(n) = (\mathbf{I} - \mu\boldsymbol{\Lambda})\mathbf{v}(n)
$$

Component-wise:

$$
v_k(n+1) = (1 - \mu\lambda_k) v_k(n) \quad \Longrightarrow \quad v_k(n) = (1 - \mu\lambda_k)^n v_k(0)
$$

**(b)** Eigenvalues of $\mathbf{R}$: characteristic equation $\lambda^2 - 4\lambda + 3 = 0$, giving $\lambda_1 = 3$, $\lambda_2 = 1$.

- $\lambda_{\max} = 3$, so $0 < \mu < 2/3$.
- Time constants:
  - $\tau_1 \approx 1/(\mu \cdot 3) = 1/(3\mu)$
  - $\tau_2 \approx 1/(\mu \cdot 1) = 1/\mu$
- The slowest mode ($\tau_1$) governs convergence; $\chi(\mathbf{R}) = 3$ indicates moderate elongation.

For example, choosing $\mu = 0.2$: $\tau_1 \approx 1.67$ iterations, $\tau_2 \approx 5$ iterations.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Steepest Descent Problems:**
>
> 1. **Sign error in the update rule:** Students often write $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu[\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n)]$ instead of $+\mu[\ldots]$. Remember: we descend in the direction of *negative* gradient.
>
> 2. **Factor of 2 mishandling:** $\nabla J = 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w} - 2\mathbf{p}$ — the factor 2 is sometimes dropped. If absorbed into $\mu$, redefine $\mu$ explicitly.
>
> 3. **Confusing $\lambda_{\max}$ with $\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$:** The convergence bound is on $\lambda_{\max}$, NOT on $\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ or $\sigma_x^2$. Using $1/\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ gives a wrong (overly conservative) bound.
>
> 4. **Misapplication of LMS vs. Steepest Descent formulae:** Don't plug in instantaneous estimates into the steepest descent formula — that becomes the LMS algorithm.
>
> 5. **Forgetting to mention positive-definiteness:** The convergence analysis assumes $\mathbf{R}$ is positive definite. Always state this assumption.
>
> 6. **Time constant formula:** Use $\tau_k = -1/\ln(1 - \mu\lambda_k)$ for the exact value; the approximation $\tau_k \approx 1/(\mu\lambda_k)$ is valid only for small $\mu\lambda_k$.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core update rule:** $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu[\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n)]$
- **Optimum weight:** $\mathbf{w}^\circ = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ (Wiener solution)
- **Minimum MSE:** $\xi_{\min} = \sigma_d^2 - \mathbf{p}^T\mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$
- **Convergence condition:** $0 < \mu < 2/\lambda_{\max}$
- **Error recursion:** $\varepsilon(n+1) = (\mathbf{I} - \mu\mathbf{R})\varepsilon(n)$
- **Modal decoupling:** $v_k(n) = (1 - \mu\lambda_k)^n v_k(0)$
- **Time constant:** $\tau_k \approx 1/(\mu\lambda_k)$; slowest mode $\tau_{\max} = 1/(\mu\lambda_{\min})$
- **Learning curve:** $J(n) = \xi_{\min} + \sum_k \lambda_k(1 - \mu\lambda_k)^{2n} v_k^2(0)$ — a sum of decaying exponentials.
- **Eigenvalue spread** $\chi(\mathbf{R}) = \lambda_{\max}/\lambda_{\min}$ directly controls convergence speed; large $\chi$ → slow convergence.
- **Steepest Descent vs LMS:** Steepest descent uses *exact* gradient (deterministic); LMS uses *instantaneous* gradient (stochastic, noisy).
- **Stability is guaranteed** iff all eigenvalues of $(\mathbf{I} - \mu\mathbf{R})$ have magnitudes less than 1.
- **Newton's method** generalizes steepest descent by pre-multiplying with $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$, giving a single-step convergence — but at the cost of computing $\mathbf{R}^{-1}$ every iteration.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LMS algorithm
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEECT746 - ADVANCED DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** LMS algorithm

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **LMS (Least Mean Squares) Algorithm** is a member of the family of stochastic gradient-based adaptive filtering algorithms that iteratively updates the coefficients (weights) of an adaptive linear filter using the instantaneous estimate of the error gradient, making it computationally efficient and suitable for real-time adaptive signal processing applications.

The LMS algorithm, introduced by **Bernard Widrow** and **Marcian Hoff** in **1960**, replaces the expensive gradient computation of the steepest-descent method with a cheap instantaneous estimate. It is the workhorse of adaptive signal processing and forms the foundation for countless modern applications ranging from noise cancellation in headphones to channel equalization in 5G modems.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The LMS algorithm is a **mandatory, high-weightage topic** in Module 4 of PEECT746. Students must master the update equation, convergence analysis, stability bounds, and the block-diagram signal flow of an adaptive transversal filter.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain English Analogy — "The Blindfolded Archer"** 🎯
>
> Imagine you are blindfolded, standing at an unknown distance from a target. You can only hear where the arrow lands (the **error signal**) and feel the wind direction (the **input signal**). You do **not** know how far the target is, but every arrow you fire teaches you something. After each shot, you nudge your aim **a little bit** in the direction that reduces the sound of the miss.
>
> - The **archer** = adaptive filter
> - The **arrows** = filter output samples
> - The **sound of the miss** = error signal e(n)
> - **Nudge size** = step-size parameter **μ**
> - **Wind direction cue** = input signal x(n)
>
> After many iterations (shots), the archer hits the bullseye without ever solving a complex equation. This is precisely how LMS works — it learns the optimal weights one sample at a time, using only local, instantaneous information.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following physical/mathematical constants are essential to LMS analysis:

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Value |
|--------|--------|------------------------|
| Step-size (learning rate) | **μ** | $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$ |
| Filter order (length) | **N** | 2 – 1024 (practical) |
| Misadjustment | **M** | $M = \frac{\mu}{2} \cdot \text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ |
| Steady-state excess MSE | $J_{ex}(\infty)$ | $\mu \cdot J_{min} \cdot \text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ |
| Convergence time constant (mode k) | $\tau_k$ | $\frac{1}{2\mu\lambda_k}$ |

Where:
- $\lambda_{max}$ = **largest eigenvalue** of the input autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$
- $\mathbf{R}$ = **autocorrelation matrix** of the input signal
- $J_{min}$ = **minimum mean-square error** (Wiener solution residual)
- $\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ = **trace** of $\mathbf{R}$ (sum of eigenvalues)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Architecture

The LMS algorithm operates on a closed-loop adaptive architecture comprising four essential functional blocks:

1. **Adaptive Linear Combiner (FIR Filter)** — produces an output $y(n)$ that is a weighted sum of the input vector $\mathbf{x}(n)$.
2. **Error Computation Block** — computes the error $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$, where $d(n)$ is the **desired response**.
3. **Weight-Update Mechanism** — adjusts the tap weights $\mathbf{w}(n)$ proportional to the error and input.
4. **Feedback Path** — feeds $e(n)$ and $\mathbf{x}(n)$ back to the weight-adaptation logic.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why LMS?** — The optimum Wiener filter requires prior knowledge of the autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$ and cross-correlation vector $\mathbf{p}$. In real-world scenarios, these statistics are **unknown and time-varying**. LMS circumvents this by **estimating the gradient on-the-fly** using instantaneous squared error $e^2(n)$.

### 2.2 The Underlying Logic — Step-by-Step

**Step 1 — Filter Output Generation:**
The adaptive FIR filter produces the output as the inner product of the input vector and the weight vector.

$$y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n) = \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} w_k(n)\,x(n-k)$$

**Step 2 — Error Signal Formation:**
The instantaneous error is the difference between the desired signal and the actual filter output.

$$e(n) = d(n) - y(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 3 — Instantaneous Cost Function:**
Unlike Wiener filtering that minimizes $J = E[e^2(n)]$, LMS minimizes the **instantaneous squared error**:

$$\hat{J}(n) = e^2(n)$$

This is the key simplification — replacing expectation with the instantaneous sample.

**Step 4 — Gradient Estimation (Stochastic Approximation):**
The true gradient of $J$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}$ is $\nabla J = -2\,\mathbf{p} + 2\,\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$.
LMS estimates this as:

$$\hat{\nabla} J(n) = \frac{\partial e^2(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = -2\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 5 — Weight Update Equation (The Heart of LMS):**
Applying steepest descent with the estimated gradient:

$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

Where $\mu$ is the **step-size parameter (learning rate)** controlling convergence speed vs. steady-state error trade-off.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — Memorize This Entire Block**

| **S.No** | **Concept** | **Equation** | **Remarks** |
|:--------:|-------------|-------------|-------------|
| 1 | Filter Output | $y(n) = \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Inner product form |
| 2 | Error Signal | $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$ | Sign convention: desired − actual |
| 3 | Instantaneous Cost | $\hat{J}(n) = e^2(n)$ | Replaces $E[e^2(n)]$ |
| 4 | Gradient Estimate | $\hat{\nabla}J(n) = -2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ | Stochastic approximation |
| 5 | **LMS Weight Update** | $\boxed{\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}$ | **Most critical formula** |
| 6 | Stability Bound | $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$ | Necessary and sufficient |
| 7 | Mean Weight Vector | $E[\mathbf{w}(n)] \to \mathbf{w}_{opt}$ | Converges to Wiener solution |
| 8 | Time Constant (mode k) | $\tau_k = \frac{1}{2\mu\lambda_k}$ | Convergence speed per mode |
| 9 | Misadjustment | $M = \frac{\mu}{2}\,\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ | Steady-state performance loss |
| 10 | Excess MSE | $J_{ex}(\infty) = \mu J_{min}\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ | Asymptotic excess error |
| 11 | Computational Complexity | $O(N)$ per sample | N multiplications + N additions |
| 12 | Convergence Condition | $\sum_k \tfrac{1}{2\mu\lambda_k} < \infty$ | All eigenvalues must satisfy stability |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The LMS algorithm is the silent engine behind a vast array of engineering applications:

- **Active Noise Cancellation (ANC)** — Bose, Sony, and Apple headphones use LMS-derived algorithms to generate anti-noise in real time.
- **Adaptive Echo Cancellation** — Used in hands-free telephony and teleconferencing systems to eliminate acoustic feedback.
- **Channel Equalization** — Mitigating inter-symbol interference (ISI) in digital modems and wireless channels.
- **System Identification** — Modeling unknown plant dynamics in control systems.
- **Beamforming** — Adaptive antenna arrays in radar and 5G base stations.
- **Biomedical Signal Processing** — Canceling maternal ECG from fetal ECG recordings, removing powerline interference (50/60 Hz) from EEG signals.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Steepest Descent to LMS Weight Update

We begin with the **steepest descent** framework applied to the mean-square-error cost function.

**Given:** The mean-square-error cost function:
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E[\vert e(n) \vert^2] = E[\vert d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n) \vert^2]$$

**Step 1 — Expand the Cost Function:**
Substituting $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n)$:

$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E[d^2(n)] - 2\mathbf{w}^T E[d(n)\mathbf{x}(n)] + \mathbf{w}^T E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)]\,\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 2 — Define Statistical Quantities:**
We define the autocorrelation matrix and the cross-correlation vector:
$$\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}^T(n)], \quad \mathbf{p} = E[d(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)]$$

Therefore, the cost function simplifies to the famous **quadratic form**:
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the True Gradient:**
Taking the gradient with respect to $\mathbf{w}$:
$$\nabla J(\mathbf{w}) = \frac{\partial J}{\partial \mathbf{w}} = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 4 — Set Gradient to Zero (Wiener-Hopf Equation):**
For the optimal weight vector $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$:
$$\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{p} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Steepest Descent Update:**
In iterative steepest descent, the weights are updated opposite to the gradient:
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\,\nabla J(\mathbf{w}(n))$$

Substituting the true gradient:
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\,(-2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n))$$
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,(\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n))$$

**Step 6 — The Practical Problem:** Computing $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{p}$ requires statistical knowledge that is generally unavailable in real-time. **This is the bottleneck that LMS solves.**

**Step 7 — LMS Trick: Instantaneous Gradient Approximation:**
Replace expectations with instantaneous samples. Since $e^2(n)$ is an unbiased (but noisy) estimator of $J$:

$$\hat{J}(n) = e^2(n)$$

Differentiate with respect to $\mathbf{w}(n)$ using the chain rule:

$$\hat{\nabla}J(n) = \frac{\partial e^2(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = 2e(n)\,\frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)}$$

Since $e(n) = d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$:

$$\frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = -\mathbf{x}(n)$$

Therefore:
$$\hat{\nabla}J(n) = 2e(n)\,(-\mathbf{x}(n)) = -2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 8 — Substitute into Steepest Descent Equation:**
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mu\,(-2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n))$$
$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}$$

This is the **canonical LMS weight update equation** — derived rigorously from first principles.

---

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: LMS Variants and Their Properties

> [!NOTE]
> The following matrix provides a comparative analytical view of LMS and its close relatives — a frequently tested area in KTU exams.

| **Parameter** | **Standard LMS** | **Normalized LMS (NLMS)** | **Sign-Error LMS** | **Sign-Sign LMS** |
|---------------|------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|---------------------|
| Update Equation | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \frac{\mu}{\epsilon + \vert\vert\mathbf{x}(n)\vert\vert^2} e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\,\text{sgn}[e(n)]\,\mathbf{x}(n)$ | $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + \mu\,\text{sgn}[e(n)]\,\text{sgn}[\mathbf{x}(n)]$ |
| Step-Size Range | $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$ | $0 < \mu < 2$ | $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_{max}\sigma_x^2}}$ | $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\sqrt{\lambda_{max}}}$ |
| Computational Cost (per sample) | $2N+1$ mults | $3N+1$ mults | $N$ mults + $N+1$ signs | $2N$ signs only |
| Gradient Noise | High | Moderate | Moderate | Highest |
| Convergence Speed | Slow for colored inputs | Faster, input-independent | Moderate | Slowest |
| Stability Robustness | Sensitive to eigenvalue spread | Excellent | Moderate | Moderate |
| Hardware Simplicity | Moderate | Moderate | High | **Highest** |

### 3.3 Stability Bound Derivation (The μ Criterion)

**Starting Point:** Take the expectation of both sides of the LMS update equation:
$$E[\mathbf{w}(n+1)] = E[\mathbf{w}(n)] + 2\mu\,E[e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)]$$

**Substitute:** $e(n)\mathbf{x}(n) = d(n)\mathbf{x}(n) - \mathbf{w}^T(n)\mathbf{x}(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$

Using the **independence assumption** (weights and input are statistically independent — a standard LMS approximation):

$$E[e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)] = \mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\,E[\mathbf{w}(n)]$$

Define the weight-error vector: $\boldsymbol{\epsilon}(n) = \mathbf{w}(n) - \mathbf{w}_{opt}$

Subtracting $\mathbf{w}_{opt}$ from both sides and using $\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}_{opt} = \mathbf{p}$:

$$E[\boldsymbol{\epsilon}(n+1)] = (\mathbf{I} - 2\mu\mathbf{R})\,E[\boldsymbol{\epsilon}(n)]$$

**Diagonalize** $\mathbf{R}$ using its eigen-decomposition: $\mathbf{R} = \mathbf{Q}\boldsymbol{\Lambda}\mathbf{Q}^T$

In the transformed coordinate system $v_k(n) = \mathbf{q}_k^T \boldsymbol{\epsilon}(n)$:

$$E[v_k(n+1)] = (1 - 2\mu\lambda_k)\,E[v_k(n)]$$

**Convergence requires** $\vert 1 - 2\mu\lambda_k \vert < 1$ for every eigenvalue $\lambda_k$:

$$-1 < 1 - 2\mu\lambda_k < 1$$
$$0 < 2\mu\lambda_k < 2$$
$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_k}$$

Since this must hold for **all** $k$, the tightest bound comes from $\lambda_{max}$:

$$\boxed{0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}}$$

This is the **necessary and sufficient stability condition** for LMS convergence in the mean.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 LMS Adaptive Filter — Block-Level Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the complete signal-flow architecture of a generic LMS adaptive filter, illustrating the **forward path** (input → output) and the **feedback path** (error → weight update).

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcX[Input Signal x of n] --> delayA[Tap 1 z^-1]
    delayA --> delayB[Tap 2 z^-1]
    delayB --> delayC[Tap N minus 1 z^-1]
    delayC --> xVec[Input Vector x of n]

    srcD[Desired Signal d of n] --> errCalc[Error Computation Junction]

    xVec --> mac[Multiplier Accumulator MAC]
    weights[Weight Vector w of n] --> mac
    mac --> yOut[Filter Output y of n]

    yOut --> errCalc
    errCalc --> eSig[Error Signal e of n]

    eSig --> gainMu[Step Size Multiplier 2 mu]
    gainMu --> corrBlock[Correlation e of n times x of n]

    xVec --> corrBlock
    corrBlock --> deltaW[Weight Increment delta w]

    deltaW --> wUpdate[Weight Update w of n plus 1]
    weights --> wUpdate
    wUpdate --> weights

    eSig --> costEst[Instantaneous Cost e squared of n]
```

### 4.2 Operational State Machine — LMS Iteration Lifecycle

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Initialize
    Initialize: Initialize w of 0 to zero vector
    Initialize --> Acquire: Sample x of n and d of n
    Acquire: Read input sample and desired response
    Acquire --> ComputeOutput
    ComputeOutput: Compute y of n equals w transpose x
    ComputeOutput --> ComputeError
    ComputeError: Compute e of n equals d minus y
    ComputeError --> UpdateWeights
    UpdateWeights: w of n plus 1 equals w plus 2 mu e x
    UpdateWeights --> CheckConvergence
    CheckConvergence: Has e squared fallen below threshold
    CheckConvergence --> Acquire: No continue iteration
    CheckConvergence --> [*]: Yes terminate
```

### 4.3 LMS Convergence Behavior — Conceptual Performance Curve

```mermaid
graph TD
    start[Start LMS Iteration n equals 0] --> trans[Transient Phase Large e squared]
    trans --> adapt[Adaptive Phase Gradient descent in action]
    adapt --> steady[Steady State Small fluctuations around Jmin]
    steady --> run[Continuous Tracking of non stationary statistics]

    trans -.-> note1[Duration approximately 1 over 2 mu lambda max samples]
    adapt -.-> note2[Excess MSE equals mu times Jmin times trace R]
    steady -.-> note3[Misadjustment equals mu over 2 times trace R]
```

### 4.4 Functional Component Interaction Map

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph ForwardPath[Forward Signal Path]
        A1[Input x of n] --> A2[FIR Filter Taps]
        A2 --> A3[Output y of n]
    end

    subgraph FeedbackPath[Error Feedback Path]
        B1[Desired d of n] --> B2[Subtract y of n]
        A3 --> B2
        B2 --> B3[Error e of n]
        B3 --> B4[Square e squared]
        B4 --> B5[Gradient Estimate]
    end

    subgraph AdaptationEngine[Weight Adaptation Engine]
        B3 --> C1[Multiply with x of n]
        C1 --> C2[Scale by 2 mu]
        C2 --> C3[Add to w of n]
        C3 --> C4[New w of n plus 1]
        C4 --> A2
    end
```

### 4.5 Decision Flowchart — Choosing μ

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Is the input signal stationary} -->|Yes| Q2{Is eigenvalue spread large}
    Q1 -->|No| A1[Use small mu for stability and tracking]
    Q2 -->|Yes| A2[Use Normalized LMS NLMS]
    Q2 -->|No| A3[Use moderate mu close to 1 over lambda max]
    A2 --> A4[Set mu between 0 and 2]
    A3 --> A5[Verify convergence empirically]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

**Q1. State the LMS weight update equation. Why is it preferred over the standard steepest-descent method in adaptive filtering?**

**Model Answer:**
The LMS weight update equation is:
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Reasons for preference over steepest descent:**
1. **No prior statistical knowledge required** — steepest descent requires $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{p}$; LMS estimates them instantaneously.
2. **Computational simplicity** — only $2N+1$ multiplications per sample, with no matrix inversion.
3. **Real-time implementability** — operates sample-by-sample, suitable for streaming applications.
4. **Robust tracking** — adapts continuously to time-varying statistics (e.g., mobile channels).
5. **Numerical stability** — does not require matrix inversions, avoiding divergence in ill-conditioned systems.

---

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**Q2. Define the stability bound on the LMS step-size parameter $\mu$. What happens if $\mu$ is chosen too small or too large?**

**Model Answer:**
The stability condition for the LMS algorithm is:
$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$$

where $\lambda_{max}$ is the largest eigenvalue of the input autocorrelation matrix $\mathbf{R}$.

| Condition | Effect on Convergence |
|-----------|----------------------|
| $\mu$ too small | Very slow convergence, excellent steady-state accuracy |
| $\mu$ at optimum | Balanced convergence speed and steady-state error |
| $\mu$ too large (but stable) | Fast convergence, higher misadjustment (oscillations near optimum) |
| $\mu \geq \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$ | **Algorithm diverges** — weights grow unbounded, output error explodes |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A — Option 1 [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the LMS weight update equation starting from the mean-square-error cost function. Clearly state all assumptions used. (7 marks)

**(b)** For a second-order LMS adaptive filter with input autocorrelation matrix:
$$\mathbf{R} = \begin{bmatrix} 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 0.5 \end{bmatrix}$$

Determine the range of step-size $\mu$ for stability. If $\mu = 0.2$, compute the convergence time constants for each eigenmode. Comment on the convergence behavior. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Step 1 — Define the MSE cost function:**
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = E[e^2(n)] = E[(d(n) - \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{x}(n))^2]$$

Expanding using $\mathbf{R} = E[\mathbf{x}\mathbf{x}^T]$ and $\mathbf{p} = E[d\mathbf{x}]$:
$$J(\mathbf{w}) = \sigma_d^2 - 2\mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{p} + \mathbf{w}^T\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 2 — True gradient:**
$$\nabla J(\mathbf{w}) = -2\mathbf{p} + 2\mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}$$

**Step 3 — Steepest descent update (requires $\mathbf{R}$, $\mathbf{p}$):**
$$\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu(\mathbf{p} - \mathbf{R}\mathbf{w}(n))$$

**Step 4 — LMS Instantaneous Approximation:**
Replace $J$ with $\hat{J}(n) = e^2(n)$:
$$\hat{\nabla}J(n) = 2e(n)\frac{\partial e(n)}{\partial \mathbf{w}(n)} = -2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$$

**Step 5 — Final LMS equation:**
$$\boxed{\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu\,e(n)\,\mathbf{x}(n)}$$

**Assumptions used:**
- Independence assumption: $\mathbf{w}(n)$ is statistically independent of $\mathbf{x}(n)$.
- $\mathbf{R}$ and $\mathbf{p}$ are stationary (or slowly varying).
- The instantaneous squared error is an unbiased estimator of MSE.

##### Model Solution for Part (b):

**Step 1 — Identify eigenvalues:** Since $\mathbf{R}$ is diagonal:
$$\lambda_1 = 2, \quad \lambda_2 = 0.5, \quad \lambda_{max} = 2$$

**Step 2 — Stability range:**
$$0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}} = \frac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

Therefore $\mu = 0.2$ is **within the stable range**.

**Step 3 — Time constants:**
$$\tau_1 = \frac{1}{2\mu\lambda_1} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.2 \times 2} = \frac{1}{0.8} = 1.25 \text{ samples}$$
$$\tau_2 = \frac{1}{2\mu\lambda_2} = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.2 \times 0.5} = \frac{1}{0.2} = 5.00 \text{ samples}$$

**Step 4 — Convergence analysis:**
The mode corresponding to the **larger eigenvalue** ($\lambda_1 = 2$) converges **4× faster** than the mode corresponding to the smaller eigenvalue. The **slower mode** ($\tau_2 = 5$ samples) dominates the overall convergence time — approximately **5 samples for the algorithm to settle** to within $\approx 63\%$ of the optimal solution, and about **15–20 samples** for practical settling within 95%.

**Misadjustment calculation:**
$$\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}) = \lambda_1 + \lambda_2 = 2.5$$
$$M = \frac{\mu}{2}\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}) = \frac{0.2}{2}(2.5) = 0.25 \quad \text{or} \quad 25\%$$

This indicates a moderate steady-state error penalty.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the operation of an adaptive transversal filter using the LMS algorithm. Define the **misadjustment** and derive its expression. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare LMS and RLS (Recursive Least Squares) algorithms in terms of computational complexity, convergence speed, numerical stability, and tracking ability. State one application where each is preferred. (7 marks)

##### Model Solution for Part (a):

**Block Diagram (drawn as a schematic by the student):**

```
                x(n) ──►[x(n)]──►[x(n-1)]──►...──►[x(n-N+1)]
                          │            │              │
                          ▼            ▼              ▼
                        w₀(n)        w₁(n)         w_{N-1}(n)
                          │            │              │
                          └──► Σ ──► y(n) ──┐
                                          │
                          d(n) ──────────(+)─► e(n)
                                          │
                                          ▼
                            ┌──────────────────────────────┐
                            │ w(n+1) = w(n) + 2μe(n)x(n)   │
                            └──────────────────────────────┘
                                          │
                                          └─► updates w₀, w₁, ..., w_{N-1}
```

**Operation:**
1. Input samples are shifted through a tapped delay line.
2. Each tap is multiplied by its corresponding weight.
3. The sum forms the filter output $y(n)$.
4. The error is computed: $e(n) = d(n) - y(n)$.
5. Weights are updated using the LMS rule and fed back to the taps.

**Misadjustment Definition:**
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Misadjustment (M)** is defined as the ratio of the steady-state excess mean-square error to the minimum mean-square error, expressed as a dimensionless quantity.
> $$M = \frac{J_{ex}(\infty)}{J_{min}}$$

**Derivation:**
Starting from the LMS update, the steady-state excess MSE is:
$$J_{ex}(\infty) = \mu\,J_{min}\,\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$$

Therefore:
$$\boxed{M = \frac{J_{ex}(\infty)}{J_{min}} = \mu\,\text{tr}(\mathbf{R}) = \mu \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \lambda_k = \mu \sum_{k=0}^{N-1} \sigma_{x_k}^2}$$

For white noise input with variance $\sigma_x^2$, this simplifies to:
$$M = \mu N \sigma_x^2$$

##### Model Solution for Part (b):

| **Criterion** | **LMS** | **RLS** |
|---------------|---------|---------|
| **Computational Complexity** | $O(N)$ per sample — very low | $O(N^2)$ per sample — high |
| **Convergence Speed** | Slow, depends on eigenvalue spread | Fast, independent of eigenvalue spread |
| **Numerical Stability** | Excellent (no matrix inversion) | Moderate (requires $\mathbf{P}(n)$ matrix updates; sensitive to round-off) |
| **Tracking Ability** | Good for slowly varying channels | Superior for fast-varying channels |
| **Memory Storage** | Stores $N$ weights only | Stores $N \times N$ matrix $\mathbf{P}(n)$ |
| **Suitability for Hardware** | Highly suitable (low-power DSP) | Resource-intensive |
| **Preferred Application** | Hearing aids, simple echo cancellers, ANC headphones | MIMO radar beamforming, fast-fading wireless channel equalization |

**Application-specific recommendation:**
- **LMS** is preferred for **battery-powered consumer audio devices** (e.g., noise-cancelling earbuds) due to its ultra-low computational footprint.
- **RLS** is preferred for **broadband mobile communications** where rapid convergence in dispersive channels is critical.

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in LMS Questions — Read Carefully!**
>
> 1. **Sign error in the weight update equation:** Many students write $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) - 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ — this is **WRONG** and will cost you the entire 7-mark derivation. Always verify by differentiating $e^2(n)$ with respect to $\mathbf{w}(n)$.
>
> 2. **Confusing stability bound with upper-only bound:** The bound is **two-sided** (technically) — it requires $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$, with **strict inequalities**. Writing $\mu \leq \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$ is a common error.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the factor of 2:** The coefficient is $\mathbf{2}\mu$, not just $\mu$. This factor arises from the derivative of $e^2(n)$. Marks are specifically allocated for this.
>
> 4. **Misidentifying $\lambda_{max}$:** Students often use the trace or the determinant instead of the **largest eigenvalue**. For a diagonal matrix, this is the largest diagonal element; for a general matrix, you must perform eigen-decomposition.
>
> 5. **Not stating the independence assumption:** The LMS convergence analysis critically depends on the **independence assumption** $\mathbf{w}(n) \perp \mathbf{x}(n)$. Omitting this in derivations will cost at least 2 marks.
>
> 6. **Mixing up LMS with NLMS bounds:** The bound $0 < \mu < 2$ applies to **NLMS**, not LMS. Writing this for an LMS question is a textbook (pun intended) error.
>
> 7. **Forgetting to mention initialization:** LMS requires an initial weight vector $\mathbf{w}(0)$. The most common choice is $\mathbf{w}(0) = \mathbf{0}$, but this must be stated explicitly.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Rapid-Revision Checklist — LMS Algorithm**

- [x] **LMS = Least Mean Squares** — stochastic gradient descent on instantaneous squared error.
- [x] **Canonical update:** $\mathbf{w}(n+1) = \mathbf{w}(n) + 2\mu e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$ — memorize exactly.
- [x] **Cost function:** $\hat{J}(n) = e^2(n)$ — instantaneous (not expected).
- [x] **Gradient estimate:** $\hat{\nabla}J(n) = -2e(n)\mathbf{x}(n)$.
- [x] **Stability bound:** $0 < \mu < \frac{1}{\lambda_{max}}$ — strictly required.
- [x] **Convergence in the mean:** $E[\mathbf{w}(n)] \to \mathbf{R}^{-1}\mathbf{p}$ (Wiener solution).
- [x] **Time constant:** $\tau_k = \frac{1}{2\mu\lambda_k}$ — slower modes dominate convergence.
- [x] **Misadjustment:** $M = \frac{\mu}{2}\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ — fundamental speed-vs-accuracy trade-off.
- [x] **Complexity:** $O(N)$ — extremely efficient, ideal for embedded DSP.
- [x] **LMS variants:** NLMS (input-normalized), Sign-Error, Sign-Sign, VSS-LMS (variable step-size).
- [x] **Independence assumption:** $\mathbf{w}(n)$ independent of $\mathbf{x}(n)$ — essential for analysis.
- [x] **Applications:** ANC, echo cancellation, channel equalization, system ID, beamforming, biomedical.
- [x] **Trade-off:** Larger $\mu$ → faster convergence but higher misadjustment; smaller $\mu$ → slower but more accurate.
- [x] **Comparison with RLS:** LMS = simple/slow, RLS = complex/fast.
- [x] **Initialization:** Typically $\mathbf{w}(0) = \mathbf{0}$.
- [x] **Adaptive filter structure:** FIR (transversal) with feedback loop — strictly causal structure.
- [x] **Excess MSE:** $J_{ex}(\infty) = \mu J_{min}\text{tr}(\mathbf{R})$ — vanishes as $\mu \to 0$.
